

### THE BOOK OF EXODUS {.heading_sH}

# Preface {.heading_s2W-0}

Readers of the book of Genesis, in this series of Bible-believing commentaries, will surely agree that ["in a coffin in Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} is no place to drop the narrative of Biblical history. (As a child once said to another child who was extremely concerned about Joseph being in prison \[Gen. 40:23\]: "Don't you worry; next week's Sunday School lesson will come out all right, 'cause all of God's stories have happy endings!")

Genesis is the book of [generations ]{.class_s4N4-0}or [beginnings]{.class_s4N4-0}; Exodus is the book of [redemption]{.class_s4N4-0}. Hence, the book of redemption is a supplement to Genesis, for Genesis was the book that told us of our [lost estate ]{.class_s4N4-0}and our physical and spiritual ruin. The fact that Darwin and Co. (Haeckel, Einstein, Huxley, Freud, Menninger, Marx, the United Nations, and the National Education Association) reject this revelation means nothing from the Divine viewpoint (Rom. 3:4). It may deeply and tragically affect the lives of millions of people who are stupid enough to follow such leadership, but it will certainly have no bearing on the progress or outcome of history (see [The Sure Word of Prophecy, ]{.class_s4N4-0}1969, 1972).

The title [Exodus ]{.class_s4N4-0}is plainly a Greek word, as the Hebrews called the book ["Ve-eleh Shemoth"]{.class_s4N4-0} (["And These Are the Names"]{.class_s4N4-0}). However, the reader will observe that the Masoretic text of A.D. 1917 calls the book [Exodus]{.class_s4N4-0} and only prints the Hebrew radicals for "names" beside it. Credit for the name [Exodus]{.class_s4NC}[ ]{.class_s4N4-0}is naturally given to the mythological [LXX]{.class_s4N4-0}, supposedly written around 250 B.C. (see [The Christian\'s Handbook of Manuscript Evidence]{.class_s4N4-0}, Peter Ruckman, 1970 \[hereafter cited as [Manuscript Evidence]{.class_s4N4-0}\]; see also [The Mythological Septuagint, ]{.class_s4N4-0}1996.). []{#c2P.xhtml_page_5}Unfortunately for the fans of the [LXX]{.class_s4N4-0}, no one has a copy of this writing (or "Bible"), and there is no evidence that it existed before Philo (A.D. 20--50) and Origen (A.D. 185--254), who both devoted their lives to allegorizing and spiritualizing the Old Testament in line with Plato and Greek Gnosticism. The term [Exodus ]{.class_s4N4-0}was used, however, by Christians in the Second and Third Centuries [before]{.class_s4N4-0} [Sinaiticus ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [Vaticanus ]{.class_s4N4-0}were written. (These manuscripts---plus [Alexandrinus]{.class_s4N4-0}---are what commentators are usually quoting when they say [Septuagint ]{.class_s4N4-0}or [LXX]{.class_s4N4-0}. All of them were written more than 200 years after the death of Christ.)

Proof that the First-Century Christians used this name is found in the Old Latin, cited by church fathers between A.D. 150--250 ([Biblical Introduction,]{.class_s4N4-0} Miller, pg. 237). This word [Exodus ]{.class_s4N4-0}was in use nearly 200 years before the publication of any [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0} manuscripts which "leading authorities" quote to prove that the [Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611) used [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0} nomenclature. They say that the Old Latin of A.D. 150--250 was copied from a [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0} written around 250 B.C.

[But we have been through this theological jungle so many times that repetition is absurd. No conservative critic can quote ]{.class_s4N3-0}[one line of any writing in the world]{.class_s4N4-1}[ that uses the term ]{.class_s4N3-0}[Exodus ]{.class_s4N4-1}[for the second book of Moses BEFORE the Old Latin of A.D. 150. They will not attempt it, nor could they attain it if attempted. When a dead-]{.class_s4N3-0}orthodox Conservative says, "The [LXX]{.class_s4N6} reads\...,"[ he is citing ]{.class_s4N3-0}[Aleph, A, B, C, D,]{.class_s4N4-1}[ etc., every time he opens his mouth; and not one of these manuscripts was written any earlier than 150 years ]{.class_s4N3-0}[after ]{.class_s4N4-1}[the Old Latin Bible had circulated all over Rome and North Africa.]{.class_s4N3-0}

Since the source of this missionary translation was the missionary center in the New Testament (Antioch---Acts 11:26), it had to be revised by the Pope's private translator---Jerome (A.D. 340--420). No believer should be awakened (or even disturbed) by the peculiar[ "sounding brass"]{.class_s4N2-0} and ["tinkling cymbal"]{.class_s4N2-0} (see 1 Cor. 13:1) to the effect that the Old Latin came from some B.C. Alexandrian depravation. No such production ever existed before the time of Philo and Origen, and Origen was not born until after the Old Latin Bible was all over the Mediterranean. If the term appears in the "fifth column" of Origen's [Hexapla]{.class_s4N4-0}, any fool would know where he got it: [he got it from the Old Latin Bible which soulwinners and street preachers were using in his hometown]{.class_s4N4-0} (see [Hasting's Bible Dictionary]{.class_s4N4-0}, Vol. III, pp. 54--55)!

You see, the same manuscripts which scholars quote for the [Hexapla]{.class_s4N4-0} are often shuffled into the same deck that contains the [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}. However, the [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0} [referred to at this time]{.class_s4N4-0} contains the New Testament; for [Aleph, A, B, C,]{.class_s4N4-0} and [D]{.class_s4N4-0} do contain the New Testament. When the [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0} is quoted, it is quoted from [Vaticanus]{.class_s4N4-0} and [Sinaiticus]{.class_s4N4-0} many times; these manuscripts contain the [Apocrypha]{.class_s4N4-0} and the New Testament. Therefore, one should approach articles like "The Critic's Corner" ([Biblical Viewpoint,]{.class_s4N4-0} published by Bob Jones University) with extreme caution, for here Dr. Neal and Dr. Custer have kidded you into thinking the "[Hexapla" ]{.class_s4N4-0}contained only the Old Testament. [Not the Hexapla which Dr. Custer and Dr. Neal use, no sir!]{.class_s4N4-0} The ["Hexapla"]{.class_s4N4-0} cited in the [Pulpit Commentary;]{.class_s4N4-0} Dummelow's commentary; Jamieson, Fausset, and Browns' commentary; Lange's commentary; Clarke's []{#c2P.xhtml_page_7}commentary; and the writings of Gregory, Robertson, Warfield, Machen, Davis, Rendall, Knowling, etc., DOES have the New Testament. Custer and Neal are pulling your leg.

Bible-rejecting Conservatives can be spotted by two clichés which occur throughout church history (1630--1980).

1\. They always "throw kisses" at the[ Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} for its [literary form]{.class_s4N4-0} (i.e., "poetic cadences," "majestic style," etc.) before they proceed to tear it apart a verse at a time. This is the universal practice among all apostate "Conservatives."

2\. They will state constantly that the Bible IS the word of God, but when pinned to the mat, they will cry that it WAS the word of God. Bob Jones Sr., for example, has stated on more than twenty broadcasts---available on tape---that the first fundamental belief of ALL Christians is that "the Bible IS[ ]{.class_s4N2-0}the word of God." Now, when he said that, he also said that the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bible[ ]{.class_s4N4-0}was "the best translation we ever had" ([The Sword of the Lord Scrapbook,]{.class_s4N4-0} 1969). When Dr. Jones Sr. made that statement, [the ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901) [had been on the market for half a century]{.class_s4N4-0} and had been promoted by every orthodox school in the country. However, the faculty at Bob Jones University does not believe for a minute that the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bible is the best translation. In [The Biblical Viewpoint]{.class_s4N4-0}, the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) is corrected better than ten times per chapter. These destructive critics do NOT believe the Bible IS the word of God anymore than they believe that A. T. Robertson was a truth-rejecting Bible perverter. As a matter of fact, they do not even believe that there is any such thing as "the Bible" in the present tense. Their "Bible" cannot be found anymore than the []{#c2P.xhtml_page_8}"Bible" of Tillich, Barth, Brunner, Ockenga, Niebhur, Kagawa, Gandhi, or Albert Schweitzer.

Now, the Greek-speaking Jews of Palestine (and the Dispersion) were using the term [Exodus]{.class_s4N4-0} for the second book of the Pentateuch before the birth of Christ. The conjecture that this usage was due to the circulation of a pre-Christian [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0} is about as sound as the information given to Joe Smith by Moroni (known to his best friends as "ole' Moron"). The only copies of the mythological [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0} which contain the word [Exodus ]{.class_s4N4-0}for the title of the Book are copies which also contain "Bel and the Dragon," "The Shepherd of Hermas," "The Epistle to Barnabas," "Tobit," "Judith," and "The Inner Life of Santa Claus." (Factually speaking, the yearly article "Yes, Virginia, There is a Santa Claus" appears with about the same regularity, monotony, and perfidy that articles appear describing the [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}.)

Exodus is plainly an excellent title for the book as it deals with the "departure" or "outgoing" of God's people. It tells of their "exit."

[Historically]{.class_s4N4-0}, the book records the departure of the children of Israel from Egypt after 400 years of servitude (see Exod. 12:40 and comments).

[Devotionally]{.class_s4N4-0} (or spiritually), the book records the experiences connected with the sinner's salvation from the power of Satan---through the blood.

[Doctrinally]{.class_s4N4-0}, the book records the details that will come to pass in the Great Tribulation (Dan. 12:1--4) and at the Rapture of the Church.

The book is plainly the work of Moses and is often referred to as "The Second Book of Moses." Jesus Christ vouches for the Mosaic authorship of the book so clearly (Mark 12:26; Luke 20:37; John 8:5) that a []{#c2P.xhtml_page_9}real Bible believer is not going to waste five minutes examining theories about "authorship." Professor Robert Dick Wilson of Princeton (1856--1930) has thoroughly answered the objections to Mosaic authorship, at least where Keil, DeWette, Havernick, Delitzsch, Jahn, Fritzsche, Marsh, Stuart, Bishop, Perowne, et al., are concerned.

Readers wishing to waste a lot of time debunking the critical theories may study the works by Alexander Harkavey, Ernst Hengstenberg, and Canon Cook, which prove conclusively that whoever wrote the book of Exodus had to have an intimate knowledge of the customs, people, climate, crops, and language of Egypt [at the time]{.class_s4N4-0} the events took place. There is no evidence that any writer, living after 1000 B.C., could have known about these things with the familiarity exhibited by the author of Exodus. Whoever wrote Exodus must have spent years in Egypt exactly as the author of Genesis did, for he shows an uncanny knowledge of local customs and contemporary habits which were unknown to many Egyptologists and archaeologists in the Seventeenth, Eighteenth, and Nineteenth Centuries.

Furthermore, the clear testimony of the Scriptures is[ that Moses wrote the book]{.class_s4N4-0} (see Exod. 17:14, 24:4, and 34:27). It is plain that the attacks on Mosaic authorship come from the same scholarly tradition that began in Genesis 3:1. The "open-minded investigator" or "broad-minded researcher" often forgets that he has as many sacred cows in the pasture as the "hidebound traditionalists." It is a tradition among all Bible-rejecting infidels to question everything about the Bible and question nothing about evolution or international Socialism. Their sacred cows are so numerous that []{#c2P.xhtml_page_10}Montana and Wyoming would not suffice to pasture them, and those acquainted with the mental processes of such "oddballs" are quite familiar with their irrational prejudices against anything that smacks of Conservatism or common sense.

If Moses didn't write Exodus, WHO DID? If "J," "P," "E," "L," and "R" wrote it (Graf-Wellhausen theory: 1760--1850), can you prove that Leviticus was not written by "A," "B," "C," "D," "E," "F," and "G"? If [Ezra]{.class_s4N4-0} wrote Exodus (see Jerome, A.D. 340--420), was he not a consummate liar? Exodus 24:4 says that Moses wrote the words---[not]{.class_s4N4-0} [Ezra]{.class_s4N4-0}.

Why did the author use more Egyptian words than anywhere else in the Old Testament? How is it that the author had such a thorough acquaintance with details like those found in Exodus 15:27 and Numbers 11:7?

Could a man have given these descriptions without being in the group that left Egypt and came to these places? In Duncan's [New Light on Hebrew Origins]{.class_s4N4-0} (1936), we learn that only a native Egyptian could have known such names as "On" (Gen. 41:45, 50, 46:20) and "Pithom," (Exod. 1:11) etc. Yahuda, in [The Language of the Pentateuch and its Relation to the Egyptians ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1933), tells us that the Eighteenth Dynasty (the New Kingdom) and the Nineteenth Dynasty never referred to its Pharaohs by name, although it is apparent that later dynasties DID (see Jer. 44:30 and 2 Chron. 35:20). If someone at the time of Jeremiah or Ezra (599--536 B.C.) had written Exodus, how could they have guessed that Pharaoh went only by his title (like "Caesar" or "Khan") and was not to be mentioned by [name]{.class_s4N4-0}? We are always left to the conclusion at which we arrived before we approached the problem: Moses wrote Exodus.

The English text has 40 chapters, 1,213 verses, and 32,685 words. The Hebrew text has 1,209 verses, 16,513 words, and 63,467 letters.

Writers place the events of the book between 1700--1300 B.C., 1700--1500 B.C., 1600--1300 B.C., 1400--1200 B.C., and 1300--1100 B.C., give or take a few decades. (Liberal scholars love to push everything Biblical as far [forward ]{.class_s4N4-0}as they can, and everything Darwinian as far [backward ]{.class_s4N4-0}as they can, so this irrational compulsion disqualifies them from ever being able to give a reasonable chronology. We shall discuss the matter further when we comment on Exod. 1:8.)

Many comments have been made on the material in the book of Exodus, and it has produced a score of written commentaries by Rivet, Rosenmuller, Keil, Delitzsch, Knobel, DeWette, Von Lengerke, Von Gohlen, Stahelin, Raschi, and others. (We will make direct reference constantly to Clarke, Dummelow, Williams, the [Pulpit Commentary,]{.class_s4N4-0} the [Wycliffe Commentary, ]{.class_s4N4-0}and Bullinger \[1836--1913\].)

The reader will find numerous references to the errors of the [ASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1901) and the ["New"]{.class_s4N4-0} [ASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1963) and the "new, new, brand new, spanking new, peachy [NASV]{.class_s4N4-0}," which are Roman Catholic corruptions now being recommended by Conservative and "Fundamental" scholars. This Alexandrian chameleon was slipped in by the gullible Seminary faculties of dead-orthodox schools in 1901, and has enjoyed the reputation of being the "most accurate translation available" since that time. Readers of [The Bible Believer's Commentary on Proverbs ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1972 \[hereafter referred to as [Commentary on Proverbs]{.class_s4N4-0}\]), and [The Bible Believer's Commentary on Galatians-Colossians]{.class_s4N4-0} (1973 \[hereafter referred to []{#c2P.xhtml_page_12}as [Commentary on Galatians-Colossians]{.class_s4N4-0}\]) have learned that the[ ASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1901) is probably second only to the [New English Bible ]{.class_s4N4-0}for inconsistent translating, slovenly scholarship, lack of attention to cross-references, pointless readings, irrational emendations, disorganized thinking, and destructive criticism. We will point out a few of its countless blunders as we proceed, but of course, we cannot take time to note all of its erratum. The [New Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_s4N4-0} incorporates many of these ancient corruptions into its text, although the frontispiece of the [New Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_s4N4-0} innocently professes to be "[The Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0}." Unable to keep up the sales of the bankrupt[ ASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1901), the dead-orthodox committee of the ["New" Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1967) simply smuggled the[ ASV]{.class_s4N4-0} text into the [Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611) and went on professing to be publishing a [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}text (see, for example, Gen. 3:5; Dan. 3:25; Prov. 3:34, 7:27, 19:24; and numerous other places). It is indeed a sad commentary on "Fundamentalists" when they have to resort to lying about the Bible text in order to sell a corrupt "Bible" that went broke. The text of the ["New" Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_s4N4-0} is by no means the ["Authorized King James Version,"]{.class_s4N4-0} even though that is what E. Schuyler English and his committee said it was on the title page. It is a mongrel version designed to replace the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) with the Roman Catholic [ASV]{.class_s4N4-0} of 1901 (see [Manuscript Evidence]{.class_s4N4-0}). \[Editor's note: this is a reference to the original [NSRB]{.class_s4N4-0} before Oxford Press actually did change the text to the [AV.]{.class_s4N4-0}\]

The Hebrew title ("[And These Are the Names]{.class_s4N4-0}") indicates the importance of "names" in the book. The Christian will observe the reoccurrence of "names" as []{#c2P.xhtml_page_13}they are related to man, God, and Israel in 3:6, 14--15, 6:3, 9:16, 23:21, 28:9--12, 31:2--6, 33:12, 19, and 39:6--7.

The book of Exodus sets forth seven great truths.

1\. Unsaved mankind is like a slave in bondage to a fierce master.

2\. It takes a direct intervention by God to free the slave.

3\. God chooses messengers to bring His "emancipation proclamation."

4\. Redemption is by blood and power.

5\. There can be no compromise with the world system by the redeemed.

6\. Redemption only starts a lifetime of trials and testings.

7\. God has standards of holiness which are fixed and absolute.

There are many other spiritual lessons in the book, and we shall discuss them as we come to the passages in which they appear.

Throughout our study, from Exodus 1:1 to Exodus 40:38, we shall assume that God has delivered to us (without proven error or contradiction) the correct text. This is the one which He wishes us to have, and He expects us to believe it and treasure it as "that inestimable treasure, [which excelleth all the riches of the earth\...]{.class_s4N4-0}"[ ]{.class_s4N4-0}([Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} Dedicatory, 1611).

Where the great Conservative linguists, etymologists, lexicographers, manuscript detectives, commentators, "leading authorities" or "qualified authorities" attack this text, we shall meet force with force and fire with fire, and do unto them as they have done unto God's Holy Bible. The author will make no apologies to anyone for his terminology in this commentary, and if it had []{#c2P.xhtml_page_14}no other justification than the language used by the Bible's adversaries, it would be entirely justified. Where Jamieson, Fausset, Brown, Dummelow, Henry, Clarke, Adams, Gray, Ellicott, Lange, Knobel, Kalisch, Grotius, Custer, Neal, Smith, Archer, Weniger, Mauro, Payne, Thieme, Thiele, Rimmer, Voss, Calvin, Baumgarten, Kippe, Frank, Rosenmuller, Kurtz, Vater, Keil, Jahn, Reinke, Winer, Ewald, Hoffmann, Canon Cook, and Delitzsch disagree with the text of the [Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611), we shall simply correct their mistakes and lay them aside.

May it forever be said of those of us who believe the Book:

"[For this cause also thank we God without ceasing, because, when ye received the word of God which ye heard of us, ye received it not as the word of men, but as it is in truth, the word of God, which effectually worketh also in you that believe"]{.class_s4N2-0} (l Thess. 2:13).

And may our attitude toward the "leading authorities" who reject it forever be:

["O fools, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Luke 24:25).

Until the scholars, critics, commentators, and the "majority of Conservative eggheads" can produce better fruit than the appalling, stinking, religious rot of 1900--1970, we shall stick by the text that converted more people to Christ than the original autographs. Therefore! we shall study and write with a humble spirit, a willing mind, and a believing heart, submitting ourselves (at all times) to that Great Teacher of John 16:13, who is perfectly able to correct any book written by any []{#c2P.xhtml_page_15}Fundamentalist or atheist who tampered with the God-honored text of the Protestant Reformation.

[]{#c4A.xhtml}

# Chapter 1 {.heading_s4G-0}

1:1 "Now these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt; every man and his household came with Jacob.

2 Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah,

3 Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin,

4 Dan, and Naphtali, Gad, and Asher.

5 And all the souls that came out of the loins of Jacob were seventy souls: for Joseph was in Egypt already.

6 And Joseph died, and all his brethren, and all that generation."

The opening word[ "Now"]{.class_s4N2-0} indicates the place which the book of Exodus occupies in the writings of Moses. Observe that Leviticus and Numbers both begin the same way in the Hebrew text, for the conjunction "now" is translated ["and"]{.class_s4N2-0} in those two books.

["Now these are the names of the children of Israel" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 1). Thus, the [names ]{.class_s4N4-0}are connected with redemption, and this explains why there are more verses in the Bible listing [names ]{.class_s4N4-0}than there are explaining the origins of the universe. Jesus tells the disciples to ["rejoice"]{.class_s4N2-0} because their ["names]{.class_s4N2-0} [are]{.class_s4N2-0} [written in heaven"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Luke 10:20), and Paul speaks of his ["fellowlabourers whose names are in the book of life" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Phil. 4:3).

["The names of the children of Israel." ]{.class_s4N2-0}The mistranslation of the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901) is apparent by the fact that it altered ["children"]{.class_s4N2-0} to "sons" and then reversed field and called them ["children"]{.class_s4N4-0} not ten verses later (see [ASV]{.class_s4N4-0}, Exod. 1:7). However, this is quite typical of the []{#c4A.xhtml_page_17}inconsistent translating of the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}committee in any of its editions (1901, 1963).

["Every man and his household came with Jacob" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 1). Then, Jacob is included in the total of ["seventy"]{.class_s4N2-0} given in verse 5. (The reader is referred to the lengthy discussion of this number in[ The Bible Believer's Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Peter Ruckman, 1969, 1995, Gen. 46:26--27 \[hereafter referred to as [Commentary on Genesis.]{.class_s4N4-0}\].)

["Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah]{.class_s4N2-0}" (vs. 2). The order is as follows: six sons of Leah, one of Rachel, two of Bilhah, and two of Zilpah. The corrupt ["LXX,"]{.class_s4N4-0} written several hundred years after the ascension of Christ, has transferred ["Joseph was in Egypt already"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 5) back to the beginning of the genealogy in verse 2. This also is quite typical of the type of manuscripts from which the [NASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1963) was translated.

["And all the souls that came out of the loins of Jacob" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 5). The reader should study the lengthy comments made on ["souls"]{.class_s4N2-0} in [The Commentary on Genesis,]{.class_s4N4-0} Genesis chapters 1--3.

The terms [soul ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [body ]{.class_s4N4-0}are often interchangeable in the Old Testament (see Gen. 19:20, 46:27; Lev. 22:6; and Num. 31:28). This is misinterpreted by Jehovah's Witnesses to mean that the body and soul are [identical]{.class_s4N4-0}---which they are NOT (1 Thess. 5:23). The soul and body are stuck together in the Old Testament and remain so until the spiritual circumcision of Colossians 2:11 (see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 2:7).

["And Joseph died, and all his brethren, and all that generation"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 6). The original twelve patriarchs and their children have perished (we shall take up the time element under vs. 8). At the very start, the reader []{#c4A.xhtml_page_18}should observe the great spiritual truths hidden in these opening verses which, to all appearances, are nothing more than a statistical table.

1\. We enter ["Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} through our fathers (vs. 5). It is our father's ["seed"]{.class_s4N2-0} which is ["corruptible"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 Pet. 1:23), so we too are corruptible. We enter the world system through ["the loins"]{.class_s4N2-0} of our ancestors who---like Joseph---["died\...and he was put in a coffin in Egypt" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Gen. 50:26).

2\. Joseph, the greatest type of Christ in the Bible (see [Commentary on Genesis,]{.class_s4N4-0} Gen. 37:1--11), dies exactly as our Lord died---in Egypt (vs. 6). Furthermore, ["his brethren" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Matt. 28:10) all died and their generation (1 Cor. 15:6).

3\. As Christ was "called out of Egypt" (Matt. 2:15), so God's children (Exod. 1--12) must be called out, since they are a ["church"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Acts 7:38) and a church is a "called-out assembly" (Gk. [ekklesia]{.class_s4N4-0}).

The book of Exodus, therefore, begins exactly where Genesis left off. Joseph (a type of Christ) is dead, and his bones are in Egypt---a type of this world (see comments on Gen. 50:25--26). But God did not leave one of Christ's bones (Exod. 12:46) in this world (Luke 24:39, 51), so Joseph's bones will have to be "called out" (Exod. 13:19). Ah, the fabulous riches of the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611)! "How unsearchable are its judgments and its ways past finding out (cf. Rom. 11:33)!"

1:7 "And the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied, and waxed exceeding mighty; and the land was filled with them.

8 Now there arose up a new king over Egypt, which knew not Joseph.

9 And he said unto his people, Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we:

10 Come on, let us deal wisely with them; lest they multiply, and it come to pass, that, when there falleth out any war, they join also unto our enemies, and fight against us, and so get them up out of the land.

11 Therefore they did set over them taskmasters to afflict them with their burdens. And they built for Pharaoh treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses.

12 But the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and grew. And they were grieved because of the children of Israel.

13 And the Egyptians made the children of Israel to serve with rigour:

14 And they made their lives bitter with hard bondage, in mortar, and in brick, and in all manner of service in the field: all their service, wherein they made them serve, was with rigour."

Verse 8 "ties the rag on the bush," to speak in the vernacular, and locating the ["new king over Egypt, which knew not Joseph,"]{.class_s4N2-0} is like locating a leaf in a tornado. There is about as much unanimity of opinion on the subject as there has been in the United Nations since 1950.

["And the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 7). The children of Israel were doing God's will (Gen. 15:13), for the command to ["multiply, and replenish the earth"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Gen. 1:28) is carried out until they[ "waxed exceeding mighty; and the land was filled with them" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 7). When they leave Egypt in chapter 12, the roster of men is 600,000 footmen plus women, children, and older men carried on []{#c4A.xhtml_page_20}animals (Exod. 12:37). The moral lesson is clear: where God's people obey His will, in the center of His will, they bear ["much fruit" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(John 15:1--5).

["Now there arose up a new king over Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 8). This king is probably the king of chapter 2; if he is, he must be reigning from the birth of Moses to Moses' private exodus (Exod. 2:15) forty years later. When was Moses born? Once the birth of Moses is established, one must date the Exodus of chapter 12 very close to eighty years from Moses' birthday, for the interval between Exodus 2:1 and Exodus 2:15 is plainly ["forty years"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Acts 7:23), and the interval between Exodus 2:15 and Exodus 4 is plainly ["forty years"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Acts 7:30). Depending upon the length of time between Exodus 5 and Exodus 12, we should be able to locate the date of the Exodus and, figuring backwards, the date of the ["new king over Egypt, which knew not Joseph."]{.class_s4N2-0}

To give you some idea of the problem involved, observe that Bullinger (as Ussher) sets the date of the Exodus at 1491 B.C. ([Companion Bible, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bullinger, App. 50) and then sets it again at 1225--1215 (Ibid., App. 37)! This is a contradiction of 266 years at a minimum. This is quite a confession for a man who documented [every year of history ]{.class_s4N4-0}in the Bible from Adam to Paul's last imprisonment! Bullinger allows 215 years for the sojourning in Egypt (as also Calvin, Grotius, Morinus, Delitzsch, Adam Clarke, Houbigant, Baumgarten, Matthew Henry, Marsham, Geddes, and Kennicott), and this would make Rameses II the Pharaoh of verse 8. But Rameses II (sometimes spelled "Ramses") has a little difficulty getting in the lineup, for Dummelow dates him at 1340 B.C. ([Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0}, pg. 49), while most []{#c4A.xhtml_page_21}Egyptologists date him at 1292--1220 B.C. Here we have another "misunderstanding" of nearly fifty years.

[The Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_s4N4-0}(pg. 294) agrees with Bullinger's second guess that the Pharaoh of the Exodus was Menephthah (sometimes "Meneptah"), who reigned from 1225--1215 or 1235--1220. The [Wycliffe ]{.class_s4N4-0}commentator, Professor Johnson, conjectures the Exodus was around 1300 B.C., and mentions the fact that some place it around 1440 B.C. If the Exodus were in 1300 B.C., the Pharaoh of our text had to reign before Seti I (1314--1292), for the ["new king over Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} was dead before Moses returned to Egypt at the age of eighty (see Exod. 2:23 cf. 4:19). Tutankhamun reigns from 1358--1350, and Harmhabtah reigns from 1350--1315, followed by Rameses I (1315--1314). Rameses is the likely candidate, but it is hard to imagine a king who reigned for only one year (1315--1314) getting everything done that is recorded in Exodus 1:11--12, 15, 22, and 2:5--15. Therefore, 1300 B.C. is a very implausible date for the Exodus of Exodus chapter 12.

The problem is even more complicated, for eighty years have to elapse between Exodus 2:2 and Exodus chapter 12. If the Exodus is 1300 B.C., the Pharaoh of the "oppression" is [Merenptah. ]{.class_s4N4-0}This is the standard Liberal view adopted by all religious infidels, and it would place the Exodus between 1235 and 1220 B.C. (If one follows this asinine line of reasoning, he must add forty years for the wilderness wanderings (making 1195--1180 for Joshua chapter 1 and another 300-plus years for the book of Judges) making it 895--880 to Samuel, and then another 120 years \[Acts 13:21--22\] would have to be added to arrive at the reign of Solomon. This would put Solomon on the throne between [775--760 B.C.,]{.class_s4N4-0} and by []{#c4A.xhtml_page_22}such a reckoning, John the Baptist could have been the moderator of the Council of Nicaea in A.D. 325!)

After placing Exodus chapter 12 at 1491 B.C., Bullinger goes into a trance and blacks out; when he "comes to," we find him agreeing with the position stated above ([Companion Bible, ]{.class_s4N4-0}App. 37). In order to do this, he must admit an error of about 270 years in chronology!

Clarke (Commentary, pg. 293) states that the[ "new king" ]{.class_s4N2-0}was either Rameses, Miamun, or Amentophis, who reigned around 1604 B.C. This "jives" with Ussher's date of 1491 for the Exodus, although it still makes the Egyptian servitude last only 215 years instead of 400 (cf. Gen. 15:13). Amentophis would have to be a "Hyksos" king by such a reckoning.

Larkin ([Dispensational Truth, ]{.class_s4N6}1918 \[hereafter referred to as [Disp. ]{.class_s4N6}[Truth]{.class_s4N4-1}[\]) places the Exodus at 1606 B.C.---with the Pharaoh being a Hyksos ruler---making the birth of Moses around 1686 B.C.. This would make the ]{.class_s4N3-0}["new king"]{.class_s4N2-1}[ of Exodus 1:8 a foreign ruler, matching the statement of Bullinger (]{.class_s4N3-0}[Companion Bible,]{.class_s4N4-1}[ pg. 73) that this Pharaoh was ]{.class_s4N3-0}["the Assyrian" ]{.class_s4N2-1}[of Isaiah 52:4.]{.class_s4N3-0}

Dummelow calls the Pharaoh of the Exodus[ Rameses II,]{.class_s4N4-0} but he insists that his reigning period was around 1340 B.C. Most tables list Rameses II as reigning from 1292--1225 B.C. Someone is off fifty years again!

[Unger ]{.class_s4N4-0}puts the Exodus at 1441 B.C.

[Thiele ]{.class_s4N4-0}puts it around 1447 B.C. (Note his date for the Temple---967 B.C.)

[Payne]{.class_s4N4-0} settles for 1446 B.C. (Temple date, 966 B.C.) .

[Albright]{.class_s4N4-0} comes up with 1438 B.C. (Temple date, 958 B.C.) .

But one cannot appreciate the real problem involved here until one faces the four terrible texts known as "the Chronicler's Nightmare."

1\. [Acts 13:20, ]{.class_s4N4-0}which states that there are "about 450 years" from Judges to ["Samuel the prophet. "]{.class_s4N2-0}

2\. [First Kings 6:1, ]{.class_s4N4-0}which states that there are "480 years" from the Exodus to the erection of the Temple.

3\. [Galatians 3:17,]{.class_s4N4-0} which states that there are "430 years" from the Abrahamic promises (Gen. 22:18) to the giving of the Law in Exodus chapter 20.

4\. [Acts 7:6,]{.class_s4N4-0} which applies 400 years to Egyptian servitude under the Pharaohs.

We can see at a glance that if No. 3 is true, the children of Israel could not possibly have been in Egypt 430 years or even 400 years, because one must allow for the lives of Isaac and Jacob between Genesis chapter 21 and the time of Jacob's entrance into Egypt (Gen. 46).

One can see at a glance that if No. 2 is correct, Paul must have misquoted something in Acts chapter 13, for as surely as oceans follow the coastline, 480 cannot be arrived at by computing from the Exodus to Solomon. Saul reigned forty years (Acts 13:21), David reigned forty years (1 Kings 2:11), Israel wandered in the wilderness forty years (Acts 13:18); so without the ministry of Samuel (which is reckoned at nearly twenty years), we have 120 years which must be added to Paul's statement in No. 1. One hundred and twenty plus 450 is certainly not the 480 of 1 Kings 6:1; it is 570 years! Bullinger senses this problem, although he computes 573 years from Exodus to 1 Kings 6. On this basis, Bullinger puts the date for the Temple at 917 B.C. (Unger, Thiele, Payne, and Albright are at variance with this date.)

["Now I beseech you, brethren\...that ye all speak the same thing" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(1 Cor. 1:10)! (As a guard at the zoo said to a drunk who had fallen into the snake pit: "You seem to have a problem there, friend.")

The last man in the ring is Clarence Larkin. He places the Exodus at 1606 B.C., puts the children of Israel in Egypt 215 years, and then places the other 215 years between Genesis 12:4 and Genesis chapter 46. By this computation, Larkin places the dedication of the Temple at 1005 B.C. (1 Kings 8:1--66), and the date of 1 Kings 6:1 at 591 years after the Exodus. This system forces the birth of Abraham back to 2111 B.C., whereas Bullinger put it at 1996 B.C. (There are other systems of chronology by Clinton, Rimmer, Thiele, and others; but by now the reader may have come to the conclusion that it is not for him ["to]{.class_s4N2-0} [know the times or the seasons"]{.class_s4N2-0} \[Acts 1:7\]!)

A. If Larkin is right, the ruler of the oppression (1:8) and the ruler of chapters 5--12 were both [Hyksos rulers ]{.class_s4N4-0}who reigned in Egypt between 1778--1580 B.C.

B. If Bullinger's second guess is right (1220 B.C.), Nebuchadnezzar invaded Judaea (Jer. 52) near 250 B.C.---about 100 years AFTER Alexander the Great defeated Persia!

C. If the Exodus was in 1491 B.C., the ruler at that time was Thutmose II or Thutmose III (spelled "Thotmose" and "Thotomose"), and the ["new king over Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} would be Amenhotep I or Thutmose I (1577--1501 B.C.).

D. If we assume the Israelites were persecuted 400 years in Egypt out of a total of 430 years (Exod. 12:40)---and this is the view taken by Kurtz, Winer, Knobel, Ewald, Delitzsch, Kippe, Frank, Beer, Rosenmuller, Jahn, Vater, Thiele, Reinke, Hofmann, and Michaelis---they []{#c4A.xhtml_page_25}must have entered Egypt around 1800 B.C. and departed around 1370 B.C. (This would place Solomon's Temple at 820--800 B.C.)

A possible way of reconciling 1 Kings Chapter 6 with Acts 13:20 and the statements of Genesis 15:13 and Exodus 12:40 is to split the "430 years" into two sections. Then we must allow somewhere from 570--591 years between Exodus chapter 12 and 1 Kings chapter 6, and say that any discrepancy is accounted for by the fact that during 90--111 years of that time, Israel was in servitude to her enemies (Judg. 3:8, 12--14, 4:2--3, 6:1, 10:7--8, and 13:1). God is not counting this time in His reckonings with Israel (see Dan. 9:24--27 for a similar situation). (Larkin does this as does Bullinger, although Bullinger arrives at 573 years for the intermission, whereas Larkin arrives at the number 591.) But even to do this, we must read 12:40 as: ["Now the sojourning of the children of Israel\...was four hundred and thirty years."]{.class_s4N2-0} Temporarily ignoring ["who dwelt in Egypt,"]{.class_s4N2-0} we can date the 430 years of Exodus 12:40 from Genesis 12:4 (Larkin, Bullinger, and the "[Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}"). However\![ this is a contradiction of Acts 7:6. ]{.class_s4N4-0}If we accept Acts 7:6 as it stands, we will have to do two things in regards to the chronological systems.

1\. We must accept Bullinger's LATE date for the Temple (917 B.C.).

2\. We must accept a later date than Larkin (1606 B.C.) for the Exodus.

The only possible setup which would retain a literal interpretation of all the facts, in all the passages given to us, would be:

1\. The building of the Temple in 930 B.C. (which would be 570 actual years from the Exodus \[Exod. 12--15\]).

2\. [Ninety years ]{.class_s4N4-0}of this 570 years would be discounted due to the servitudes in the book of the Judges. (This would place the Exodus at 1500 B.C., during the reign of Thutmose III.)

3\. Four hundred and thirty years before this would place the chronology at 1930 B.C. for Jacob and his family to enter Egypt (Gen. 46), and this would push the birth of Abraham back to around 2196 B.C.

This system does not dovetail with Bullinger's chronology, which we used for the [Commentary on Genesis]{.class_s4N4-0}. However, one can see that Larkin has approached this position in placing the birth of Abraham at 2111 B.C. instead of the normally accepted 1996 B.C. (Bullinger and others). One can also see that Bullinger tried to approach this position by giving[ a late date]{.class_s4N4-0} of 917 B.C. for the Temple, opposing Larkin\'s 1005 B.C. As a matter of fact, if we move the Temple date (930 B.C.) back to 910 B.C.---only seven years off Bullinger's estimation---we can move the whole scale forward twenty years and arrive at:

1\. The Temple built in 910 B.C., which is 570 years from the Exodus; this figure would include the ninety years of servitude in Judges.

2\. The Exodus placement would be at 1480 B.C. with Thutmose III on the throne.

[3. ]{.class_s4N3-0}Jacob would then be entering Egypt around 1910 B.C. (Gen. 46), and this would push the birth of Abraham back to 2176 B.C.

The summation of the evidence would indicate that if the 430 years of Exodus 12:40 means the Israelites []{#c4A.xhtml_page_27}were in Egypt that long, the Pharaoh of the Exodus is Thutmose III ("Thotmose" III), and the Exodus took place between 1500--1480 B.C. If the figures are correct as given by Larkin, then the Pharaohs of Exodus chapter 1 and Exodus chapter 12 were BOTH Hyksos rulers (1770--1606 B.C.).

If all sides are balanced, Larkin has much in his favor, for it may be noted that the Pharaoh of Exodus 1:8 is obviously a foreigner---not a native Egyptian (see Exod. 1:9 and comments). In favor of a later date (1440--1420), it is noticed that Thutmose IV was not the firstborn son of Amenhotep II (1447--1420). The "Dream Stele" recently found ([A Survey of Old Testament Introduction,]{.class_s4N4-0} Archer, 1964, pg. 218 \[hereafter referred to as [Survey of O. T. Intro.]{.class_s4N4-0}\]) would indicate that Thutmose IV got on the throne by a miracle, which was revealed to him in a dream about "taking sand off of the Sphinx." Since Amenhotep II left no firstborn, he himself could be the Pharaoh of the Exodus, making the ["new king"]{.class_s4N2-0} of our text Thutmose I or Amenhotep I (sometimes "Amenhoteph").

Returning to our text: having taken "absence without leave," we settle for Amenhotep I or a Hyksos as the ["new king" ]{.class_s4N2-0}of Exodus 1:8.

["Which knew not Joseph"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 8). This is more important than WHO the king was, for the expression implies the following: he didn't know anything about Joseph, he didn't care to find out anything about Joseph, and he didn't care anything about Joseph's people. Joseph, of course, is a type of Christ (see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 37:25--28). The statement, then, is ominous: ["a new king which knew not Jesus."]{.class_s4N4-0} (See 2 []{#c4A.xhtml_page_28}Thess. 1:8 and Exod. 5:2. Judg. 2:10 and Hos. 2:8 show how the word ["knew" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is used in this passage.)

["And he said unto his people" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 9). What this Pharaoh says indicates that he is NOT a native Egyptian. Note:[ \"the people of Israel are more and mightier than we."]{.class_s4N2-0}

Well, at this time, they do not nearly come to "600,000 men" plus women and children (see Exod. 12:37). The population of Egypt was well over this figure at any time between 1800--1300 B.C. In Josephus' ["Antiquities" ]{.class_s4N4-0}(ii, 9), we are told that the crown of Exodus 1:8 had gone to a different "family." He may well have been ["the Assyrian"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Isaiah 52:4, who is a type of the Antichrist (see [The Mark of the Beast, ]{.class_s4N4-0}1960 \[hereafter referred to as [Mark of the Beast]{.class_s4N4-0}\]).

["Come on, let us deal wisely with them"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 10). Observe that Pharaoh desires to keep Israel in the land, and that his main worry is that they will leave it. He counts this to be as great a catastrophe as defeat in battle: ["and fight against us, and so get them up out of the land." ]{.class_s4N2-0}In all of this, we feel forebodings of Adolf Hitler and Adolf Eichmann. While they wanted to keep the Jew in Germany and Europe, they wanted to [exterminate him at th]{.class_s4N4-0}e same time (vs. 22). Pharaoh's program, however, includes integration, and the type of integration so vividly described by the great Communist Michael Luther King Jr. in "I Had a Dream"---one gray race produced by COLORED MEN and WHITE WOMEN. Observe: ["if it be a son, then ye shall kill him" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 16).

["Therefore they did set over them taskmasters\...and they built\...treasure cities"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 11). The verse has been used to prove that the Exodus []{#c4A.xhtml_page_29}had to take place around 1220 B.C., because Rameses I does not show up until 1315--1314 B.C. It is taken for granted that the city ["Raamses"]{.class_s4N2-0} must be named in his honor or his son's honor (Raamses II, 1292--1225 B.C.). However, the name Rameses (Ramses, Raamses, etc.) means "Begotten of Ra," and many royal names of the Hyksos dynasties end with this name, thus fixing it 400 years before Rameses I showed up on the throne.

However, the serious student of Scripture would have never been deceived for a moment by ANY scholar's attempt to make Rameses I (or Seti I) the Pharaoh of the oppression, for the serious student has access to information unknown to the writers of the[ Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0} (Vol. I, pg. 10). In Genesis 47:11 in the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) Holy Bible, one will find[ "the land of Rameses"]{.class_s4N2-0} mentioned, showing the name was in common use more than 100 years before Exodus 1:11 (if this 215--215 year setup is used for "the sojourning"). This would be more than 200 years if the children of Israel were in Egypt the full "430" years.

Dummelow refuses to read the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}text and calls[ "the land"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Genesis 47:11 "the city" ([Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0}, pg. 49), but again, this is quite typical of "higher scholarship" of any sort.

["Treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 11). Pithom is the "Patumos" of Herodotus (ii. 158), and the [Septuagint ]{.class_s4N4-0}conflates the passage by adding "and On, which is Heliopolis, the city of the Sun." Modern[ Tel-el-Maskhuta]{.class_s4N4-0} or [Tell Erretabeh]{.class_s4N4-0} is given as the approximate location. Raamses is Tanis ["in the field of Zoan"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Psa. 78:12). Some identify it as the modern[ Tel-el Kebir.]{.class_s4N4-0}

These cities are cities containing grain elevators for the storage of grain (Gen. 41:48), and they were []{#c4A.xhtml_page_30}"frontier" outposts stocked with weapons and munitions. Josephus interprets the passage as meaning that the Jews built the pyramids at this time. However, Diodorus Siculus ([Biblical History, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Lib i, cap lxiv) says that there is little evidence that this was so. Furthermore, the mathematical marvels contained in the Great Pyramid ([Disp. Truth,]{.class_s4N4-0} 32) demonstrate clearly the hand of the "gods" of Genesis 6:1--4, not a Hamitic ruler who was worried about losing slaves (see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 1, 6). The date of the Great Pyramid is well beyond 1600--1200 B.C. A more appropriate date would be 3000 B.C.

["But the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 12). One is reminded of the first three centuries of church history following the ascension (Acts 1). "The blood of the martyrs is the seed of the church," and the more the early Christians were afflicted, ["the more they multiplied"]{.class_s4N2-0} (in converts) ["and grew"]{.class_s4N2-0} (in grace). Jailors were converted while they were starving Christian prisoners, torturers were converted while they were carrying out orders to kill Christians, and pagan philosophers were converted while attacking the doctrines of the Bible.

["And they were grieved because of the children of Israel"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 12). So the world has always been[ "grieved" ]{.class_s4N2-0}when God's people prosper (Neh. 3--10), and where there is "great rejoicing" and "great results," the Christ-rejectors (Acts 13:50) are filled with apprehension and fear (John 11:47--53). The silversmiths (see [Bible Believer's Commentary on Acts, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Acts 19:21--28 \[hereafter referred to as [Commentary on Acts]{.class_s4N4-0}\]) were grieved to the point of rioting, and the Pharisees were grieved to the point of murdering (John 12:10).

["And the Egyptians made the children of Israel to serve\...hard bondage\...with rigour" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 13--14). To appreciate the picture, one should read [Forged in Fury]{.class_s4N4-0} (Elkins, 1971, pp. 1--193) or[ Treblinka ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Steiner, 1966). Truth is stranger than fiction, and one can hardly imagine "working conditions" like the following:

1\. Carrying 100-pound cement sacks at a trot, running for distances of 100--200 yards, [four hours at a stretch,]{.class_s4N4-0} without resting or getting a drink of water.

2\. Carrying 75-pound blocks of hewn stone up an incline in bare feet, returning, carrying up 85-pound blocks, returning, carrying up 95-pound blocks, etc., until you fall flat on your face. You are then clubbed to death by your "employer."

3\. Sorting out clothes eight hours a day from a pile of clothes twenty feet high. On top of the pile sits an SS man with a Luger. If you [look up ]{.class_s4N4-0}from your work one time, you are shot, and if you [slow down ]{.class_s4N4-0}from your work, you are shot (without knowing when the shot is going to come). If you [overwork ]{.class_s4N4-0}to keep from getting shot, you will die in three months, because you are already living on sub-normal rations for any worker.

4\. Being harnessed to a wagon full of logs with nineteen other men, and then being driven by a whip through snow, while the driver commands you to sing as you haul the load five miles.

5\. Forced to sleep in below-zero weather [in your underwear only,]{.class_s4N4-0} in a barracks where the "bed" is a flat board with each man getting one blanket. Any man caught sleeping in his clothes is hauled outside naked and left there to freeze to death.

Now this is only a very small part of what went on at Belsen-Belsen, Auschwitz, Buchenwald, Sachsenhausen, []{#c4A.xhtml_page_32}and Treblinka. Furthermore, the same things are going on, [at this moment,]{.class_s4N4-0} in prison camps in Romania, Bulgaria, Poland, Russia, Siberia, and Red China. "Pharaoh" is a cruel taskmaster, and bondage in "Egypt" is a picture of the unsaved man, alone in the world without hope and without God (Heb. 2:15; Gal. 4:3; Rom. 8:21; Eph. 2:11--12). What men call "hell on earth" is often only a foretaste of ["the wrath to come"]{.class_s4N2-0}. (Matt. 3:7; Luke 3:7; 1 Thess. 1:10).

[The Israelites---we will call them "Jews" as Paul did (Rom. 3:1--3) when he spoke of Israel at Sinai and the descendants of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Rom. 9:24)---are set to work carrying huge lumps of clay, water jars, and bricks on their shoulders. They dig canals and irrigation ditches,]{.class_s4N3-0} and build walls and ramparts (Philo, pg. 86, Mangey's edition).

Mehemet Ali (not "Cassius Clay") lost 20,000 laborers out of 150,000 in the construction of the Alexandrian canal in the Nineteenth Century. In the time of the Pharaohs, Josephus says that nearly 120,000 workmen lost their lives. Working all day in a blazing Egyptian sun, under the whip of a Hamite, is not exactly the best way in the world to enjoy "the art of gracious living."

According to Samuel Bochart (1599--1667), the word [Pharaoh]{.class_s4N4-0}, in Arabic, means[ ]{.class_s4N4-0}"crocodile"[ ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Adam Clarke's Commentary, pg. 189). The word is highly suggestive of Satan (Ezek. 29:3); so much so, in fact, that the infantile [New Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_s4N4-0} makes the ridiculous suggestion that ["leviathan" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Job 41:1) is perhaps "a crocodile." The[ Leviathan]{.class_s4N4-0} of Job 41 is the [Dragon ]{.class_s4N4-0}of Revelation 12 and the [Serpent]{.class_s4N4-0} of Isaiah 27:1 (see Psa. 74:14), and anybody with an atom of sense could have []{#c4A.xhtml_page_33}read any one of the passages. (But as we have said before, this is quite typical of "Conservative scholarship"---of any kind.) All the service of Pharaoh in this world is ["with rigour,"]{.class_s4N2-0} brother (Acts 15:10), while all the service of the Lord ["in heavenly places"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Eph. 2:6--10) is ["with joy unspeakable and full of glory"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 Pet. 1:8)!

1:15 "And the king of Egypt spake to the Hebrew midwives, of which the name of the one was Shiphrah, and the name of the other Puah:

16 And he said, When ye do the office of a midwife to the Hebrew women, and see them upon the stools; if it be a son, then ye shall kill him: but if it be a daughter, then she shall live.

17 But the midwives feared God, and did not as the king of Egypt commanded them, but saved the men children alive.

18 And the king of Egypt called for the midwives, and said unto them, Why have ye done this thing, and have saved the men children alive?

19 And the midwives said unto Pharaoh, Because the Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women; for they are lively, and are delivered ere the midwives come in unto them.

20 Therefore God dealt well with the midwives: and the people multiplied, and waxed very mighty.

21 And it came to pass, because the midwives feared God, that he made them houses.

22 And Pharaoh charged all his people, saying, Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river, and every daughter ye shall save alive."

Now radical measures are taken to stop the "multiplying and growing." It is apparent to Pharaoh that []{#c4A.xhtml_page_34}enforced slave labor is not going to solve the problem, so a drastic step is taken to cut down the number of the children of Israel: murder. Exactly as Caesar tried to murder Christ before He was born (Luke 2:1--5, [fifty miles on the back of a donkey, ]{.class_s4N4-0}when a woman is within a week of delivery), and as Herod tried to murder Christ when He was born (Matt. 2:16), so Pharaoh tries to prevent any "Moses" from growing up. The orders are clear: infanticide.

["And the king of Egypt spake to the Hebrew midwives"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 15). Two are selected by name, which would indicate that they are overseers or "head nurses" of larger groups. Their job would be to report all births, even if they did not oversee the actual birth every time. ["The king of Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} remains anonymous to this day, while these two insignificant Jewish women have their names recorded for posterity and they are preserved (here) for more than 3,100 years (see comment under 1:1).

["Shiphrah\...Puah" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 15). The words mean[ "handsome"]{.class_s4N4-0} and ["mouthy"]{.class_s4N4-0} (or "elegant" and "one who cries out").

["And he said, When ye do the office of a midwife\...But the midwives feared God, and did not as the king of Egypt commanded them"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 16--17). Here is the classic case of divided loyalties, repeated in Matthew 22:21. ["Caesar"]{.class_s4N2-0} has given a clear order, and Caesar represents ["the powers that be"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Rom. 13:1--3). Exodus 1:21 tells us that God will bless some people for ignoring ["Caesar,"]{.class_s4N2-0} and the test case is clear: God has commanded one thing (Gen. 9:6), and ["the powers that be"]{.class_s4N2-0} have commanded another thing (vs. 16). Where the political, religious, or civil []{#c4A.xhtml_page_35}powers give orders that are in opposition to God, they are to be ignored (see [Commentary on Acts, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Acts 4:19).

Since every falsehood is three-fourths truth (see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 3:1--2), the Jehovah's Witnesses and the "conscientious objectors" have seized on this principle and insisted that the command[ to bear arms in defense of one's country ]{.class_s4N4-0}is a violation of Exodus 20:13---one of ["the commandments of God"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 Cor. 7:19). This twisted, non-Christian, pagan perversion is arrived at by refusing to believe 1 Samuel 15:1--3 and then refusing to accept the authority of the Lord Jesus Christ on the matter of the interpretation of the word ["kill" ]{.class_s4N2-0}in ["Thou shalt not kill"]{.class_s4N2-0} (see Matt. 19:18). Jesus defines the word as ["murder"]{.class_s4N2-0} in Matthew 19:18, exactly as it is found in Numbers chapter 35, and the pitiful whining cowardice of "conscientious objectors" on "religious grounds" is only the superficial self-righteousness of a turncoat who will not pay his debt to the society in which he lives. All conscientious objectors, in wartime, could serve as MEDICS and first-aid men, attached to RIFLE COMPANIES. And an "objector" who would object to this great humanitarian ministry has no real religious convictions about anything; he is a mouse---stuffed full of cream cheese.

They had a saying back in World War II, which I have always thought was very appropriate: "If you don't believe in shootin' anybody, okay. We'll take you where the shootin' is goin' on, and then you can make up your mind there!"

There are thousands of good jobs in the infantry and the medical corps for men who don't believe in "killing," nor can any amount of sophistry cover the real issue. Saying "If I have ANY PART in it, I am just as guilty []{#c4A.xhtml_page_36}as the man who pulls the trigger" doesn't solve a thing. If you're working in a factory that makes nylon---used in parachute shroud lines---you have a "part" in it, and if you are enjoying a good income because a nearby Army base has attracted a large population to your town, you have a "part" in it. The only "part" you don't have in it is the part of a mature, responsible citizen who realizes his obligation to his government. You're a freeloader (koine on a Transylvanian diorite for ["bum"]{.class_s4N4-0}).

Shiphrah and Puah were not drafted to defend their country (Num. 1--2); they were ordered to butcher babies.

["If it be a son, then ye shall kill him" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 16). Observe, again, the unseen hand behind Pharaoh (2 Cor. 4:4), who is still trying to make sure that Genesis 3:15 is not fulfilled.

["And see them upon the stools" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(for this manner of giving birth, see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 30:3). These "birth stools" were often the two circular stones on the potter's wheel (see Jer. 18:3).

["And the king of Egypt called for the midwives\...Why have ye done this thing\...Because the Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 18--19). The answer to Pharaoh is the truth, although it is not the whole truth. Not only are ["the Hebrew women not as the Egyptian women," ]{.class_s4N2-0}but Israel is not as Egypt (Exod. 11:7); and the Egyptian [horses ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Isa. 31:3) are not like the horses of God's people (Rev. 19:14), in spite of Jehoshaphat's confusion (1 Kings 22:4). God forever separates His people from Pharaoh's people (2 Cor. 6:14--17), and He holds in abomination any preacher engaged in integrating (synthesizing, interrelating, programming, communicating, socializing, []{#c4A.xhtml_page_37}etc.) the two opposites (see [Commentary on Proverbs,]{.class_s4N4-0} preface).

["And are delivered ere the midwives come in unto them"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 19). The Hebrew women are living hard outdoor lives. They are in good physical condition and are not pampered with the luxuries and sensual pleasures of the Hamites who have slaves to wait on them. (Slavery, one must remember, is an AFRICAN and Arabian institution \[see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 10:10\]. What Dutch privateers and New England merchantmen learned \[1600--1800\], they learned from Ham, [not Japheth.]{.class_s4N4-0}) In many parts of the world today, women deliver themselves, and in many parts of the world, they are up and working two days after giving birth.

["Therefore God dealt well with the midwives\...he made them houses"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 20--21). The Hebrew ["lahem"]{.class_s4N4-0} (masculine gender) is used for ["made them,"]{.class_s4N2-0} leading some scholars to think that the blessing was bestowed on Israel, not on the midwives (since that word is feminine). However, the genders are used interchangeably in 1 Kings 22:17 and 2 Chronicles 18:6. The sense of the passage is that God blessed the midwives for their courage and increased their households and families exactly as they allowed the other families to increase (see how the word ["house" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is used in Luke 11:17; Acts 16:31; 1 Tim. 3:4; Tit. 1:11; etc.).

["Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 22). The command would not have shocked any Egyptian in Egypt. The river was sacred, and pagans have often sacrificed children to rivers. Beyond this, the Egyptians (as the ancient Romans and Assyrians) were as cruel in their treatment of slaves and captives as the []{#c4A.xhtml_page_38}SS units in charge of the concentration camps. In Syria and Carthage, children were constantly offered to idols. In Carthage and Phoenicia, children were burned alive. In Egypt, the monarchs sometimes mixed the blood of their captives with the mortar they used in building walls (Diod. Sic. i, 67,[ Manual of Ancient History, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Lenormant, Vol. I, p. 423 \[hereafter referred to as [Manual of Ancient History]{.class_s4N4-0}\]). In Africa today, twins are still stomped to death in a hole, then buried, and then the earth is packed down on them with the feet of drugged dancers. Unwanted children in Africa and Rome (2000--50 B.C.) were simply abandoned in the streets or jungles (like they are in Cleveland and Philadelphia); as late as A.D. 1937, soldiers were still throwing one and two-year-old babies up in the air and catching them on their bayonets (Japanese invasion of Shanghai, 1937).

["Ye shall cast into the river."]{.class_s4N2-0} So according to the eternal laws of sowing and reaping (Psa. 35:8), a Pharaoh loses his firstborn (12:29) and winds up drowned (15:4). Students who have often wondered why the Holy Spirit would never bear witness to the rash of translations that flooded America between 1880 and 1900, should take a "breather" here to observe the comments made on chapter 1 by the original author of the "[Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}." (As far as anyone can tell \[see[ Manuscript Evidence]{.class_s4N4-0}\], Origen was the compiler of the so-called "[LXX]{.class_s4N4-0}." His own fifth column in the Hexapla was the prototype for [Vaticanus ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [Sinaiticus]{.class_s4N4-0}. Although Symmachus, Aquila, and Theodotian also tried their hand at perverting the Old Testament, Origen's fifth column was the first real "[Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}"; it was written more than 100 years AFTER the completion of the New Testament canon.) Origen says of Exodus chapter 1:

a\. The male and female children are the "natural and [rational ]{.class_s4N4-0}faculties of the soul."

b\. The "midwives" are the "Old and the New Testaments."

c\. The male seed is[ "spiritual SCIENCE"]{.class_s4N4-0} (!) through which people try to get to Heaven.

This is what is known as the "allegorical" method of private interpretation (see [Commentary on Proverbs, ]{.class_s4N4-0}preface). In "layman's language," this means: the private interpretation of the Bible by a Bible-rejecting [Ebionite ]{.class_s4N4-0}who was hung up on neo-Platonic philosophy (Col. 2:8). Origen is a part of the great heritage of "Christian schools," and this heritage explains why the "old-time revivals" and the sweeping power of the Holy Spirit have left America forever. The first "Christian school" was that of Philo of Alexandria (20 B.C.--A.D. 50), and his spiritual children were Pantaenus (A.D. 150--200), Clement (A.D. 150--215), and Origen (A.D. 185--254).

[Not one man in the lot professed the New Birth apart from sprinkling by water. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Origen goes so far as to say that we are "born of Pharaoh" at the first birth and are "born of Moses" at the second birth through "the WATERS OF BAPTISM" (Second Homily on Exodus). God has never blessed, nor will He ever bless, [any Bible ]{.class_s4N4-0}translated by men who followed the manuscripts manufactured and transmitted by the "World's Most Unusual University" at Alexandria (see[ Satan's Masterpiece, the New ASV, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Peter Ruckman, 1972, \[hereafter referred to as [Satan's Materpiece]{.class_s4N4-0}\]).

[]{#c89.xhtml}

# Chapter 2 {.heading_s4G-2}

2:1 "And there went a man of the house of Levi, and took to wife a daughter of Levi.

2 And the woman conceived, and bare a son: and when she saw him that he was a goodly child, she hid him three months.

3 And when she could not longer hide him, she took for him an ark of bulrushes, and daubed it with slime and with pitch, and put the child therein; and she laid it in the flags by the river's brink.

4 And his sister stood afar off, to wit what would be done to him."

The first chapter skips over a period varying from 135 to 350 years (see notes on the chronology under 1:8), and this is done in 22 verses. Chapter 2 narrows the time element down to eighty years, and from chapter 3 onward, we are dealing with matters of days, weeks, and months.

["And there went a man of the house of Levi"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 1). Since Numbers 36:6--7 commands the marriages to be "inter-tribe," [this part]{.class_s4N4-0} of the marriage is proper. The man is Amram and the woman is Jochebed---his aunt (Exod. 6:20). [This part ]{.class_s4N4-0}of the marriage would have been illegal under Leviticus 18:12, but the Mosaic Law had not yet been given (note [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 4:17 and comments). The comment by the [Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_s4N4-0}is quite typical of the modern attitude towards the "[LXX]{.class_s4N4-0}" (see above). We are told that the ["daughter of Levi"]{.class_s4N2-0} could NOT have been an actual ["daughter,"]{.class_s4N2-0} for this is "irreconcilable with the chronology\...." Then the [Commentary ]{.class_s4N4-0}refers us []{#c89.xhtml_page_41}to the "[LXX]{.class_s4N4-0}," which has conjectured "a wife of the DAUGHTERS of Levi." This, of course, was done by Philo, Clement, Origen, and company, as they did not believe that four generations would make up the 215 year period (see remarks under 1:8). Exactly what the [Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Vol. I, pg. 23) meant by "irreconcilable" is "incomprehensible"---if not downright "inscrutable." Observe:

1\. Levi begat Kohath and Jochebed (Exod. 6:16, 20).

2\. Kohath begat Amram (Exod. 6:18).

3\. Amram married Jochebed (Exod. 2:1).

4\. Amram and Jochebed gave birth to Moses, Miriam, and Aaron (Exod. 6:20, 2:4, 15:20).

What is the "irreconcilable" problem?

1\. Jacob lived to be 147 years old (Gen. 47:27).

2\. Joseph lived to be 110 years old (Gen. 50:26).

3\. Moses lived to be 120 years old (Deut. 34:7).

4\. If Levi lived to be 137 (Exod. 6:16), and Kohath lived to be 133 (Exod. 6:18), and Amram lived to be 137 (Exod. 6:20), and Moses was eighty at the time of the Exodus, then there is every possibility that Moses became eighty exactly 215 years after Jacob and Levi came down to Egypt (Gen. 46). The ages are all given in Exodus chapter 6, and every man whose age is given in the list lived longer than Joseph and Moses. As a matter of fact, by a late computing (reckoning some sons born around sixty or seventy years old, which is not unreasonable considering the lifespan is nearly twice what it is today), it is not hard to figure 300 years from Genesis chapter 47 to Exodus chapter 12.

["And the woman conceived, and bare a son"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 2). This boy is destined to become one of the greatest men in the history of Israel, and only []{#c89.xhtml_page_42}Abraham succeeds in establishing himself in the hearts of the Jewish people as firmly as Moses. ["Our father Abraham" ]{.class_s4N2-0}appears in many of the New Testament controversies (Matt. 3:9; Luke 20:37; John 8:33--53; etc.), but we find also ["Moses commanded,"]{.class_s4N2-0} and ["Moses said,"]{.class_s4N2-0} etc. (Matt. 19:7, 22:24; Mark 1:44, 7:10, 12:26; Luke 24:27; John 5:45, 7:19, 23; Acts 6:11; etc.). This is the great lawgiver who outclassed Hammurabi as far as the [Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611) outclassed its three nearest rivals. So great was he in God's sight, that God told Israel that the greatest prophet Israel would ever see---His own Son---would be a prophet "like unto Moses" (Deut. 18:15).

1\. Like Jesus, Moses was hid in Egypt.

2\. Like Jesus, Moses was rejected when he "came to his own."

3\. Like Jesus, he was "drawn out of the water" (see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 1:6--7 and analysis).

4\. Like Jesus, he had a Gentile bride.

5\. Like Jesus, he was sent to deliver his people from bondage.

6\. Like Jesus, he was divinely chosen and preserved in infancy.

7\. Like Jesus, he gave his people living water (1 Cor. 10:1--4).

8\. Like Jesus, he was a Shepherd (Isa. 63:11).

9\. Like Jesus, he died before he got into the Promised Land.

10\. Like Jesus, his converts leave Egypt for the Promised Land.

11\. Like Jesus, he arose from the grave (Jude 9).

12\. Like Jesus, he was a prince in disguise (Exod. 2:19). Of course, there are many other Scriptural similarities.

["And when she saw him that he was a goodly child" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 2). The [AV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611) says ["fair"]{.class_s4N2-0} and ["proper"]{.class_s4N2-0} in addition to ["goodly"]{.class_s4N2-0} (see Acts 7:20 and Heb. 11:23). The writers of the corrupt [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}, written more than 110 years after the completion of the New Testament canon, have placed the New Testament GREEK of Acts 7:20 ("[asteois]{.class_s4N4-0}") back into the book of Exodus to give one the impression that Stephen was quoting the publication of Philo, Origen, Clement, et al. All manuscripts containing this erratum are from the Third to the Fifth Century A.D. (Cf. [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 46.)

It is apparent that two children are born before Moses is born: these are [Miriam ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [Aaron]{.class_s4N4-0}. Miriam has to be at least ten years old (see Exod. 2:4, 7) at the time of the passage, although Aaron was only three years older than Moses (Exod. 7:7). There is a chance that the edict of Pharaoh was put into effect AFTER the birth of Aaron, but this is conjecture. The baby Moses is ["hid\...three months,"]{.class_s4N2-0} and when his mother ["could no longer hide him,"]{.class_s4N2-0} she made an ark and ["put the child therein; and she laid it in the flags by the river\'s brink" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 3).

There is some significance in the expression ["when she saw him that he was a goodly child," ]{.class_s4N2-0}for any mother would have attempted to preserve a baby of her own whether it was[ "goodly"]{.class_s4N2-0} or not. However, assuming that the command did not come forth until after the birth of Aaron, and assuming that there were periodic "searches" for new babies (cf. 2 Chron. 22:10--12), we arrive at the conclusion that Jochebed did not report the []{#c89.xhtml_page_44}birth for fear of what would happen to the baby. You can take it for granted that if the midwives had told the [mothers ]{.class_s4N4-0}of the order of Pharaoh, not half a dozen Jewish mothers would have turned the children over. Egyptians were used to murdering babies---[somebody else\'s babies.]{.class_s4N4-0}

Jochebed senses some great destiny for her child, and it is highly probable that God told Jochebed something about this destiny. Instead of being turned over to the midwife, or even having the baby reported, Moses was secreted away somewhere in the house until he was in danger of being detected through a cry of hunger at the wrong time. Pharaoh cannot keep track of every woman, nor can the midwives. There are somewhere around 100,000 wives, and the children are coming like hailstones (Exod. 1:7).

["She took for him an ark of bulrushes" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 3). As Noah's ark and the Lord's ark (1 Sam 5:1), this is a coffin-shaped object (Arabic: "[taboth]{.class_s4N4-0}"). It was made of papyrus reeds, as stated in Isaiah 18:2. The ["slime"]{.class_s4N2-0} and ["pitch"]{.class_s4N2-0} are the bitumen and tar "coating" which water-proofed the vessel.

["And she laid it in the flags by the river\'s brink"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3). The ark is placed where it will not move with the current, and then Miriam is stationed near it for protection. Among many things not mentioned in the text are: the danger of fly or mosquito bites (and the malaria that accompanies the latter), the danger of predatory birds attacking the eyes of the baby, and the danger of crocodiles. So to all appearances, Moses is abandoned to God. With the exception of a sister (who may have been only a teenager at the time), the baby rocks alone in the bulrushes in a frail boat and waits for death; after all, you cannot raise a boy in a reed coffin!

2:5 "And the daughter of Pharaoh came down to wash herself at the river; and her maidens walked along by the river's side; and when she saw the ark among the flags, she sent her maid to fetch it.

6 And when she had opened it, she saw the child: and, behold, the babe wept. And she had compassion on him, and said, This is one of the Hebrews' children.

7 [Then said his sister to Pharaoh's daughter, Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for thee?]{.class_s4N7-0}

8 And Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Go. And the maid went and called the child's mother.

9 And Pharaoh's daughter said unto her, Take this child away, and nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages. And the woman took the child, and nursed it.

10 And the child grew, and she brought him unto Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. And she called his name Moses: and she said, Because I drew him out of the water."

Commentators are divided upon whether Pharaoh's daughter came to the river for a morning dip or a "ritual bath." Either way---it doesn't make that much difference---God directs her footsteps to the right place at the right time, and the child who was "abandoned to God" gets better care than many a child who was turned over to Social Security in the welfare Socialist State of America.

["And the daughter of Pharaoh came down to wash herself"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 5). The historians have called her[ "Bithia," "Merrhis," "Artapanus," "Pharia,"]{.class_s4N4-0} and ["Thermuthis";]{.class_s4N4-0} so she is a perfect match for the ["new king" ]{.class_s4N2-0}of Exodus 1:8. You could no more identify her exactly than you could point out a Christian at []{#c89.xhtml_page_46}the Woodstock Festival (Aug. 17, 1969). What follows would indicate that Miriam was a maiden attendant for Pharaoh's daughter, and she already knew the appointed place of bathing.

1\. His sister stayed with the ark to see what would happen.

2\. When Pharaoh's daughter came, ["she sent]{.class_s4N2-0} [her maid" ]{.class_s4N2-0}to get it (vs. 5).

3\. When it was opened the ["maid"]{.class_s4N2-0} who was there was Miriam (vs. 7)!

4\. For this to take place, Miriam would have had to be walking along the bank (vs. 5) with the ["maidens"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Pharaoh's daughter.

All of this means that Jochebed was taking a calculated risk, and she never intended for a moment to leave Moses exposed to the elements more than a day or two at the most. The ark  was planted where Pharaoh's daughter bathed. Miriam knew where she bathed, and Miriam was one of her attendants who knew the heart and character of Pharaoh's daughter. These facts detract some subject matter from sermons about the "providence of God" in regards to Moses' preservation, but still, one must not forget that one cry from the baby at the wrong time could have been the end for him, because [Pharaoh ]{.class_s4N4-0}might have showed up the next morning (Exod. 7:15!), instead of his daughter.

The crucial moment in the account is verse 6---["and when she had opened it, she saw the child: and, behold, the babe wept."]{.class_s4N2-0} One sees Jochebed's foresight; the ark (like Noah's and the Lord's) was "covered" (vs. 6[---"she opened it"]{.class_s4N2-0}). The Lord says to Moses, "Yell your head off!" The baby squalls, and what woman with a real woman's heart can stand the sight []{#c89.xhtml_page_47}of an abandoned baby [crying? ]{.class_s4N4-0}Pharaoh and some of his NKVD friends might have upended the ark and drowned the boy right on the spot, but Miss Pharaoh ["had compassion on him, and said, This is one of the Hebrews' children" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 6).

[Again, the Holy Spirit reminds us that Miriam is in the entourage of Pharaoh's daughter,]{.class_s4N5-0} for the expression which Pharaoh's daughter uses to ["her maid"]{.class_s4N2-0} is the expression of one group speaking of a foreign group:[ "This is one of the Hebrews' children" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 6). If Miriam had suddenly popped up on the bank, sticking her nose into royal business, would not Pharaoh's daughter have said, "This child belongs with some of YOUR people"?

Miriam is quick to seize any opportunity. No sooner have the words fallen from the mouth of royalty (vs. 6), than Miriam rushes in with, ["Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for thee?" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 7). Notice the last two words. Talk about the power of "positive suggestion." Miriam has talked the princess into adopting an abandoned baby before the lady can even analyze her own feelings.

The picture is quite vivid. Pharaoh's daughter is still gazing at the baby without thinking much of anything, and her own feelings at this time are nothing but [love ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [pity. ]{.class_s4N4-0}In this passive state, which is almost hypnotic, Miriam pulls in close and makes a "suggestion." If the king's daughter had been thinking, she might have stopped long enough to ask:

1\. Why should YOU go?

2\. Why should you go right NOW? What's the hurry?

3\. Why not get an EGYPTIAN nurse for it?

4\. What makes you think that I want to be burdened with the responsibility? But where the heart loves, the mind often has to go to sleep.

["And Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Go. And the maid went and called the child's mother" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 8). ["The maid,"]{.class_s4N2-0} then, is Miriam (cf. vss. 5--7). One can imagine the anxiety and anticipation which flooded Jochebed's soul when she saw her firstborn daughter running back to the house from the river. Did Pharaoh's SS men find the baby? Did Pharaoh's daughter turn it over to the guards? Was the ark caught in the current and taken down the river? In spite of well laid plans (see Jacob, Gen. 32:13--24!), we still worry and fret. What mother would not have found her pulse pounding when Miriam came running toward the house a little after sunup?

["And Pharaoh's daughter said unto her\...nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 9). Again, we find the great lesson of "trust and obey" driven home forcibly to the reader. Here is a woman who abandons her child to God and the elements and, then, probably spends the whole night in prayer; in the morning, she not only has a live baby, she has her own baby to nurse herself! If that weren't enough (see 1 Sam. 2:21!), she gets [paid ]{.class_s4N4-0}for doing what most mothers have to pay to do.

["Nurse it for me"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 9). This matches the truth of Hebrews 11:24, and indicates that Pharaoh's daughter intended to adopt Moses and, perhaps, make him an heir to the throne (see comments under 1:8). Observe, further, that[ "he became her son" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 10).

Moses, then, is taken into the royal household and raised as a "child of the King." Of course, this is the []{#c89.xhtml_page_49 .class-1}[wrong King ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Job 41:34), and the learning and wisdom which he obtains in this estate (Acts 7:22) is the ["wisdom of this world"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 Cor. 2:1--6; James 3:15). Josephus states that Moses became a General in the Egyptian army and defeated the Ethiopians in battle, for Moses ["was]{.class_s4N2-0} [mighty in words and in deeds" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Acts 7:22). Opponents of the Bible find a contradiction between Acts 7:22 and Exodus 4:10, but there is a time element to consider in Acts 7:22 (cf. Exod. 2:15, for example). The mighty words and deeds which Stephen mentions occur AFTER Moses has been sent down into Egypt.

["Take this child away, and nurse it for me"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 9). The devout student of the Bible, who ignores translations like the [NASV, ]{.class_s4N4-0}cannot fail to see the beautiful anti-type. God delivered His own Son to a Hebrew woman, and she[ "took the child, and nursed it" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 9)!
