["Nurse it for me"]{.class_s4N2-0} would fit the angel's mouth when he spoke God's message to Mary (Luke 1:35), and one cannot but marvel at ["the foolishness of God" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(1 Cor. 1:25--28) in allowing Himself to enter His own created world as a helpless, hungry, crying baby who needed [nursing ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1 Tim 3:16)! (The subject is beyond comprehension when one studies it. If the "Son of Man" was man as well as God \[John 1:1, 14 and 1 John 1:1--2\], He was as helpless and as dependent as any baby who is dependent on its mother during the years of his infancy.)

["And she called his names Moses\...I drew him out of the water"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 10). The word is an Egyptian word, and yet it matches a Hebrew word. Combining the two words, we arrive at several meanings. (This is natural, as the tri-consonantal roots of most Hebrew []{#c89.xhtml_page_50}words can be expanded into anywhere from two to eight other words.)

1\. A son (Egyptian ["Mes,"]{.class_s4N4-0} as in ThothMES).

2\. To bear (Egyptian[ "Mesi"]{.class_s4N4-0}), or to produce.

3\. To draw out (Heb. ["Mashah"]{.class_s4N4-0}).

4\. One drawn out (Heb. ["Mosheh"]{.class_s4N4-0}).

5\. To draw forth.

Moses is a son, produced and drawn forth in birth from the water. (See remarks under "water birth" in [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 1:20. The first birth, as in John 3:5, is a water birth, and so all Hebrews understood the matter.)

Moses' education included theology, medicine, arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, orthography, grammar, history, and engineering. (You might say he held a B.S. and a B.D. degree which, in some cases, should stand for "Brainwashed Silly" and "Beyond Deliverance.")

2:11 "And it came to pass in those days, when Moses was grown, that he went out unto his brethren, and looked on their burdens: and he spied an Egyptian smiting an Hebrew, one of his brethren.

12 And he looked this way and that way, and when he saw that there was no man, he slew the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand.

13 And when he went out the second day, behold, two men of the Hebrews strove together: and he said to him that did the wrong, Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?

14 And he said, Who made thee a prince and a judge over us? intendest thou to kill me, as thou killedst the Egyptian? And Moses feared, and said, Surely this thing is known.

15 Now when Pharaoh heard this thing, he sought to slay Moses. But Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh, and dwelt in the land of Midian: and he sat down by a well.

16 Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters: and they came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father's flock.

17 And the shepherds came and drove them away: but Moses stood up and helped them, and watered their flock.

18 And when they came to Reuel their father, he said, How is it that ye are come so soon to day?

19 And they said, An Egyptian delivered us out of the hand of the shepherds, and also drew water enough for us, and watered the flock.

20 And he said unto his daughters, And where is he? why is it that ye have left the man? call him, that he may eat bread.

21 And Moses was content to dwell with the man: and he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter.

22 And she bare him a son, and he called his name Gershom: for he said, I have been a stranger in a strange land."

Moses is now forty years old (Acts 7:23). He dresses, walks, talks, and acts like an Egyptian (Heb. 11:24). However, underneath the royal robes there beats the heart of a covenant child of Abraham, and under the royal headdress there ticks the brain of a man who has assimilated nearly all the details of Genesis chapters 1--50. You see, if his mother raised him in the formative years (vss. 9--10), you can be certain that she drilled him in the oral history and traditions of their race clear back []{#c89.xhtml_page_52}to Shem and beyond the Flood. When Moses goes out to look on the burdens of the slave laborers, it is recorded that ["he went out unto his brethren, and looked on their burdens\...."]{.class_s4N2-0}

There is no reason why Moses should kill an Egyptian if he did not recognize that ["his brethren"]{.class_s4N2-0} were Jews and the Egyptian was NOT a Jew.

["He went out unto his brethren" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 11). This is the fulfillment of Hebrews 11:24, and it implies a turning from the courtly house at Tanis and all of the plush life connected with it; Moses renounced his Egyptian birthright. Although we are not given a close-up of the scene that took place, it is certain that Moses had some hard words with his foster mother and, perhaps, with Pharaoh himself. We are told in the book of Hebrews that Moses chose[ "to suffer affliction,"]{.class_s4N2-0} and this implies that his "going out" (vs. 11) cost him a great price.

["He slew the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 12). On the face of the account, this would be MURDER; however, ["justifiable homicide"]{.class_s4N4-0} might have showed up on the records of Heaven, for the word ["smiting" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(found in Gen. 32:8, 11; Exod. 21:12; Num. 3:13, 33:4, and 35:18) is a reference to someone killing somebody else. The Noahic law on this matter is clear (Gen. 9:4--6), although there is a question about Moses' authority to carry out the execution. However, ["the]{.class_s4N2-0} [revenger of blood"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Num. 35:19--27) is usually kin to the murdered man, and Moses was "kin" to ["his brethren"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 11).

It is apparent, from verse 13, that Moses knew of his mission. He felt called to be a deliverer to the Jewish slaves (called[ "Jews"]{.class_s4N2-0} by Paul in Rom. 3:29 and 1 Cor. 10:32, etc.). Stephen says that when Moses performed the []{#c89.xhtml_page_53}acts of Exodus 2:11--14, ["he supposed his brethren would have understood how that God by his hand would deliver them"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Acts 7:25). Moses' error was in getting ahead of the Lord, and subsequent studies in the life of Moses (Num. 20:11) show that though his[ heart condition]{.class_s4N4-0} was meek (Num. 12:3), his[ head condition]{.class_s4N4-0} was strong (Num. 16:15). Moses was running ahead of God's timetable by forty years when he attempted to be "Sir Galahad" (or "St. George" or "Kissinger" or whatever it was), and the full 430 years were not up yet.

Bullinger conjectures that Moses saw "no man" to HELP the Hebrew (vs. 12), but this is a little farfetched. Moses was not the kind of a man to look frantically up and down to see if anyone would show up to knock down an Egyptian overseer. Moses is "casing the joint" ("checking the terrain," "canvassing the area," etc.) to make sure he can kill the rascal and get away with it.

Furthermore, this interpretation is confirmed absolutely by what follows in verse 14. Moses didn't think the thing was known. He had made sure the "coast was clear" before he decked (flattened, creamed, tumbled, erased, did-in, etc.) the Egyptian overseer.

["And when he went out the second day\... Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 13). Moses has gone back to the working area the day after the murder (or capital punishment), and he roams up and down among the labor gangs and "looks upon their burdens" (vs. 11). Here are two men carrying a long log (supposedly [Dathan ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [Abiram]{.class_s4N4-0}), and they drop it. The man at the fore-end of the log really "drops it," for as the man on the rear is squatting and lowering the log onto a pile, the man at the front simply lets go and the whole weight crashes down, mashing his companion's fingers. []{#c89.xhtml_page_54}An argument follows. One word leads to another, and the first thing you know, the man who dropped the load hauls off and pastes his co-worker in the chops. The blow is returned, and the two men come to grips and struggle by the pile of logs. At this moment, Moses steps in and separates them; since he is wearing Egyptian robes, the men release their holds quickly. They stand apart, panting from anger and exhaustion, and Moses turns to the man who started the ruckus and says, ["Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?"]{.class_s4N2-0}

Clarke finds it "strange" that persecuted slaves should be persecuting [each other,]{.class_s4N4-0} but this is real naiveté. American prisoners of war, on board Japanese prison ships, killed each other to get blood to drink ([Give Us This Day, ]{.class_s4N4-0}S. Stewart, W. Norton, and Co., 1957). Clergymen in Russian prisons (1946--1966) argued with each other, ridiculed each other, and betrayed each other while being tortured by Communist guards ([The Wurmbrand Letters, ]{.class_s4N4-0}1967, pp. 35--63). These authentic eyewitness accounts reinforce the great central truth of Scripture (Jer. 17:9) that human nature is human nature anywhere, even in a concentration camp.

Observe, further, how the "hit dog" yells first. The man who ["did the wrong"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 13) is the first one to answer---as usual. (At an intersection where two cars collided, four different "body shop" trucks showed up at the same time, each looking for a customer. One of the car drivers, who had been in the accident, turned purple with rage and said, "Here you guys are just worried about making a buck! Why, someone might have been killed in this accident!" He stomped off and failed to hear the Biblical remark of one of the truckers: "Ah bet a dime to a donut thas' the fella what caused the wreck." And it was.)

["And he said, Who made thee a prince and a judge over us?\...Moses feared, and said, Surely this thing is known" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 14). Then someone [was looking]{.class_s4N4-0} when Moses was shoveling sand around (vs. 12). If one man knew, then two knew; if two knew, their wives knew; and if two women know anything, the whole town knows it.

["Who made thee a prince and a judge?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 14). [Well,]{.class_s4N4-0} [God Almighty did]{.class_s4N4-0} (Acts 7:35), exactly as Jesus was made a Prince and Judge over Israel (see [Commentary on Acts, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Acts 3:14--15). If you reject Him as a Prince (1 Cor. 2:3--8), you will meet Him as a Judge (Mal. 3:1--3).

["Intendest thou to kill me, as thou killedst the Egyptian?" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 14). No, Moses had come ["to seek and to save that which was lost"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Luke 19:10), and he had no intention of killing any of his brethren. However, as in the case of Christ, Moses is treated as a murderer. Capital punishment was the sentence for murder (Matt. 26:66), and it was A MURDERER who was originally scheduled for the middle cross (Luke 23:19).

["And Moses feared" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 14), which is quite typical of Moses (Exod. 3:6; Deut. 9:19; Heb. 12:21, etc.). Moses is thoroughly human (James 5:17). We find him overconfident (Exod. 2:12), angry (Num. 20:10), strong in faith (Exod. 10:26), weak in faith (Num. 11:21--22), worn-out (Exod. 17:12), full of righteous indignation (Exod. 32:19), unsure of himself (Exod. 18:15--19), discouraged (Exod. 5:22--23), elated (Exod. 8:9), upset (Num. 12:13), argumentative (Exod. 4:13--14), disobedient (Exod. 4:24--26), impatient (Exod. 2:11--12), scared to death (Heb. 12:21), zealous (Num. 11:29), powerful (Num. 16:28--32), faithful (Heb. 3:1--2), victorious (Deut. 32:43), curious (Exod. 3:13), and []{#c89.xhtml_page_56}disappointed (Deut. 3:25--26). Considering the whole record, it must be said that Moses was one of the most "human" of all the human beings who ever walked the dusty roads of this planet.

["Now when Pharaoh heard this thing, he sought to slay Moses" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 15). This time, Moses runs, so the reference in Hebrews 11:27 cannot refer to the passage. [Hebrews 11:27 ]{.class_s4N4-0}is a reference to [Exodus 10:28. ]{.class_s4N4-0}The reader will observe the undesigned coincidence found in the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) text:[ "Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Exod. 2:15), and ["Moses fled from before it"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Exod. 4:3). The ["it" ]{.class_s4N2-0}of the latter verse is[ "a SERPENT. "]{.class_s4N2-0}

["And dwelt in the land of Midian: and he sat down by a well" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 15). The reader will observe the peculiar time sequence so commonly found in the Old Testament. First,[ a complete statement ]{.class_s4N4-0}is given in regards to where Moses settled down to live---["dwelt in the land"]{.class_s4N2-0}---and then the mention is made of an incident that took place when he first arrived in this land---["and he sat down by a well." ]{.class_s4N2-0}This is important, for it shows that the apparent contradiction mentioned (Acts 7:22 cf. Exod. 4:10) can be the same type of time sequence; i.e., Moses[ "was mighty in words and in deeds" ]{.class_s4N2-0}all of his life with the children of Israel---a complete statement---and then, ["when he was full forty years old"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Acts 7:23) mentions an incident which took place BEFORE he led Israel out. The[ Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0} fails to see this Biblical pattern and alters ["sat down by a well"]{.class_s4N2-0} to "and he DWELT by the well." The Masoretic text reads as the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611).

On the humorous side, one should imagine how Moses felt penning the words of Exodus 2:14--15. He wrote []{#c89.xhtml_page_57}Exodus (see prefatory remarks), and speaking under the inspiration of the Holy Ghost (2 Pet. 1:21), he had to "tell it like it was."

["Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 16). The ["priest of Midian"]{.class_s4N2-0} is the[ "Reuel"]{.class_s4N2-0} of verse 18, and supposedly the ["Raguel"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Numbers 10:29, the ["Jethro"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Exodus 4:18, and the ["Hobab" ]{.class_s4N2-0}of Judges 4:11. The difference in spelling between Reuel and Raguel is much like the differences in Dodanim and Rodanim (alternative spelling given by the [AV]{.class_s4N4-0} translators in Gen. 10:14; 1 Chron. 1:7), Zabdi and Zimri (Josh. 7:1 cf. 1 Chron. 2:6), and Amram and Hemdan (1 Chron. 1:41 cf. Gen. 36:26). The student should observe that the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}translators had no trouble at all in copying the ["Reuel"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Genesis 36:4 into 1 Chronicles 1:35 as ["Reuel." ]{.class_s4N2-0}They had no trouble with the ["ain"]{.class_s4N4-0} (Heb.) and did NOT translate it as a "g" in ["Raguel."]{.class_s4N2-0} Plainly, the ["Reuel"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Exodus 2:18 was[ the son of ]{.class_s4N4-0}["Raguel"]{.class_s4N2-0}[ in Midian]{.class_s4N4-0} (Num. 10:29)[.]{.class_s4N4-0}

The next thing to observe about the multiplicity of names is that the name [Jethro ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Exod. 4:18) can be a [title]{.class_s4N4-0}, not a name. It is kin to such terms as [your excellency, your clemency,]{.class_s4N4-0} [honorable, ]{.class_s4N4-0}or [the right reverend,]{.class_s4N4-0} etc. (Williams fails to mention ["Hobab"]{.class_s4N2-0} as the father-in-law of Moses, in spite of the clear statements in Judg. 4:11 and Num. 10:29.)

["Hobab"]{.class_s4N2-0} is a name which Reuel's daughter Zipporah (or Moses) gave him; the word means "beloved." This word does not occur until [after ]{.class_s4N4-0}Zipporah marries Moses, and it only occurs during and after the wilderness wanderings (Num. 10:29 and Judg. 4:11).

["Raguel the Midianite"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Num. 10:29) was the father of Reuel, and there has been no "misspelling" anywhere.

["Jethro" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Exod. 4:18) is a title given to Reuel, the father-in-law of Moses, and the word means "preeminence."

Here, we have advanced light given from the English text which surpasses the Hebrew, for the Masoretic text has written ["Reuel"]{.class_s4N4-0} in Numbers 10:29. All of the commentators have assumed that the expression ["Moses' father-in-law" ]{.class_s4N2-0}was a reference to ["Reuel"]{.class_s4N4-0} (or ["Raguel"]{.class_s4N2-0}). However, we are told plainly in Judges 4:11 that Hobab (not Raguel) was the father-in-law of Moses. Raguel was the [father ]{.class_s4N4-0}of Reuel, who was called [Hobab]{.class_s4N4-0} by Moses and Zipporah; his[ priestly title]{.class_s4N4-0} was [Jethro]{.class_s4N4-0}. Notice that there is no priestly tribe until "Jethro" disappears. To all practical purposes, Jethro is the "chief priest" until Aaron is chosen (after Exod. 18).

["And the shepherds came and drove them away" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 17). Strife over "water holes" (Gen. 26:20) is as common as western movies and movies about the Foreign Legion. (Jacob assists Rachel in an argument about water \[Gen. 29:7--10\].) Someone is always filling them in or putting stones on them that women cannot lift or, in this case, scattering the flock so they cannot get their snoots in the trough. Again, Moses plays "fire-eater," and again, he meddles with strife not belonging to him (Prov. 26:17). His royal robes add to his righteous indignation, and he passes off for an Egyptian General (see comments under 2:10).

["And they said, An Egyptian delivered us out of the hand of the shepherds" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 19). Moses' act of chivalry earns him a meal (vs. 20) and, then, room and []{#c89.xhtml_page_59}board (vs. 21) and, finally, a bride (vs. 21): ["he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 21).

Here, then, is a picture of Christ in rejection. He is heir to a throne, but unable to sit down on it. He is rejected by His people Israel. In his rejected state, He is a Gentile priest who has a Gentile bride, and He feeds a foreign flock (Exod. 2:17), waiting until "His enemies be made His footstool" (Psa. 110:1).

["And she bare him a son, and he called his name Gershom" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 22). Here, the [Vulgate ]{.class_s4N4-0}and the [Septuagint ]{.class_s4N4-0}(both written more than 200 years  after the New Testament was completed) have "helped God out" again [by inserting 27 words which are not in the Hebrew ]{.class_s4N4-0}(or the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}1611) [text]{.class_s4N4-0}. (The reader should avail himself of the [Commentary on Proverbs]{.class_s4N4-0} to really grasp the significance of this last sentence. Literally scores of times the corrupt Alexandrian manuscripts add to the words of God, and sometimes Jerome follows their perverted leadership, even in the Old Testament.)

The words have been stolen from Exodus 18:4 and added here, since the corrupt Latin scribes of North Africa and Rome have assumed that Moses forgot one of his sons when he wrote the verse, or that the Holy Spirit made a mistake, or the Hebrew text was "corrupt," etc. At any rate, God couldn't get along without the "World's Most Unusual University" to help Him straighten out His Bible! Clarke suggests that this transposition is "necessary," but the serious student of Scripture will observe, immediately, that it not only is unnecessary, it is [senseless]{.class_s4N4-0}.

Eliezer is born very soon after Gershom (Exod. 4:20), but the "thoughtful assistance" of the Alexandrian faculty is hardly necessary. It certainly is dangerous considering []{#c89.xhtml_page_60}the attitude of the Alexandrian faculty which produced the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901) reading ["who"]{.class_s4N4-0} for ["God"]{.class_s4N2-0} in 1 Timothy 3:16, and the [NASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1963) reading ["man"]{.class_s4N4-0} for ["God" ]{.class_s4N2-0}in John 9:35 and "father" for ["Joseph"]{.class_s4N2-0} in Luke 2:33, etc. (Sometimes the "thoughtful assistants" turn out to be ["thoroughbred assassins."]{.class_s4N4-0})

["I have been a stranger in a strange land" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 22). So the Lord Jesus was ["a stranger in a strange land"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Matt 25:35; Psa. 39:12, 137:4, etc.), and the transition from the courts of Tanis to the backside of a desert (Exod. 3:1) was a sightseeing trip alongside the trek that brought Jesus Christ from[ "the ivory palaces"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Psa. 45:8) and the streets of gold (Rev. 21:21) to the foul-smelling straw of Bethlehem's manger. From the glory which God gave Him before the foundation of the world (John 17:5), Jesus passed to the odious smell of unwashed brutes in a stable, and there He entered the back door of an inhospitable world to be gazed at by shepherds and a man and woman who were so poor they could not offer a lamb for purification (Lev. 12:8; Luke 2:24). Quite a "transition" for One whose glory made the eyes of the angels burn like stars when they beheld Him on the throne of the universe.

"I have been a stranger in a strange land."

2:23 "And it came to pass in process of time, that the king of Egypt died: and the children of Israel sighed by reason of the bondage, and they cried, and their cry came up unto God by reason of the bondage.

24 And God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.

25 And God looked upon the children of Israel, and God had respect unto them."

The ["process of time"]{.class_s4N2-0} referred to in verse 23 is nearly forty years according to Stephen (Acts 7:30).

["And the children of Israel sighed\...and they cried" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 23). The hour of help has been delayed until things couldn't get much worse. ["By reason of the bondage" ]{.class_s4N2-0}occurs twice in the same verse and indicates that things have deteriorated since Exodus chapter 1. What no Israelite could have guessed would have been that this training was going to equip him to live forty years in the Sinaitic wilderness and then fight his way through the Ammonites, Moabites, Amorites, and Canaanites.

Every nation that came up "the hard way" survived until it got "soft." Even though Hitler lost a war, he was right, to some extent, in his estimation of the "decadent" democracies. The American armies of World War II were not of the fiber of World War I, and the armies of Vietnam and Korea (exclusive of [volunteer outfits]{.class_s4N4-0}) were not the armies of World War II. Hitler's SS divisions and regular Wermacht rarely had any trouble in obtaining a three-to-one kill ratio against any nation twice their size. [America can barely maintain that ratio against a country smaller than Arizona.]{.class_s4N4-0}

Physical examinations of army recruits between 1950 and 1970 revealed what no historical optimist would like to have made known: Americans are soft by [any standards,]{.class_s4N4-0} and they are overweight and undisciplined by [any set of regulations. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Whereas, Russia has been a military dictatorship for nearly forty years, America has been sitting and watching "Queen for a Day," and the []{#c89.xhtml_page_62}American and National "Leagues" as they horse around in "bowls" and "stadiums." Neither the Russians nor the Red Chinese have been that stupid.

The Israelites of Exodus chapters 1--3 are preparing to become ["sabres." ]{.class_s4N4-0}The brutal facts of history are that Spartan-like character and Roman-like regulations with German-like work cause nations to survive; where they conform to Italian eating habits, French sex habits, and African industriousness, they perish. No man has to be a seer to know what will happen to the "revolutionary" College professors and their hippy students when Russia or Red China finally take over: they will go to slave-labor camps, where the Negroes will either be exterminated or forced to kill and torture prisoners, exactly as the Ukrainians did to the Jews (Treblinka). There is no place in Lenin's theology (or Marx's or Trotsky's or Engels') for free speech, "women's liberation," demonstrations, revolutionary ideas, or "attacks on the establishment." Once the Socialistic government gets established and becomes "the establishment," the loafers, demonstrators, "street people," "ecologists," and dissident College professors will go to concentration camps.

Israel, in Exodus chapters 1 and 2, is in training for national survival, while her oppressors are rehearsing their own destruction.

Moses is still out on ["the backside of the desert"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Exod. 3:1), and he is probably studying the book of Job, since this book deals with the problem of "why do the righteous suffer." And there is a fair chance that Moses wrote some of Genesis at this time. He had forty years to write, and he had, at his right hand, a patriarch in the tradition of Job and Melchizedek.

["God heard\...God remembered\...God looked\...God had respect"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 23--24). The word ["God"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Heb.,[ "Elohim"]{.class_s4N4-0}) occurs five times in the three verses, and it indicates that the children of Israel are now "DEAD" (see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 5:5 and remarks). As in Abraham's case, God can now do something for them, and produce ["life from the dead"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Rom. 11:15).

The Bible believer cannot fail to note that the greatest persecution of Israel (since the recorded text) took place in Europe between 1935 and 1945. This clearly warns us that the musical number "Exodus" (played to death from 1968 to 1972), from the screenplay of the book, is a signal for Pharaoh and Moses to reappear on the scene! (See [Bible Believer's Commentary on Revelation, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Peter Ruckman, Bible Baptist Bookstore, 1988, Rev. 11:1--5 \[hereafter referred to as [Commentary on Revelation]{.class_s4N4-0}.\])

To properly appreciate the "groanings" and "cries" of the text, one should acquaint himself with the literature (1950--1970) on the German SS State, and especially the activities at Treblinka, Belsen-Belsen, Auschwitz, Buchenwald, etc. There are several good eyewitness accounts of these operations, and when the accounts are read, one closes the ledger with the feeling that if God could look upon such things and not have[ "respect" ]{.class_s4N2-0}for Israel (vs. 25), He could not be the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Exod. 3:6).

["And God looked upon the children of Israel, and God had respect unto them"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 25).

[]{#cCG.xhtml}

# Chapter 3 {.heading_s4G-1}

3:1 "Now Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father in law, the priest of Midian: and he led the flock to the backside of the desert, and came to the mountain of God, even to Horeb.

2 And the angel of the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}appeared unto him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush: and he looked, and, behold, the bush burned with fire, and the bush was not consumed.

3 And Moses said, I will now turn aside, and see this great sight, why the bush is not burnt.

4 And when the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}saw that he turned aside to see, God called unto him out of the midst of the bush, and said, Moses, Moses. And he said, Here am I.

5 And he said, Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground.

6 Moreover he said, I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. And Moses hid his face; for he was afraid to look upon God."

The narrative has now telescoped itself into details regarding minutes and hours, after speaking of events that took place through a period of nearly 400 years.

["Now Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father in law"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 1; see comments under 2:18). ["And he led the flock to the backside of the desert\...to Horeb."]{.class_s4N2-0} ["Horeb" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is another name for Sinai (Exod. 17:6, 33:6; Deut. 1:2, 19, 4:10, 15, etc.). One term may refer to a range or a particular mount in the range, but the terms are used interchangeably in the Old Testament (1 Kings 8:9, 19:8; Psa. 106:19; and Mal. 4:4). This causes []{#cCG.xhtml_page_65}Dummelow to adopt at least a portion of the Graf-Wellhausen theory, and the "worthy divine" suggests that the two [different writers ]{.class_s4N4-0}who wrote the Pentateuch each had "his own expression" for the locality. (See treatment under "Deutero-Dumpty" in the [Commentary on Genesis]{.class_s4N4-0}, Gen. 17:7--10.)

["The mountain of God"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 1) ["in Arabia"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Gel. 4:25), ["gendereth to bondage," ]{.class_s4N2-0}according to Paul (Gal. 4:24); and it is amazing to note that Israel's deliverance from bondage begins at the very place that bound them to ["the curse of the law"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Gal. 3:13)---putting them into [spiritual bondage ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Gal. 5:1)---until after the resurrection of Jesus Christ (Acts 15:10--11). St. Catherine's monastery is located on Mt. Horeb, supposedly on the very spot where Moses encountered the burning bush. But this type of produce has a familiar smell to it: it smells like the "manger" and the "cross" that Mrs. Constantine (A.D. 320) removed from Bethlehem and Jerusalem and took back to Rome with her. Perhaps no greater proof for the corruption of [Sinaiticus ]{.class_s4N4-0}(manuscript "[Aleph]{.class_s4N4-0}") could be found than the fact that Tischendorf discovered it in this dead-orthodox monastery on the spot where Israel came into bondage under the Law (John 1:17). The dead-orthodox apostate who showed Tischendorf this "great uncial codex" (to cite the destructive critics of the Bible) bragged about the fact that it was a copy of the "[Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}." Tischendorf pretty well showed the Body of Christ what he was when he obtained the manuscript, for he immediately began translating work on one of [the Apocryphal books ]{.class_s4N4-0}in its New Testament.

["And the angel of the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N8-2}[appeared unto him in a flame of fire\...and the bush was not []{#cCG.xhtml_page_66}consumed" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 2). ["The angel of the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}["]{.class_s4N2-0} bears the [Tetragrammaton ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Hebrew radicals for "Jehovah": [Yod, He, Vau, He]{.class_s4N4-0}), and although Clarke and the [New Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_s4N4-0} have a great deal to say about "the" and "an," the Greek text of Acts 7:30 says ["an," ]{.class_s4N2-0}not ["THE."]{.class_s4N2-0} (The hypercritical attitude which desires to destroy the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}\[1611\] Bible makes much of the "absence" or the "presence" of the article \[see any "new" version of John 4:24\]. At the same time, the [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_s4N4-0}calls the ordinance of water baptism a "SACRAMENT." \[See [NSRB]{.class_s4N4-0}, footnote to page 1174.\]) ["The angel of the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord,]{.class_s4N9-0}["]{.class_s4NE-0}[ ]{.class_s4NW}here, is the one who ["dwelt in the bush"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Deut. 33:16), and He is the ["God"]{.class_s4N2-0} of verses 4, 6, 13, and 14, in the context of Exodus chapter 3.

The bush is "[seneh]{.class_s4N4-0}" (Heb.)---a thorn bush. This burning bush is the first miraculous phenomenon mentioned in the Bible apart from the rapture of Enoch and Lot's wife turning into ["a pillar of salt" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Gen. 19:26). As a matter of fact, it is the first clear contradiction of natural processes ever to be [observed ]{.class_s4N4-0}by a human being. The Flood came from cosmic and natural disturbances. The fire on Sodom could have been manufactured from natural elements, and Lot's wife could easily have turned into ["a pillar of salt"]{.class_s4N2-0} under certain atmospheric conditions, fallout particles, and combinations of chemical elements. However, the burning bush is something else.

W. R. Bowie, the noted infidel ("Christian infidel"), states that this miraculous burning bush was the shimmering heat of the desert under the blaze of the eastern sun, and he tells us that the bush only "seemed" to be on fire. Evidently W. R. Bowie never observed the 3,000-year miracle of Malachi 3:6, which is the cross-[]{#cCG.xhtml_page_67}reference to the passage before us. The sign of Israel is NOT the six-pronged "Star of David" (called the star of the demon-god ["Remphan"]{.class_s4N2-0} by Stephen---Acts 7:43); in the Bible, the sign of Israel is a[ fig tree ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Jer. 24; Matt. 24:32) or an [olive tree ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Rom. 11:24) or a [burning bush]{.class_s4N4-0} (Mal. 3:6).

Ah, the bush that burns! Enslaved by Pharaoh (Exod. 2--12), harassed by the Gentiles (Judg. 4--5), oppressed by Sennacherib (2 Kings 19), massacred by Nebuchadnezzar (Lam. 1--4), slaughtered by the Romans (A.D. 70), banished from England (Canute---A.D. 1020), persecuted by Europeans (A.D. 1096, "The Holy War"), exiled by Edward I (A.D. 1272), stripped of possessions by France (A.D. 1306), burned by Germany (A.D. 1348--1350), robbed by Spain (A.D. 1492), and gassed and cremated by Himmler (A.D. 1935--1945); the burning bush still burns, and still it is NOT consumed (Mal. 3:6).

Today, Israel is the only ancient nation left alive on the face of the earth, and it is the only nation still speaking the languages of the Bible in everyday concourse. Latin is dead Biblical Greek is dead; and the languages of ancient Babylon, Egypt, Syria, Persia, and Assyria have fallen to pieces under the dust of the centuries.

The greatest miracle in the Bible, until the plagues on Egypt, is the sign of the burning bush; and the greatest miracle in history, aside from the literal, physical resurrection of Jesus Christ, is the survival of a nation against opposition that would have obliterated any other nation a dozen times. Name one nation besides Israel that could have survived 3,000 years of dispersion, separation, betrayal, persecution, ridicule, and 10,000 bloody pogroms by [eighteen nations]{.class_s4NG}, many of them []{#cCG.xhtml_page_68}more than ten times her size. The United States has only been a nation for just over 200 years; and it has already collapsed from moral corruption, insane foreign policies, overtaxing, physical softness, and leadership by Socialistic evolutionists who don't have enough sense to find Psalm 14:1 in a Bible. The first real "sign" in the Bible is a burning bush; and the signs, of course, are connected with [Israel ]{.class_s4NG}(1 Cor. 1:22).

["And Moses said, I will now]{.class_s4N2-1}[ turn aside\...And when the ]{.class_s4N7-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N8-1}[saw that he turned aside to see"]{.class_s4N7-0} (vss. 3--4). The passage is fatal to the Deutero-Dumpty theories of Graf-Wellhausen and their school of thought, even [though this nonsense is still being published by ]{.class_s4N3-0}[Life]{.class_s4N4-1}[ magazine and taught as "fact" in high school courses on "Bible." The words ]{.class_s4N3-0}["]{.class_s4N2-1}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-2}["]{.class_s4N2-1}[ and ]{.class_s4N3-0}["God"]{.class_s4N2-1}[ ("J" and "E" writers, supposedly) both occur in the ]{.class_s4N3-0}[same verse, ]{.class_s4N4-1}[with the ]{.class_s4N3-0}[same thought ]{.class_s4N4-1}[continuing through the usage of both names. One writer obviously wrote the entire verse, and he was acquainted with both names when he wrote.]{.class_s4N3-0}

Curiosity (see Zacchaeus, Luke 19:1--3) is the motivation which leads to Moses' commission as a ["king in Jeshurun"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Deut. 33:5). What W. R. Bowie in his deep and vast ignorance calls the "desert sun" and the "shimmering heat," the desert shepherd---[with forty years of experience in the "desert sun" and the "shimmering heat"!!]{.class_s4N4-0}---calls ["this great sight"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3).

["Moses, Moses" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 4). The Lord calls him twice, exactly as he calls Saul (Acts 9:4), Samuel (1 Sam. 3:10), Martha (Luke 10:41), and Simon Peter (Luke 22:31). ["And he said, Here am I."]{.class_s4N2-0}

The lesson before Moses' eyes is the lesson of Genesis chapter 15, where God first spoke of the sufferings of His people (Gen. 15:13--17). ["Our God is a consuming []{#cCG.xhtml_page_69}fire" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Heb. 12:29), and where this fire is often a light to His people (Exod. 40:38) and a purifier to their lives (Isa. 6:1--7), it is also the[ ]{.class_s4N2-0}"unquenchable fire"[ ]{.class_s4N2-0}of Mark 9:44, 46 and the fire that will[ "burn as an oven"]{.class_s4N2-0} against the wicked (Mal. 4:1). Pharaoh persecuting Israel is God's own manifestation of His wrath (Rom. 9:17; see also Jer. 52:3), so the "burning flame of fire" (vs. 2) is not merely the holiness of God manifested (Exod. 13:21, 19:18; Deut. 4:24), it is also a manifestation of His wrath on sin (2 Sam. 23:7). The "thorn bush" of our text is found again in Hebrews 6:8 and 2 Samuel 23:6. [Read those verses.]{.class_s4N4-0}

["And he said, Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes\...for the place\...is holy ground" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 5). Here, for the first time, we find the word ["HOLY"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Heb. ["qodesh"]{.class_s4N4-0}). The first time the word occurs, it is connected with:

1\. A piece of ground---[material ]{.class_s4N4-0}dirt.

2\. A burning bush---[material ]{.class_s4N4-0}vegetation.

3\. A literal mountain---[material ]{.class_s4N4-0}rock.

All of this is extremely significant for an understanding of either Testament and an understanding of the covenants made with Israel as they stand in contrast with New Testament salvation. You see, the holiness of [God]{.class_s4N4-0} Himself has not been mentioned. It is not until Exodus 15:11 that the word is connected directly to [God's Person. ]{.class_s4N4-0}The "holiness of God" is only [intimated ]{.class_s4N4-0}in the book of Genesis, and one can only comprehend his holiness there by the results of certain actions (see Gen. 3, 4, 6, 11, and 13). But throughout the next book (Exodus), one will find the most remarkable profligacy of the term [holy:]{.class_s4N4-0} [holy ]{.class_s4N4-0}place, [holy ]{.class_s4N4-0}water, [holy ]{.class_s4N4-0}people, [holy ]{.class_s4N4-0}convocation, [holy ]{.class_s4N4-0}anointing oil, [holy ]{.class_s4N4-0}garments, [holy ]{.class_s4N4-0}name, and [holy ]{.class_s4N4-0}crown.

One can see, immediately, where the papists went to get their sacramental Fascism. [They stole it from Israel]{.class_s4N4-0} (see extended comments in [The Sure Word of Prophecy, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Peter Ruckman, Bible Baptist Bookstore, 1972, \[hereafter referred to as [Sure Word of Prophecy]{.class_s4N4-0}\]). "Holy Mary," "Holy Mother," "Holy See," "holy water," and "Holy Mass" are a few of the manmade inventions which papists have added to the divine revelation in order to convince people that the "kingdom" switched hands. Observe how all of the new Catholic Bibles---[ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901), [New English]{.class_s4N4-0} (1970), [RSV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1952), and the [NASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1963)---omit the ending to Matthew 6:13, since it is a prayer for this kingdom!

The removal of the shoes is only mentioned in one other place in the Scriptures (Josh. 5:15). Although the ceremony of the "spit and polish" (Ruth 4:8; Deut. 25:9--10) is connected with Israel, these two references (Exod. 3:5 and Josh. 5:15) are unique, for they mark the beginning and the end of the route of the Second Advent of Jesus Christ. Observe how Christ's feet split the ground (Zech. 14:3--4) once He actually lands on it. This route begins at Sinai (Deut. 33:2; Hab. 3:3--6; Judg. 5:5), goes up ["the king's highway"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Num. 20:17, 21:22) across Jordan to Gilgal (Josh. 3--4), and terminates on the Mount of Olives (Zech. 14:4). (See details in the booklet [The Path of the Second Advent,]{.class_s4N4-0} Peter Ruckman, 1989[.]{.class_s4N4-0}) Bullinger, Stam, O'Hair, Clarke, Williams, Lange, Thieme, Neal, Custer, Scofield, Larkin, Dummelow, Henry, Ellicott, Pember, Sauer, McClain, Delitzsch, Keil, Gesenius, Fausset, Hort, Brown, Hengstenberg, and Pentecost naturally miss all of the references.

["Put off thy shoes"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 5). So many Shemites in the Twentieth Century remember the admonition. The []{#cCG.xhtml_page_71}Mohammeden takes off his shoes when he enters the mosque, and the Japanese take them off when they enter their homes. The ancient Greeks also borrowed the custom, and the worshippers of Diana and Jupiter picked it up also (Clarke, Vol. I, pg. 304).

["Put off thy shoes"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 5). Moses had failed to "redeem the inheritance" in his first try (!), and according to Ruth 4:1--7, it was time to "loose the shoe." Look at Psalm 60:8 where [God ]{.class_s4N4-0}kicks off His own shoes.

["Moreover he said, I am the God of thy father\...Moses hid his face" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 6).

["The God of Abraham"]{.class_s4N2-0} made promises to Ishmael, [the Arab]{.class_s4N4-0} (Gen. 21:13).

["The God of Isaac"]{.class_s4N2-0} made promises to Esau, [the Edomite]{.class_s4N4-0} (Gen. 27:38--40).

["The God of Jacob"]{.class_s4N2-0} made promises to [the twelve patriarchs]{.class_s4N4-0} (Gen. 49), which now belong to the Jews as a nation.

According to Clarke (Ibid., pg. 305), this means that the Arab and the Turk are "heirs to the promises," and they will be "gathered in" with ["the fulness of the Gentiles" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Rom. 11:25). But the last expression, when used by Clarke (and others), means "when all the Gentiles finally get [converted ]{.class_s4N4-0}and bring in the Kingdom [without the Lord coming back."]{.class_s4N4-0} This is the Postmillennial fiasco, commented on at length in [The Sure Word of Prophecy, ]{.class_s4N4-0}and the reader will notice that the "promises" are to the twelve tribes (Num. 24:9) and the spiritual seed of Isaac (Gal. 3:6--12). Arabians (Gal. 4:25) and Turks (Col. 3:11) are found nowhere in the "promises" where those promises deal with the [political kingdom ]{.class_s4N4-0}of Israel established [in the land ]{.class_s4N4-0}of Palestine. Clarke, as most Postmillennialists, has []{#cCG.xhtml_page_72}spiritualized Romans 11:25 to mean "spiritual blessings" instead of "total destruction" (Jer. 30:11); then he has spiritualized the promises (Gen. 12:1--3) to mean "salvation"; and finally, he made "gathering in" refer to universal salvation, where the "gathering" (Zeph. 3:8) is plainly for BURNING (Matt. 13:40). Typical Conservative scholarship.

["And Moses hid his face" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 6), as Elijah (1 Kings 19:13) and the seraphim (Isa. 6:2). Liberal commentators assume that Moses had a hallucination (see [Commentary on Acts, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Acts 9:3--6 and comments), or else he saw a flying saucer, or someone hooked a P.A. system up to the bush and spoke through a microphone back in a cave somewhere (or something like that). Nearly all "Modernists" assume that modern man is very, very smart, and that the ancients were very, very stupid (see [Commentary on Acts, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Acts 8:9--11 and comments). This conceit is universal among any group of Bible critics.

Moses is seeing a Theophany (an appearance of God), and he has every right in the world to hide his face (Gen. 3:8). He would have been justified if he had dived into a slit trench (Judg. 13:22).

The apostate [imitation ]{.class_s4N4-0}of Moses' covered face is the cowl and hood of the Pope's flunkies. They are not aware of the fact that since the ascension (2 Cor. 3:13--18), we look straight into God's face at all times (2 Cor. 4:3--6). No Halloween costume is necessary when gazing at the bloody cross, the open tomb, the upper room, the Mount of Olives, the rent veil, the empty place over the north, or the stretched-out wings of the cherubim. By Jesus, we have ["access"]{.class_s4N2-0} to God (Eph. 2:18), and we are ["accepted in the beloved"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Eph. 1:6) so that we may enter ["boldly unto the throne of grace"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Heb. 4:16).

The Roman hood and veil are the theatrical props of a Madison-Avenue production. They create an aura of mystery and anticipation, which carries with it the spooky feeling of: "I cannot approach God unless I put on the priestly robes, and since I would feel like a monkey (or a monk?) dressed up that way, I'll let the priest do it for me!" (Or as another has so aptly said: "I thought that monsters lived in monasteries.")

["For he was afraid to look upon God"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 6). One senses the gap between Moses and John the apostle, who not only "looked upon God," but touched and held Him (see 1 John 1:1--2 and John 13:25).

3:7 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said, I have surely seen the affliction of my people which are in Egypt, and have heard their cry by reason of their taskmasters; for I know their sorrows;

8 And I am come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians, and to bring them up out of that land unto a good land and a large, unto a land flowing with milk and honey; unto the place of the Canaanites, and the Hittites, and the Amorites, and the Perizzites, and the Hivites, and the Jebusites.

9 Now therefore, behold, the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me: and I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them.

10 Come now therefore, and I will send thee unto Pharaoh, that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt."

Now, Moses is properly commissioned for one of the greatest tasks in history. He has been called of God to take a church out of the National Council of Churches (Ecumenical World Council) against the will []{#cCG.xhtml_page_74}of the president, vice-president, chairman, moderator, secretary, and treasurer of that organization. This "pastorate" has over 1,000,000 on the roll, and not one of them is making the equivalent of \$30.00 a week! (It is called ["church"]{.class_s4N2-0} in Acts 7:38, which see.) He has been called to lead this congregation into a rocky desert infested with scorpions and serpents, and supply them with food and water out of nowhere. Truly a unique calling!

["And the ]{.class_s4N2-1}[Lord ]{.class_s4N8-1}[said, I have surely seen\...And I am come down to deliver\...Come now therefore, and I will send thee unto Pharaoh" ]{.class_s4N2-1}[(vs. 7--10). We have already commented on verse 7 under 2:23--25.]{.class_s4NF-0}

["And to bring them up out of that land unto a good land and a large"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 8). There is a description of this land found in Deuteronomy 8:7. It was not a "large land" compared to European countries, for it was about 10,000 square miles---about the size of Belgium. However, the original land grant (Gen. 15:18--21) was around 60,000 square miles, and the context of Genesis chapter 15 is the servitude in Egypt (Gen. 15:13--14).

Six nations are mentioned (vs. 8) out of the ten mentioned in Genesis 15. The post-Christian [Septuagint ]{.class_s4N4-0}has added ["Girgashites"]{.class_s4N4-0} to the list, hoping to save God the embarrassment of contradicting Himself. But the addition means nothing; for the Rephaims, Kenites, Kenizzites, and Kadmonites are still missing from the list, and the word "[Hivites]{.class_s4N2-0}" has been [added]{.class_s4N4-0}. There has been a population shift since Genesis chapter 15, and this would certainly be expected in a period of 400 years. The Kenizzites, Kenites, and Kadmonites are obviously in bondage to the Canaanites. The Rephaims (giants) []{#cCG.xhtml_page_75}are included among the Amorites, which is apparent by Joshua chapters 10, 11, 13, and 24.

["Unto a land flowing with milk and honey" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 8) is a form of speech known as a synecdoche; it is a proverbial expression that occurs many times in the Old Testament (Lev. 20:24; Num. 13:27; Deut. 31:20; Jer. 11:5; etc.).

Verse 9 has been commented on under 2:23--25, which see.

["Come now therefore, and I will send thee\...that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 10). Here, then, is a clear commission. The same commission (spiritually) is given to the Lord Jesus. He was sent by God (John 17:4) to bring forth His people (John 17:14) out of the world (John 17:24). However, the response of the Lord Jesus to His commission and calling (Heb. 10:7) is a "sight different" than the response of Moses!

3:11 "And Moses said unto God, Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh, and that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt?

12 And he said, Certainly I will be with thee; and this shall be a token unto thee, that I have sent thee: When thou hast brought forth the people out of Egypt, ye shall serve God upon this mountain.

13 And Moses said unto God, Behold, when I come unto the children of Israel, and shall say unto them, The God of your fathers hath sent me unto you; and they shall say to me, What is his name? what shall I say unto them?

14 And God said unto Moses, I AM THAT I AM: and he said, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, I AM hath sent me unto you.

15 And God said moreover unto Moses, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, The [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, hath sent me unto you: this is my name for ever, and this is my memorial unto all generations.

16 Go, and gather the elders of Israel together, and say unto them, The [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, of Isaac, and of Jacob, appeared unto me, saying, I have surely visited you, and seen that which is done to you in Egypt:

17 And I have said, I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt unto the land of the Canaanites, and the Hittites, and the Amorites, and the Perizzites, and the Hivites, and the Jebusites, unto a land flowing with milk and honey.

18 And they shall hearken to thy voice: and thou shalt come, thou and the elders of Israel, unto the king of Egypt, and ye shall say unto him, The [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}God of the Hebrews hath met with us: and now let us go, we beseech thee, three days' journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God."

In spite of eighty years of preparation for the ministry (1 Tim. 3:6!), Moses is not ready to answer the call. It is a characteristic of human nature---especially in a human being subject to strong feelings---that we first presume and then despair (Num. 16---17 cf. 1 Sam. 12:16--19). First, it is Uzzah trying to help God move His ark (2 Sam. 6:7), and then everyone is so "tore up," they []{#cCG.xhtml_page_77}leave the ark sitting (2 Sam. 6:9, 11). Moses feels his calling (Exod. 2:12) and acts on it in a hotheaded fashion, which was plainly the outworking of the flesh (Gal. 5:21). Having become demoralized (2:15), he has not merely "taken a break" from his calling, he has quit the whole mess altogether. Now having renounced his people and his destiny as their deliverer (Acts 7:35), Moses is in no mood for someone to come along and say, ["You've]{.class_s4N4-0} been called to deliver Israel!" The fact that God is doing the speaking means no more to Moses than it did to Simon Peter (see Acts 10:14). Moses has had forty years to take stock of his aptitudes, potentialities, liabilities, and assets. He has reached an inflexible and fixed opinion: "I wasn't never called to do nothin' in Egypt!"

["Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh?]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 11). Like Gideon (Judg. 6:13, 15), Moses pleads his case. As most men in this position, Moses has forgotten two things: 1 Corinthians 1:28--29 and the words of Exodus 3:8 (["I am come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians"]{.class_s4N2-0}). God calls no man to the ministry who does not realize his own insignificance and who does not recognize the fact that ["all power\...in heaven and earth" ]{.class_s4N2-0}belongs to the Divine Deliverer (Matt. 28:18) ---not the "vessel of clay" (2 Cor. 4:7). Moses has learned [humility]{.class_s4N4-0}; now he must be taught to have faith in God.

["And he said, Certainly I will be with thee\...ye shall serve God upon this mountain"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 12). God has looked upon His people as He looked on Hagar (Gen. 21:17), and as "[a man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief]{.class_s4N2-0}" (Isa. 53:3), He Himself will go with Moses and accomplish the mission. "Serving God on the mountain" is an added revelation, for the original order []{#cCG.xhtml_page_78}was to go from Egypt to Canaan. God is telling Moses that he can believe the full commission, and to prove it, he will live to see Israel worshipping God right there where the bush is burning.

["And Moses said unto God, Behold, when I come unto the children of Israel\...they shall say to me, What is his name?" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 13). Now Moses is thinking up objections. That his mind is working on the subject is apparent by the fact that the most natural objection to bring up next would have been the one found in Exodus 4:10. It may have been that the Israelites were so absorbed with the gods of Egypt that they needed a name for God (notice: Kneph, Khem, Hathor, Osiris, Horus, Pasht, Thoth, Taouris, Set, Isis, Anubis, Sobek, et al.). But even if this were so, Moses would have known God's name already if he had written the book of Genesis during these forty years in the Sinaitic desert. Furthermore, it is impossible that Moses' mother would have failed to give him a definite name for the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (see comments under 2:9--10). Moses is probably acquainted with "[Jehovah-jireh]{.class_s4N2-0}" (Gen. 22:14), "Elohim" (Gen. 1:1), "Elyon" (Gen. 14:18), "Adonai" (Gen. 15:2, 8), and "Jehovah" (Gen. 2:4--5, 7--8). If Moses' question is in sincerity, he is asking for one specific name to use in contrast to the multiplicity with which he is acquainted. He is not going to take a chance on Exodus 2:14 happening again! However, from what follows, the question appears to be a "dodge."

["And God said unto Moses, ]{.class_s4N2-0}[I AM THAT I AM\...I AM]{.class_s4NE-0}[ hath sent me unto you"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 14). The [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) has chosen block capital letters here, as they have in Exodus 28:36, Revelation 17:5, and several other places. The name is one of the greatest revelations in the []{#cCG.xhtml_page_79}history of man, and [it is the only definition existing in all of the world's religious literature]{.class_s4N4-0}---often miscalled "scriptures"---which accurately defines God. If God is a Spirit, a Creator, an Absolute Sovereign, and an Ultimate Being of perfect holiness and power, the only conclusive definition that one could give for Him would be ["I AM."]{.class_s4N2-0} Any and all attempts to define God in other terms dissolves into incoherency.

Witness, for example, this super specimen from the archives of the University of Chicago: "God is the absolute whatness of the unmoved mover." Or Columbia University: "God is the thinghood of the whatness." Why not: "God is the whatness of the howness before the wherefore of the whoness becomes whyness"?

Since the [Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611) and its translators have, here, grasped the absolute essence of definition in their work, we should not be surprised to find the "qualified authorities," among the "majority of Conservative scholars," running for their rifles and machine guns.

1\. [Geddes:]{.class_s4N4-0} "I will be that I will be" (Moffatt's reading also).

2\. [Boothroyd:]{.class_s4N4-0} "I am because I am."

3\. [Septuagint:]{.class_s4N4-0} "I am the one being." (Ditto Catholic Commentary.)

4\. [Vulgate:]{.class_s4N4-0} "I am who I am," which matches the [RSV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1952), since that version, as the [NASV]{.class_s4N4-0}, is just Jerome's Latin[ Vulgate]{.class_s4N4-0} in disguise.

5\. [Meek: ]{.class_s4N4-0}"I cause to be what comes to pass."

Anything but the text.

The text of the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) towers above its critics like the Empire State Building towers over a Swahili hut.

"I AM THAT I AM."

"I am simply because I exist and always have."

"I am that which I am, and there is no explanation for it."

"I am the Way, the Truth, the Life, the Door, the Comforter, etc."

"I am and always 'I am' without any reference to past, present, or future."

When Jesus says ["I am," ]{.class_s4N2-0}His persecutors fall backward (John 18:6--8). When the Pharisees heard Him say ["I am" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(John 8:58--59), they knew what He professed to be; they never made the mistake of Pastor Russell and Judge Rutherford, who thought the text (John 8:58) said "I WAS."

No Buddhist or Brahmist ever defined the absolute essence of God any better, and no Greek philosopher could improve on it. The attempt of the Hebraists (and Moffatt) to force God into a time setting by saying "I WILL BE" is quite characteristic of that type of destructive criticism which cannot lay ahold of the simplest truths of revelation. "I will be" is a statement on what God intends to be in the [future]{.class_s4N4-0}. It has nothing to do with an absolute definition describing the proper name for an Eternal [presence]{.class_s4N4-0}. The proper name is ["I AM THAT I AM,"]{.class_s4N2-0} and no alteration of those five words is an improvement on the text.

As Williams has remarked, ["I AM" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is given as a blank check to Israel. Whatever they needed they could write in. If they needed power---"I AM power" (Matt. 28:18). If they needed comfort---"I AM comfort" (John 14:16). If they needed healing---"I AM the healer" (Exod. 15:26), etc. Any definition of an Eternal Being beyond ["I AM THAT I AM" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is beyond the powers of comprehension, for the very thought of an eternal, [uncreated]{.class_s4N4-0}, []{#cCG.xhtml_page_81}omnipresent Being staggers the mind of man; so much so that Darwin, Marx, Huxley, Haeckel, Engels, Kerensky, Lenin, Freud, the CFR, and the NEA (with the SDA, SDS, and HHS!) have simply rejected the thought---[as much as possible.]{.class_s4N4-0}

We may define the attributes of God by negation; we notice what He is NOT. We may define the attributes of God by observing His creation and then reasoning backwards. We may prove the existence of God by ontological, teleological, and ethnological arguments, or by using Kant's "Categorical Imperative"; but when we are forced to define Him in [absolute ]{.class_s4N4-0}terms, we find ourselves facing what Gautama Buddha (563--483 B.C.) faced: "He who knows does not [say]{.class_s4N4-0}, and he who [says ]{.class_s4N4-0}does not know." We learn more about God's nature from the revelation of the Bible than any other thirty sources combined, and of course the Christian experiences (personally) fellowship with God. Even with all this, the words [God]{.class_s4N4-0} and [Lord ]{.class_s4N4-0}can be applied to the Devil (2 Cor. 4:4) or human masters (Gen. 33:13--15), and even the words [Jesus]{.class_s4N4-0} and [Christ]{.class_s4N4-0} refer to specific functions or offices within the Godhead: Jesus---"Jehovah Saves"; and Christ---"Anointed One" or "Messiah."

When all is said and done, the nearest that anyone can get to an absolute definition is: ["I AM THAT I AM." ]{.class_s4N2-0}These words were spoken on the backside of an Arabian desert more than 500 years [before ]{.class_s4N4-0}the [Rigveda]{.class_s4N4-0}, the [Samaveda]{.class_s4N4-0}, the [Yajurveda]{.class_s4N4-0}, and the [Atharvaveda ]{.class_s4N4-0}were written. What the [Zend Avesta ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Parsees and Zoroastrians) and the [Tripitaka ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Buddhism) and the [Koran ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Mohammedanism) say about God's attributes (or His nature) adds nothing to the revelation of Exodus. Nothing in any of these works would indicate that the []{#cCG.xhtml_page_82}writer was able to come even this close in narrowing down an absolute definition.

["This is my name for ever, and this is my memorial unto all generations"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 15). The reader will observe that [Jesus ]{.class_s4N4-0}is "[a name which is above every name," ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Phil 2:9) "[not only in this world, but also in that which is to come"]{.class_s4N2-0} (see [Commentary on Galtians-Colossians, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Eph. 1:21 and comments). He is the "I AM of I AMS" (Rev. 19:16). Charles Taze Russell and Judge Rutherford were grossly in error in assuming that the name [Jesus]{.class_s4N4-0} was a second fiddle to the Tetragrammaton. The New Testament and the Old Testament equate the Lord Jesus with the Jehovah of the passage before us in Zechariah 12:10; Isaiah 9:6; and Colossians 1:2--6.

"All hail the power of Jesus' name! Let angels prostrate fall!

Bring forth the royal diadem, and crown HIM Lord of all!"

["I have surely visited you, and see\...and I have said, I will bring you up"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 16--17). Here, ["the elders" ]{.class_s4N2-0}are mentioned for the first time, and their number is given in Exodus 24:1 as ["seventy." ]{.class_s4N2-0}The [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0} conjectures that this number was arrived at by adding Nadab and Abihu (Aaron's sons) to the number "[seventy]{.class_s4N2-0}," thus making 72, which would represent six elders from each tribe. However, all of this smacks of the infamous ["Septuagint" ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Gk. "seventy"), which was supposedly written by 72 men, six from each tribe, according to the phony [Letter to Aristeas]{.class_s4N4-0} (see [Manuscript Evidence, ]{.class_s4N4-0}"The Mythological [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}"). The [Septuagint ]{.class_s4N4-0}was not written by seventy Jews, nor was []{#cCG.xhtml_page_83}it written by 72 Jews, nor was it written before A.D. 100, nor did any Bible-believing apostle have any more respect for it than for Plato's [Republic]{.class_s4N4-0} or Aristotle's [Golden Mean. ]{.class_s4N4-0}The [Septuagint ]{.class_s4N4-0}was compiled by Origen (A.D. 185--254), and his "original" was added to the New Testament by the writers of [Sinaiticus]{.class_s4N4-0}, [Vaticanus]{.class_s4N4-0}, and Papyrus 66 and 75.

"[The elders of Israel" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 16) are not given as "[seventy]{.class_s4N2-0}" in Exodus chapter 3, although we may assume that they are the same number given in Exodus 24:1. The number "[seventy]{.class_s4N2-0}" is plainly a number chosen for a definite reason, and this reason is clearly given in the [Authorized Version. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Observe, now, how the "King's English" of Exodus 1:5 solves the problem without Nadab, Abihu, Aristeas, Alexandria, [Vaticanus]{.class_s4N4-0}, or the [Pulpit Commentary. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Seventy elders are chosen, in Egypt, to represent the original "[seventy]{.class_s4N2-0}" who came into Egypt. (See lengthy discussion of the corrupt [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0} in the [Commentary on Genesis,]{.class_s4N4-0} Gen. 46:27.)

The Lord has ["visited," "seen," ]{.class_s4N2-0}and[ "said,"]{.class_s4N2-0} and now He will ["bring"]{.class_s4N2-0} Israel up from Africa and destroy the Africans in Palestine who are "out of bounds" (Deut. 2:21, 23, 32:8; Acts 17:26) in the "[land flowing with milk and honey]{.class_s4N2-0}." "[The iniquity of the Amorites" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is now full: the cup is overflowing (Gen. 15:16). The hourglass is empty, and now it must be turned over, for "[it is high time"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Rom. 13:11). Palestine is filled with a half-breed mongrel race of Hamites whose leaders are giants (Deut. 2:11--22) and whose religion is the Baal-Ashtoreth (male-female), love-peace (sex-compromise) fertility cult of homosexuals (1 Kings 14:24). That is, the condition of Palestine at the time of this calling (1500--[]{#cCG.xhtml_page_84}1450 B.C.) was the condition of the United States in A.D. 1977--2010.

["And they shall hearken to thy voice" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 18). This is fulfilled in Exodus 4:31. ["Ye shall say unto him" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 18). What follows is fulfilled in Exodus 5:1--3. The reader will observe that "sacrifices" are taken for granted by Moses, Pharaoh, the elders of Israel, the Egyptians, and the Lord Himself.

The comment in the [Wycliffe Commentary ]{.class_s4N4-0}to the effect that the expression ["three days"]{.class_s4N2-0} is not to be taken [literally, ]{.class_s4N4-0}because it only stands for a "current expression of considerable distance" comes from the same type of infidelity that calls the Millennium (Rev. 20:1--6) an "indefinite period of time." (Many "Conservatives" are downright atheistic in their approach to certain passages.) The words of Exodus 3:18 are true, and it is a bonafide proposition which is offered to Pharaoh, in spite of the additional material in verse 17.

It is ["]{.class_s4N2-0}[eleven days]{.class_s4N4-0}["]{.class_s4N2-0} from Horeb to Kadesh-barnea (Deut. 1:2), if [a long detour is made around Mount Seir]{.class_s4N4-0} (Deut. 1:2). In a [straight line,]{.class_s4N4-0} this distance would have been called an eight-day journey, and one can plainly see that the distance from Horeb to Kadesh-barnea (in a straight line) is longer than the distance from the Red Sea crossing to Horeb by at least four days. (See comments under 15:22 and 19:1.)

If Pharaoh had accepted the offer, Moses would have been obligated to take the seventy elders (vs. 18) and Aaron and go three days into the wilderness and return;[ this would not have taken them to Sinai. ]{.class_s4N4-0}However, the Lord knows what Pharaoh will do (Exod. 3:19), and He is perfectly safe in adding that the whole nation of Israel will leave Egypt and journey to Canaan.

3:19 "And I am sure that the king of Egypt will not let you go, no, not by a mighty hand.

20 And I will stretch out my hand, and smite Egypt with all my wonders which I will do in the midst thereof: and after that he will let you go.

21 And I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians: and it shall come to pass, that, when ye go, ye shall not go empty:

22 But every woman shall borrow of her neighbour, and of her that sojourneth in her house, jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment: and ye shall put them upon your sons, and upon your daughters; and ye shall spoil the Egyptians."

The reading of the corrupt [RSV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1952) is to be rejected at verse 19. The wording of the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) is much better, and the serious student of Scripture will note the contrast with what follows. ["And I am sure\...no, not by a mighty hand" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 19), and then, ["And I will stretch forth MY hand\...and after that he will let you go"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 20). To equate the ["hand"]{.class_s4N2-0} of verse 19 with the ["hand"]{.class_s4N2-0} of verse 20 destroys the power and authority of God's hand when it does move (Deut. 6:21, 7:8). Notice how the [RSV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1952) reading destroys the entire sense by suggesting this sequence: "Pharaoh will not let you go unless a mighty hand does it, and I will stretch forth a mighty hand, and he will do it!" The [RSV ]{.class_s4N4-0}reading is, of course, the Roman Catholic reading of Jerome's Latin [Vulgate ]{.class_s4N4-0}which was copied from the "[LXX]{.class_s4N4-0}" of A.D. 100--300.

If "unless" or "except" is used in verse 19, the following thought would have to be: "BUT I WILL stretch forth, etc." In the correct reading, preserved by the Holy []{#cCG.xhtml_page_86}Spirit, we find the thought: "he will not let you go by a mighty hand, but I have a hand that is stronger than the mightiest hand, and when I stretch forth MY hand, he'll move!" The reader should notice that the word ["mighty"]{.class_s4N2-0} refers to [men ]{.class_s4N4-0}as well as [God]{.class_s4N4-0}, and the Lord's mention of the ["mighty hand"]{.class_s4N2-0} in verse 19 is not a reference to the [divine hand]{.class_s4N4-0} (see Gen. 10:9; Judg. 5:23; 2 Sam. 1:19; 1 Chron. 11:10--14; Job 6:23; etc.).

Zechariah 12:7 points out the interpretation clearly. God Himself is going to get the glory out of Pharaoh's destruction (Rom. 9:17), and He will deliver Israel by His outstretched hand, not the "[mighty hand]{.class_s4N2-0}" of Moses and Aaron.

Verses 21 and 22 are fulfilled literally in Exodus 12:35. We should observe the parallelism between the exodus of Israel, here, and the exodus of Jacob in Genesis chapter 31. In both cases, the Jews spoil the Gentiles before returning to the Promised Land. This is the source of the Twentieth-Century grievances which one finds aired in such publications as[ "The Plain Truth," "The Plot to Destroy America," "The World Conspiracy," "The International Bankers," "The Zionist Plot," "International Jewry," "Esau and the Khazars," ]{.class_s4N4-0}etc.

["But every woman shall borrow of her neighbour\...and ye shall spoil the Egyptians"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 22). Bullinger (agreeing with Philo, Clement, Origen, and Pantaenus) calls the word ["borrow"]{.class_s4N2-0} a "most unfortunate rendering." (This is S.O.P. with dead-orthodox theologians who get involved in defensive warfare instead of offensive warfare. Paul said "[FIGHT" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(1 Cor. 9:26; 1 Tim. 6:12; 2 Tim. 4:7); he did not say "hold the fort." Many a scholar needs the []{#cCG.xhtml_page_87}advice which Von Clausewitz gave: "As a commander, you should be reminded that your duty is to destroy the enemy, not merely 'maintain the peace'.") People like Machen, Origen, Robertson, Davis, Moulton, Milligan, Trench, Vincent, Driver, Keil, Delitzsch, Bullinger, Clarke, Tischendorf, and Thieme are always running into "unfortunate renderings" in the [Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611) because:

1\. They don't know how to [answer ]{.class_s4N4-0}its critics.

2\. They don't [believe ]{.class_s4N4-0}they are dealing with the Holy Spirit.

3\. They have assumed that they have the authority to correct the word of God (see [Manuscript Evidence]{.class_s4N4-0}).

4\. They are [scared to death]{.class_s4N4-0} when an agnostic or an infidel attacks the Bible.

5\. They desire to help God out and prove to agnostics and infidels that the Bible was written not only by the Holy Spirit, but by nice, respectable, "godly" people like [themselves]{.class_s4N4-0}.

Clarke's commentary helps us track down the culprit. Readers of the [Commentary on Proverbs]{.class_s4N4-0} do not have to guess where the tracks will end; the [Septuagint ]{.class_s4N4-0}says ["aitasei" ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Gk.) for ["borrow."]{.class_s4N2-0} Clarke suggests we adopt the Roman Catholic [Vulgate ]{.class_s4N4-0}reading (["demand"]{.class_s4N4-0}) in place of ["borrow." ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Anything but the text.]{.class_s4N4-0}

Isn't it a pity that we must adopt the [RSV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1952) and the [NASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1963) reading here ("[ask]{.class_s4N4-0}"), when we know how these "Bibles" feel about the Deity of the Lord Jesus Christ (Luke 2:33, 23:42, 24:51--52; John 1:18; and 1 Tim. 3:16)? I wonder what could have possessed the poor, deluded translators of the "archaic" [Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611) to write[ "borrow" ]{.class_s4N2-0}for "[ask]{.class_s4N4-0}"? Didn't they know the word[ "shaal" ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Heb.) should be translated "[ask]{.class_s4N4-0}"?

Well, it is pretty evident that the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}translators knew everything that the[ Vulgate, Septuagint, ASV, NASV, NIV, ]{.class_s4N4-0}or [RSV ]{.class_s4N4-0}knew about the word, for they translated it as "[ask]{.class_s4N2-0}" 87 times. However, if one will read the preface of the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) carefully, one will find that the translators sought God's help, prayerfully, in translating words, and did not use a uniform translation every time. The Hebrew word has been translated as "[beg]{.class_s4N2-0}" once, "[demand]{.class_s4N2-0}" four times, "[desire]{.class_s4N2-0}" nine times, "[enquire]{.class_s4N2-0}" 22 times, "[wish]{.class_s4N2-0}" once, etc., etc. The insistence, then, on the part of the hypercritical gnat-strainers that the word MUST BE "[ask]{.class_s4N4-0}" is childish and stupid. The careful student of Scripture should not be seduced by the trivial remarks of Williams, Trench, Thayer, Clarke, Lange, Ellicott, Cook, Schaff, Johnson, Thieme, Wuest, Frankenberg, DeWette, Kalisch, Stahelin, Raschi, and Hengstenberg about the expression "[borrow]{.class_s4N2-0}"; for he will have already noticed that ["shaal"]{.class_s4N4-0} is capable of meaning:

1\. To interrogate

2\. To inquire

3\. To ask

4\. To petition

5\. To request

6\. To beg

7\. TO ASK AS A LOAN: [TO BORROW]{.class_s4N4-0} (see 1 Sam. 2:20).

That is, the "majority of accredited scholars" have simply lied to the student of the Bible and have sought to destroy his faith in the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) by deliberately misrepresenting the truth. (The definitions above will be found on page 694 of the [Analytical Hebrew and Chaldee Lexicon, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Samuel Bagsters, London, 1967.)

Having cleared the ground of scrub pine and scrub oak, we should ask ourselves this question: "If the word ['shaal' ]{.class_s4N4-0}can mean any number of things, why did the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}translators choose [']{.class_s4N4-0}[borrow]{.class_s4NC}['?" ]{.class_s4N4-0}Evidently, the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}translators saw a meaning in the word that Origen, Eusebius, Keil, Philo, Delitzsch, Jerome, Robertson, Celsus, Porphyry, Schaff, Altizer, Schweitzer, Gesenius, Dr. Green, and Dean Weigel missed.

Now, the term "[borrow]{.class_s4N2-0}" implies a temporary loan which later will be repaid. It is clear that the Egyptians gladly "loan" the required articles (Exod. 11:2, 12:35), but it is not so clear (to Origen, Eusebius, et al.) as to when these articles were paid back. But the Bible believer who has access to the hidden wisdom of the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611)---concealed from the committees of the [NIV, ASV, NASV,]{.class_s4N4-0} etc.---observes that Israel pays back the loan in full.

1\. Deuteronomy 28:68---they served again.

2\. Jeremiah 44:26---they served again.

3\. Hosea 12:1---they paid some revenue back.

4\. Second Chronicles 12:9---they paid the gold back.

You see, it was a LOAN that was BORROWED. The [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) text is correct, and every Bible on the market that changed the text was evidently written by a shallow, superficial Bible exegete who could neither understand nor exposit the words of truth.

Moral: where the majority of Conservative [scholars]{.class_s4N4-0} and the majority of Conservative [commentators]{.class_s4N4-0} and the majority of [translations ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [versions]{.class_s4N4-0} and the majority of Greek [linguists ]{.class_s4N4-0}alter the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}text, discard them without a second thought.

[]{#cGM.xhtml}

# Chapter 4 {.heading_s4G-2}

4:1 "And Moses answered and said, But, behold, they will not believe me, nor hearken unto my voice: for they will say, The [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}hath not appeared unto thee.

2 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto him, What is that in thine hand? And he said, A rod.

3 And he said, Cast it on the ground. And he cast it on the ground, and it became a serpent; and Moses fled from before it.

4 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Put forth thine hand, and take it by the tail. And he put forth his hand, and caught it, and it became a rod in his hand:

5 That they may believe that the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}God of their fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, hath appeared unto thee."

Moses is not through objecting (see comments under 3:11). He, like the Lord Jesus (Phil. 2:7), has become a man of ["no reputation"]{.class_s4N2-0}; he is not about to go down and get "involved in a personal confrontation" (Twentieth-Century cliché) with the King of Egypt.

["But, behold, they will not believe me, nor hearken unto my voice" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 1). God seems to have a hard time every time He calls a man into the ministry. Gideon, like Moses, feels too small to take on 135,000 Midianites (cf. Judg. 8:10); Saul is hiding "[among the stuff]{.class_s4N2-0}" (1 Sam. 10:22); Jonah wants to run west instead of east (Jon. 1); Peter is crying out, ["Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N8-2}[!" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Luke 5:8)[;]{.class_s4N2-0} and Jephthah is not about to do anything until he gets a blank check (Judg 11:9). Very few men step into the pulpit like Matthew (Matt. 9:9), and the "call" is nearly always accompanied by ["suffer me first to go and\..."]{.class_s4N2-0} (Luke []{#cGM.xhtml_page_91}9:59). One can almost hear the unrecorded "dialogue" between Noah and the Lord back in Genesis 6.

"That's right; build it, and build it that size."

"But Lord, that's three times the length of a football field, and it's square!"

"You mean [rectangular?"]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Yes, but how in the world will I get ahold of all them animals?"

"You don't do it; [I'll do it."]{.class_s4N4-0} (See Exod. 3:8!)

"But God, I'm a preacher, not a carpenter!"

"You got three boys, and one of them is a master, and one is a journeyman."

"But Lord, if I start to build this thing right out in\.... "

"You wanna drown or stay alive?"

The Bible is not speaking facetiously when it notes that "[man is born like a wild ass's colt]{.class_s4N2-0}" (Job 11:12). Do you reckon that Moses was any kin to Thomas Didymus?

["For they will say, The ]{.class_s4N2-1}[Lord ]{.class_s4N8-1}[hath not appeared unto thee."]{.class_s4N2-1}[ How this reminds one of the scene that takes place in Luke 24:11. How vividly it describes the attitude of Professor Charles Guignebert, Eugene Carson Blake, Harry Emerson Fosdick, Dr. Joseph Klausner, Bishop Ralph W. Sockman, Kirsopp Lake, Dr. Gardiner-Smith, Ernest Renan, Strauss, Astruc, Canon Burnet Streeter, F. J. Jackson, and Adolf Harnack.]{.class_s4N3-0}

Paul's testimony stood or fell on whether or not ["the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N8-2}[hath\...appeared unto thee" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(see [Commentary on Acts, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Acts 9:17). If Paul lied about the matter, every Christian who breathed air from A.D. 34 to A.D. 2010 was deceived, "[dead in trespasses and sins]{.class_s4N2-0}" (Eph. 2:1), and a purveyor of falsehood (1 Cor. 15:12--20).

The [LXX ]{.class_s4N4-0}(supported by Geddes and Boothroyd) has altered the verse considerably, and has stricken out eleven words of the text which the Holy Spirit has preserved for us in the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611). (The corrupt LXX, from which the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}\[1901\] and the [NASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}\[1963\] took their texts, was written between A.D. 150 and A.D. 550. The student should never confuse these documents with the mythological "[LXX]{.class_s4N4-0}" of "pre-Christian" times; [no such book ever existed.)]{.class_s4N4-0}

["And the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N8-2}[said unto him, What is that in thine hand?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 2). God will use what a man has (2 Cor. 8:12), so he can usually "abide in the calling wherein he was called" (1 Cor. 7:24), in a sense. Peter swaps one net for another (Luke 5:10). Matthew keeps on dipping his pen in ink, but instead of serving as an Internal Revenue agent for Caesar, he serves as an inspired recorder for God the Holy Spirit. Samson is caught with the jawbone of an ass in his hand, but he does as well with it as Audie Murphy did with a 50-caliber machine gun (1945). David becomes shepherd of Israel in exchange for shepherding one of Jesse's flocks (1 Sam. 16:11). And Amos goes right on shaking trees (Amos 7:14) to get fruit; he just swapped [sycamores for oaks.]{.class_s4N4-0}

["And he said, A rod" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 2). Henceforth, this is referred to as ["the rod of God"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Exod. 4:20). This rod passes on to Aaron (Exod. 7:9--10), but Moses uses it on occasion (Exod. 10:13).

["And he said, Cast it on the ground\...it became a serpent; and Moses fled from before it"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3).

The rod of [Moses ]{.class_s4N6}represents Christ as PROPHET (Deut. 18:15).

The rod of [Aaron ]{.class_s4N6}represents Christ as PRIEST (Num. 17:8).

The rod of [David ]{.class_s4N4-0}represents Christ as KING (Psa. 2:9).

The rod here (Heb. ["matteh"]{.class_s4N4-0}) probably is not the shepherd's staff of Psalm 23:4, in spite of the unanimity of opinion on the subject---see any commentary. The "heathen," of course, adopt the "rod" in all circumstances. Magicians carry their "wands"; officers carry their "riding crops"; Romans carry the "rods" in the fasces; teachers use a "pointer"; Hell's Angels (California, 1966--1970) use a billiard cue stick; and symphony conductors wave the "baton."
