Moses' rod is a shepherd's "rod," but a shepherd's rod is often a wooden mace cut from knotted roots and, quite often, studded with nails. When David caught the beast ["by]{.class_s4N2-0}[ ]{.class_s4N4-0}[his beard, and smote him" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(1 Sam. 17:35), he was not whacking him with a "staff"; he was beating his brains out with a club. Moses' rod can be a hooked "staff," but no information is given in the text.

["It became a serpent"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3). All of the commentators miss the cross-reference in Ezekiel 29:3. Since all of them missed the reference in Isaiah 27:1 and Job 41:1 (see the [New Scofield Reference Bible,]{.class_s4N4-0} for example), none connected Pharaoh with Satan; the [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0} even goes so far as to say that any connection with Genesis 3:1--4 is "fanciful." To the contrary, it would be "fantasy" to ignore the connection. Pharaoh is plainly a type of the Antichrist (see [Mark of the Beast]{.class_s4N4-0}), and in the Scriptures, he stands for the second head on the ["serpent"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Revelation 12:3 (Nimrod was the first head).

["And it became a serpent; and Moses fled from before it"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3). This is the first miracle in the Bible performed by a human being. However, if you make []{#cGM.xhtml_page_94}"science" your supreme court, the first thing it will do will be to declare that all "miracles" are unconstitutional. You will then take your place among that vast number of superstitious people who believe that "the ancient Hebrews" did not have the "benefit of the scientific inductive method," etc., and therefore, they spoke of natural phenomena as "miracles." This august company of "Christian" infidels never look any better than when demonstrating their defense mechanisms in an effort to explain everything (and anything), they cannot rationally explain.

1\. Christ [swooned ]{.class_s4N4-0}and revived in the tomb.

2\. Peter just thought Malchus' ear was cut off; it was really just cut badly.

3\. Jesus didn't walk on the water; He was walking on [cakes of ice.]{.class_s4N4-0}

4\. Peter and the apostles healed no one; it was the psychological effect of the mass hysteria which accompanied the legend of blah, blah, blah, etc.

5\. The sun did not "stand still" (Josh. 10:12). The day just seemed longer because everyone was so "pooped-out."

6\. The sundial didn't go down (Isa. 38:8); some practical joker moved the center dial over.

7\. Paul was not converted by a post-Resurrection appearance of Jesus Christ; he had a little touch of sunstroke in the middle of an attack of [epilepsy]{.class_s4N4-0}.

8\. At the Crucifixtion, darkness was over the earth (Matt. 27:45) because there was an eclipse.

9\. The Red Sea didn't really part (Exod. 14:21--22); a high tide inundated some marshy ground near the "Bitter Lakes."

10\. Christ didn't curse the fig tree (Mark 11:14, 20); He just took advantage of the disciples' ignorance when He saw the tree was half dead.

But "turnabout is fair play," and if we are to match the "giant intellects" of those "brilliant scientists" who think this way, we should note that:

1\. George Washington never was born; King George III planted a spy in America who assumed the name and built the legend.

2\. The astronauts did not get to the moon; all the photos were made on a Hollywood set.

3\. Martin Luther King Jr. was not assassinated; he hired Rap Brown to shoot him so he could get his name in the paper again.

4\. JFK (Dallas, 1963) was shot by Lyndon Johnson who was disguised as an undercover agent riding in the death car.

5\. Einstein and Pope Paul VI are not real human beings; if the truth were known (Hedda Hopper!), they are MONKEYS disguised as men!

The first miracle performed by man in the Bible is a sign to Israel. Its teaching is crystal clear, in spite of the muddled expositions of the [Wycliffe Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0}, the [Pulpit Commentary,]{.class_s4N4-0} Dummelow, and Williams. The first man-made miracle is a statement of two facts: first, that Israel was a nation of [shepherds ]{.class_s4N4-0}before their bondage, and they could return to that estate again by a miracle of God; secondly, that the man who controls the sheep can defeat the Devil through the [same instrument]{.class_s4N4-0} that he uses for the sheep. The reader will observe that Moses "runs in circles" with the snake chasing him (vs. 3), until he turns and faces it.

["And he put forth his hand, and caught it"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 4). Moses catches this snake ["by the tail"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 4), and yet it is apparent that he would have grabbed its [mouth ]{.class_s4N4-0}if the snake had not "turned tail" and gone in the other direction. The moral lesson is obvious:[ "Resist the devil, and he will flee from you" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(James 4:7). Moses becomes the prototype of the apostolic witness in the Tribulation (Mark 16:17--18), and you can "bet your bottom dollar" that he never laid down to rest again, at night, with that rod beside him that he did not eye it closely before going to sleep (Isa. 10:15 cf. Ezek. 31).

4:6 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said furthermore unto him, Put now thine hand into thy bosom. And he put his hand into his bosom: and when he took it out, behold, his hand was leprous as snow.

7 And he said, Put thine hand into thy bosom again. And he put his hand into his bosom again; and plucked it out of his bosom, and, behold, it was turned again as his other flesh.

8 And it shall come to pass, if they will not believe thee, neither hearken to the voice of the first sign, that they will believe the voice of the latter sign.

9 And it shall come to pass, if they will not believe also these two signs, neither hearken unto thy voice, that thou shalt take of the water of the river, and pour it upon the dry land: and the water which thou takest out of the river shall become blood upon the dry land."

[The Lord has attached the deepest significance to the snake-rod miracle, for He has stated that it was done]{.class_s4N3-0}[ "that they may believe" ]{.class_s4N2-1}[(vs. 5). What follows is "more of the same," and all of it is done to prove that God has []{#cGM.xhtml_page_97}"appeared unto Moses" and that he is not on some ]{.class_s4N3-0}wild-goose chase like the one he was chasing in 2:11--14.

["Put now thine hand into thy bosom\...and when he took it out, behold, his hand was leprous as snow"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 6). The hand and the heart are plainly connected (Mark 7:21--23; Col. 3:22; Prov. 26:15; Eccl. 9:10). (Observe how the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}\[1901\] destroyed the cross-reference to Prov. 26:15 by changing that verse.) What a man carries out with his hands was originally conceived in his heart (Prov. 4:23). The second sign, therefore, is a vivid demonstration of works related to belief; it is also a vivid demonstration of God's power to heal.

A. There are no diseases mentioned in the Bible until Exodus 15:26, and the "sicknesses" of Genesis (Gen. 48:1, for example) are only the debilities of old age; they are not real "diseases." Disease begins in Egypt---a type of this world (see [Commentary on Genesis,]{.class_s4N4-0} Gen. 50).

B. Leprosy is an [African ]{.class_s4N4-0}disease (Exod. 15:26; Gen. 13:1), and where the leper turns white all over (Lev. 13:13), he or she is clean (Gen. 19:26---Lot's wife).

C. Sin can be hidden or exposed (Job 11:13--15, 31:33), and God wants us to pull it out and show it (Psa. 32:5).

D. Israel had been "whole," but they had contracted the sin and disease of the world, and unless their "hearts" were cleansed, their hands would remain stained with the fleshpots of Egypt.

These lessons escape the notice of about 80% of the commentators; most of them deal with the text in terms of broad generalities or vague spiritual applications.

Comparing the text with history, famous men (e.g., Napoleon) have been known to walk around with one hand stuck into their jackets, and the "hand-over-heart" []{#cGM.xhtml_page_98}salute is quite well known in most nations. (That is, the Bible sets the pace; you keep up if you can.)

Few, if any, of the commentators have observed that Moses contracts leprosy four times: once here, once before Aaron (vs. 28), once before the elders (vs. 30), and again before Pharaoh himself (vs. 21: note ["see that thou do all those wonders\...which I have put in thine hand"]{.class_s4N2-0}).

["And\...if they will not believe thee, neither hearken to the voice of the first sign, that they will believe the voice of the latter sign"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 8). Israel's deliverance (and her beginning as a nation) is ushered in with "signs" (1 Cor. 1:22). To qualify as a Divinely appointed deliverer, the Lord Jesus must be given these signs (see [Commentary on Acts, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Acts 2:22), and even His birth is a ["sign"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Isa. 7:11--14) which is spoken against (Luke 2:34). His successors (Mark 16:17--19) must also have ["the signs" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(2 Cor. 12:12) which were given initially to Moses.

["Thou shalt take of the water of the river\...and the water which thou takest out of the river shall become blood upon the dry land"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 9). Moses has picked up some tricks in the last thirty minutes that are going to be hard for the faculty at Alexandria to reduplicate (and one must not forget that Alexandria was in Egypt).

The third sign is one of the greatest revelations in the Bible on retribution, the Deity of Christ, [and the two births;]{.class_s4N4-0} although all of this (except the retribution) escapes the major commentators and 90% of them only speak vaguely of the nature of the retribution. Opening the unsearchable riches of the [Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611) []{#cGM.xhtml_page_99}to gain light on the obscure commentaries and revisions, we discover:

1\. A water birth is a [physical birth,]{.class_s4N4-0} and ["the life" ]{.class_s4N2-0}of the [physical flesh]{.class_s4N4-0} ["is in the blood"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Gen. 1:20; Lev. 17:14; Prov. 5:16; Isa. 48:1).

2\. Spilled-out blood (Lev. 17:13) is similar to spilled-out water (2 Sam. 23:16--17), for a man needs water for [physical life]{.class_s4N4-0} before he needs anything outside of air itself.

3\. Where someone like Pharaoh has spilled blood (Exod. 1--2), retribution is necessary (Gen. 9:4--6).

4\. This blood was "spilled" in water (Exod. 7:20), and in water which people used for drinking water (Rev. 16:5--7); therefore, the water must be converted to blood to make the proper payment (Rev. 19:2).

5\. Christ's blood was Divine blood (Acts 20:28, [AV]{.class_s4N4-0} 1611; ignore all other translations which have attacked His Deity in this verse), but He had a [human nature]{.class_s4N4-0} through Mary (Luke 1:35).

6\. Therefore, [WATER AND BLOOD represent His two natures ]{.class_s4N4-0}(John 1:14), and the "side-piercing" (John 20:34) was so dramatic an event that John says it is connected with believing (1 John 5:6--7).

7\. That is, the WATER AND BLOOD of 1 John 5:6--7 is a witness to two separate births and two separate natures. The context of the entire chapter is BIRTH (1 John 5:1, 4--6).

8\. The famous "Johannine Comma" (1 John 5:7--8), therefore, is for an absolute certainty an inspired text. Its opponents (John R. Rice, Bob Jones University, the [NASV, RV, RSV,]{.class_s4N4-0} Lockman Foundation, Lamsa, Weymouth, Moffatt, Goodspeed, Nestle, Westcott, Hort, Montgomery, []{#cGM.xhtml_page_100 .class-1}[Berkeley]{.class_s4N4-0}, [New English Bible,]{.class_s4N4-0} [Riverside]{.class_s4N4-0}, [Living Letters,]{.class_s4N4-0} Moulton, Custer, Neal, and the [New World Translation]{.class_s4N4-0}) are as out of order as a Rhode Island "Red" picking a fight with a German Shepherd.

The ["water"]{.class_s4N2-0} of 1 John 5:6--8 is no more connected with water baptism than it is with ["the]{.class_s4N2-0} [washing of water by the word"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Eph. 5:26). Commentators of contrary opinion[ "do\...err, because ]{.class_s4N2-0}\[they\][ know not the scriptures, neither the power of God?" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Mark 12:24).

The three who bear witness in Heaven are absolute Deity: note, ["the Word was God"]{.class_s4N2-0} (John 1:1; 1 John 5:7). Of the three who[ "bear witness in earth"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 John 5:8), only TWO are Deity (1 John 5:6)---[the Spirit and the blood]{.class_s4N4-0} (Acts 20:28)[. ]{.class_s4N4-0}["The water"]{.class_s4N2-0} is a plain reference to Christ's earthly birth on this earth like ["the first Adam"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 Cor. 15:45)---who was "earthly" (1 Cor. 15:47). It is the WORD who becomes FLESH (John 1:14), while ["the Father"]{.class_s4N2-0} and ["the Holy Ghost"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 John 5:7) are never ["born of water" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(John 3:5 cf. Gen. 1:20).

[That is, what is designedly obscure in the Satanic "versions of the Bible" and the obtuse commentaries]{.class_s4N4-0} (written by men puffed and bloated with their ability to "help God out")[ are as clear as the streets of New Jerusalem to anyone who can read a King James, 1611, Authorized Version. ]{.class_s4N4-0}When in doubt throw all of the Conservative commentators out.

4:10 "And Moses said unto the[ Lord, ]{.class_s4N8-0}O my Lord, I am not eloquent, neither heretofore, nor since thou hast spoken unto thy servant: but I am slow of speech, and of a slow tongue.

11 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto him, Who hath made man's mouth? or who maketh the dumb, or deaf, or the seeing, or the blind? have not I the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}?

12 Now therefore go, and I will be with thy mouth, and teach thee what thou shalt say.

13 And he said, O my Lord, send, I pray thee, by the hand of him whom thou wilt send.

14 And the anger of the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}was kindled against Moses, and he said, Is not Aaron the Levite thy brother? I know that he can speak well. And also, behold, he cometh forth to meet thee: and when he seeth thee, he will be glad in his heart.

15 And thou shalt speak unto him, and put words in his mouth: and I will be with thy mouth, and with his mouth, and will teach you what ye shall do.

16 And he shall be thy spokesman unto the people: and he shall be, even he shall be to thee instead of a mouth, and thou shalt be to him instead of God.

17 And thou shalt take this rod in thine hand, wherewith thou shalt do signs."

["And Moses said unto the ]{.class_s4N2-2}[Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}[, O my ]{.class_s4N2-2}Lord[, I am not eloquent, neither heretofore, nor since thou hast spoken\...I am slow of speech, and of a slow tongue" ]{.class_s4N2-2}[(vs. 10). This is the third objection (see 3:13 and 4:1). Moses has been assured that God will go with him and be with him. He has been given a revelation of God's name to use as a carte blanche, and finally, he has been given miraculous signs which were denied to Noah, Enoch, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. What more could a minister want?]{.class_s4N3-1}

"But you see, Lord, I never have been a good speaker. I get all shook-up when I stand before a crowd. I know []{#cGM.xhtml_page_102}what I want to say, but I get tongue-tied, and I never could get my grammar right. I split my infinitives and end my sentences with prepositions, and I don't know a gerund from a German, and\...."

"Shut up a minute, Moses."

(Now that is not exactly the "original Hebrew" \[to use the apostate cliché\], but it is much closer to it than the [New English Bible]{.class_s4N4-0} or anything printed since 1880.)

The Lord is "put out" with His servant, which is perfectly apparent from verse 14: ["And the anger of the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[was kindled against Moses\...."]{.class_s4N2-0} The only time this expression occurs in the Bible is when God is ready to kill somebody. Observe Numbers 11:1, 10, 33, 22:22, 25:3; Deut. 6:15, 7:4, 11:17, 31:17; Josh. 7:1, 23:16, etc.).

In Exodus 6:12, the complaint of Moses is mentioned again. Moses' problem seems to be a combination of an impediment in his speech and a lack of ability to express himself in dealing with HOLY things. His conversation with the Lord has not cured the trouble a bit, and at this moment, he is probably as nervous as a cat in a room full of rocking chairs. He states:[ "nor since thou hast spoken unto thy servant."]{.class_s4N2-0} Daniel picked up some strength from a conversation with the Angel of the Lord (Dan. 10:18), but Moses is getting "punier" (early American runic from a stele in Little Rock) by the minute.

"And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N9-1}said unto him, Who hath made man's mouth?\...have not I the [Lord]{.class_s4N9-1}?"[ (vs. 11). Yes, obviously (Job 12:20; Psa. 19:14, 40:3; Matt. 21:16; Mark 7:37; Luke 1:64, etc.). God can make a "dumb" Christian speak up and witness; He can make a "deaf" Christian listen to the commandments and reproofs; and He can make a "blind" Christian see the meaning of Scripture []{#cGM.xhtml_page_103}(Luke 24:45) and the condition of the world around him (Acts 26:18).]{.class_s4N3-2}

When a famous atheistic lecturer was presenting his "religion" at a state school for deaf mutes, he was constantly irritated by the Scriptural responses of the children. Speaking through sign language and writing answers on the blackboard, he finally "lost his cool" (Twentieth-Century cliché) altogether and addressed one ten-year-old girl with this question: "If God is as loving and as powerful as you say He is, then how come He made you like you are?!" The ten-year-old smiled when she understood the question, went to the blackboard, and wrote down: ["Even so, Father: for so it seemed good in thy sight"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Matt. 11:26)!

In the old First Baptist Church of Fort Worth, when J. Frank Norris was the pastor, a homely teenaged girl, with the intellect of a six-year-old, would walk around the building during the invitation. While the choir was singing and the preacher exhorting, she would wander through the aisles and stop every few minutes in front of some man or woman. She would then stretch out her arms, and with tears streaming down her face, she would implore, "You're going to Hell if you don't trust Jesus!" Every Sunday, between five and ten adults received Christ as their Saviour through this simple childlike witness.

["I will be with thy mouth, and teach thee what thou shalt say"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 12) is one of the greatest promises in the Bible that God ever gave to a witnessing servant.

Moses had no real alibi, and neither do any of us. [New converts ]{.class_s4N4-0}cannot plead inexperience---look at the woman of John 4:39; [young people ]{.class_s4N4-0}cannot plead uselessness---look at the lad of John 6:9;[ old people]{.class_s4N4-0} []{#cGM.xhtml_page_104}cannot back out---look at Simeon and Moses (Luke 2:25); not even[ the handicapped ]{.class_s4N4-0}can plead deferment---Paul was handicapped all of his life (2 Cor. 12:7--10).

["And he said, O my ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N7-1}[, send, I pray thee, by the hand of him whom thou wilt send"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 13). The drift of the verse is: "Lord, please send somebody else!" It is worded in the "slow tongue" which Moses professes to have, and it is muddled enough so that it could mean: "Please send ME by the hand of someone else [instead of Yourself!"]{.class_s4N4-0} Either way, it strikes flint to the seraphic coals (Isa. 6:6), and the Lord's anger is kindled (see above).

["Is not Aaron the Levite thy brother?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 14). The Lord had already made all the necessary arrangements and had already taken everything into account. His rebuke of Moses in verse 11 was merely a stall to teach Moses a lesson in obedience. God had actually already taken account of Moses' infirmities (Heb. 12:21) and had made allowance for them (Phil. 4:19). The reader will observe that the time sequence of the second half verse 27 follows verse 17; that is, in the first half of verse 27, Aaron had already received orders to leave Egypt and head for Sinai BEFORE Moses even saw the burning bush.

["I know that he can speak well"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 14). The Lord is getting tired of explaining things. If men must give account for ["every idle word" ]{.class_s4N2-0}they speak in the day of Judgment (Matt. 12:26), and there was not a "word on David's tongue" that the Lord did not know[ "altogether"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Psa. 139:4), certainly He knows who can speak well and who can't.

["And thou shalt speak unto him, and put words in his mouth"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 15). Then Moses does not have any trouble talking to anyone in private; his trouble is that he []{#cGM.xhtml_page_105}does not feel called to be a public speaker. ["And I will be with thy mouth, and with his mouth"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 15), so there really isn't going to be any problem.

["He shall be to thee instead of a mouth, and thou shalt be to him instead of God" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 16). All of the major commentators, without exception, fail to see the humor in the rebuke. Bullinger's cross-references are correct (2 Tim. 3:16; Num. 12:6, 11:29; Deut. 33:1), but the statement is not quite as serious as that. The reader will note that not only is Moses playing "God" for Aaron, he is playing "God" for Pharaoh (Exod. 7:1). The serious sense, of course, is that Aaron receives his communications "via the grapevine" (vs. 30), at least until they leave Egypt (Lev. 11:1, 13:1, 15:1, etc.).

However, Aaron turns out to be one of those "answers to prayer" (Exod. 4:13) which we sometimes wish the Lord had not answered (Deut. 9:20). The price for a mouthpiece (stand-in, ghost writer, second, patsy, pinch hitter, etc.) [was 3,000 graves]{.class_s4N4-0} (Exod. 32:28).

Notice: ["he shall be to thee instead of a mouth"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 16)! That is, "you have renounced your mouth which I made, and therefore, you can't use it anymore; I gave it to somebody else!" (This is the gist of the "original," to quote the lying apostates of the Twentieth Century, even if you can't find it in Hebrew.)

We learn from Deuteronomy 32:1--4 that Moses had just about as much need for Aaron's mouth (Exod. 32:24!) as Samson had need of David's sling.

["And thou shalt take this rod in thine hand, wherewith thou shalt do signs" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 17). Here follows verses 27 (the second half) and 28, or at least shortly after. There is no indication that Aaron saw the burning bush. We are left with the impression that when the []{#cGM.xhtml_page_106}conversation ended at verse 17, the angel vanished, the fire flickered out, and Moses was left alone with his serpentine ["rod"]{.class_s4N2-0} and a flock of Jethro's sheep. The bush stands before him shimmering in the sunlight, its green leaves as fresh and moist as a satsuma on an early morning in November.

4:18 "And Moses went and returned to Jethro his father in law, and said unto him, Let me go, I pray thee, and return unto my brethren which are in Egypt, and see whether they be yet alive. And Jethro said to Moses, Go in peace.

19 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses in Midian, Go, return into Egypt: for all the men are dead which sought thy life.

20 And Moses took his wife and his sons, and set them upon an ass, and he returned to the land of Egypt: and Moses took the rod of God in his hand.

21 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, When thou goest to return into Egypt, see that thou do all those wonders before Pharaoh, which I have put in thine hand: but I will harden his heart, that he shall not let the people go.

22 And thou shalt say unto Pharaoh, Thus saith the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, Israel is my son, even my firstborn:

23 And I say unto thee, Let my son go, that he may serve me: and if thou refuse to let him go, behold, I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn."

When Moses reports back to his daddy-in-law, he gives a report like Saul gave in 1 Samuel 10:16. That is, he omits any reference to God or the call to preach. ["Let me go, I pray thee, and return unto my brethren which are in Egypt\...And Jethro said to Moses, Go in peace"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 18). Although we are not told [why ]{.class_s4N4-0}Moses []{#cGM.xhtml_page_107}misrepresented his trip in this way, it is clear from what follows that Moses is going to have trouble telling "the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth." (See the comments under vss. 22 and 23.) One may conjecture that this was a "secret mission," and Jethro had no business knowing about it. But Moses' confrontation with Pharaoh is anything BUT "secret" (Exod. 5:1, 7:10, 15, 8:8, etc.).

Furthermore, it appears that Aaron is with Moses when he returns to Jethro (vs. 18 cf. vss. 27--29). Moses evidently takes his wife and children to ["the inn"]{.class_s4N2-0} mentioned in Genesis 42:27. (Dummelow's remark that it is "not necessarily a building" and then his reference to the corrupt [RV ]{.class_s4N4-0}\[1885\] means nothing one way or another.)

The fraudulent [Septuagint ]{.class_s4N4-0}adds nine words to verse 18 and then changes ["an ass"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 20) to "upon ASSES." The thinking behind both of these perversions of Scripture is that if Moses had two sons (see notes under 2:22), it would have taken two or three ASSES to carry the family; therefore, to "help God out," etc., the Alexandrian revisers have amended the text exactly as the [NASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1963) amended it in Luke 2:33; John 9:35; 1 Timothy 3:16; Luke 23:42; Acts 9:5--6; and the other 800 places. (See [Bible Believer's Commentary on Matthew, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Peter Ruckman, 1970 \[hereafter referred to as [Commentary on Matthew]{.class_s4N4-0}\], comments on Matthew 21:5.)

As in the case of the Lord Jesus, Moses returns to Egypt as[ "God"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 16, 7:1), and as in the case of the Lord Jesus, the ["powers that be" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Rom. 13:1) which sought to kill him (Matt. 2:20) are dead (vs. 19).

The order of events is:

1\. Aaron meets Moses and sees the signs (vss. 27--28).

2\. Moses returns to his "in-laws" and gets permission to go. Permission is required as a matter of courtesy (Gen. 31:25--28), for Zipporah is going with him (vss. 24--25).

3\. Aaron returns to Egypt and waits at an ["inn" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(cf. vs. 24).

[4. They meet Aaron at the inn, and the following scene ensues ]{.class_s4N3-0}(vss. 24--26). Zipporah returns to Midian (see Exod. 18:1--2), and Aaron and Moses go on into Egypt and assemble the elders (vs. 29). (We have commented already on vs. 21 under 3:19--20 and 4:1--9.)

["And thou shalt say unto Pharaoh, Thus saith the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N8-2}[, Israel is my son, even my firstborn"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 22). At this point, one of the greatest dispensational truths in the Bible is revealed, and again, it escapes the notice of 90% of the commentators from A.D. 500 to A.D. 1975. Dummelow grasps at the verse to prove that Gentiles must be the "second born" if Israel is the firstborn. Professor Johnson ([Wycliffe Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0}) cannot even find a truth in the passage worth commenting on. And Clarke stumbles off the track mumbling something about the Hebrew people being "dear to God."

But the passage is "real boss" (junkie expression).

1\. A [corporate nation]{.class_s4N4-0} has been chosen as a "SON OF GOD."

2\. The SONS OF GOD, until here, are angels (Job 1:6, 38:7; Gen. 6:1--4).

3\. THE Son of God is Jesus Christ.

4\. Believers on Him (John 1:12--13) will replace the sons of God mentioned in Job 1:6 and Matthew 22:30.

5\. Therefore, we are told, in no uncertain terms, that the only "new birth" in the Old Testament is [the corporate birth of a nation]{.class_s4N4-0} (Hos. 11:1 cf. Gal. 4:1--6).

Individual "sons of God" do not exist until ["the firstborn of every creature"]{.class_s4N2-0} is Mary's firstborn (Col. 1:15 cf. Matt. 1:25). The omission, therefore, of the word ["firstborn"]{.class_s4N2-0} from the Dark Age Jesuit Bible of 1582 and the [NASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (recommended by the faculty at Bob Jones University) is "very interesting." It is a Satanic obliteration of the truth.

Israel---as a nation---can be called the ["sons" ]{.class_s4N2-0}and ["daughters"]{.class_s4N2-0} of God (Isa. 43:6), but no individual Israelite was ever called a "SON of God" until THE SON (Matt. 21:36--37) showed up! When Jesus Christ showed up, He was God's ["holy child"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Acts 4:27)---born of God. (The corrupt [NASV,]{.class_s4N4-0} promoted by [The Sword of the Lord,]{.class_s4N4-0} matches the Roman Catholic Bibles in Acts 4:27, 30. Both readings are to be handled like the serpents of Mark 16:18. These Bibles can't hurt you; pick them up and then shake them off \[Acts 28:5\][ into the fire]{.class_s4N4-0} \[Heb. 6: 8\].)

The ["church of the firstborn,"]{.class_s4N2-0} then, in Hebrews 12:23 may refer to Israel; since that book is written to the Hebrews---not the Body of Christ---that is probably the meaning.

4:24 "And it came to pass by the way in the inn, that the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}met him, and sought to kill him.

25 Then Zipporah took a sharp stone, and cut off the foreskin of her son, and cast it at his feet, and said, Sure1y a bloody husband art thou to me.

26 So he let him go: then she said, A bloody husband thou art, because of the circumcision."

The passage is one of the most difficult in the book of Exodus. Most of the commentators seem to think that God suddenly met Moses and would have killed him (Moses) if Zipporah had not circumcised her child. [But there were TWO, remember?!]{.class_s4N4-0}

Professor Johnson blames the thing on Zipporah and insists that she delayed circumcising her son until God nearly killed her husband; the [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0} takes the same view. Some think that Zipporah approved of circumcision (since the Midianites were descended from Abraham, Gen. 25:1-4), but objected to circumcision at such an early age---the eighth day. Professor Johnson has a little trouble when quoting Moffatt, for before blaming Zipporah, he mentions that the circumcision had been neglected by Moses. One can hardly imagine the scene if it is presented as some of these scholars would present it.

Moses and Zipporah and his two sons go to the inn. They step through the door, and the Lord suddenly shows up. Before all four of them, He hauls out a sword (Num. 22:23) to kill Moses--- after just telling him: ["all the men are dead which sought thy life" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 19). Then Zipporah, by instinct, sees the necessary steps to take; instead of screaming, praying, stepping in front of Moses, running out the back door, or assuming a karate stance before the Lord, she picks up a flint knife (cf. Josh. 5:2) and cuts off the foreskin of ONE child. \[Sometimes commentators lack imagination and fail to conjure up the true picture created by their comments.\]

Driver has a much better explanation, which matches Deuteronomy 1:3; Exodus 5:1--3; Psalm 80:2, 7; and Genesis 17:10, 23. The Lord has showed up to kill one of the boys who has not yet been circumcised. Moses has evidently refused to circumcise his "firstborn" on the []{#cGM.xhtml_page_111}grounds that he is no longer a "Hebrew." Upon leaving Egypt in chapter 2, Moses renounced his nationality and refused to circumcise [Gershom]{.class_s4N4-0} (see the remarks under 3:1, 11). Still, it is hard to explain how Eliezer got circumcised later; if both boys were uncircumcised, [both of them ]{.class_s4N4-0}would have been in danger according to Genesis 17:14.

However, the "tie-in" between verses 23 and 24 indicates clearly that it is the firstborn of Moses who is in danger. [Gershom ]{.class_s4N4-0}is the one who is not circumcised; at this time, he could not have been much under 38 years old and would have had no more need to ride an ass (see [LXX ]{.class_s4N4-0}reading under vs. 20) than Glenn Cunningham or Jim Ryun. Furthermore, Zipporah seems to know exactly what to do without anyone telling her (vs. 25). The correct scene, then, must be projected on the screen as follows:

l Aaron meets Moses, Gershom, Zipporah, and Eliezer at the inn.

l The last thing Moses was told before leaving for the inn was: "Tell Pharaoh that if he doesn't let [my ]{.class_s4N4-0}firstborn go, I'll kill [his."]{.class_s4N4-0}

l Moses tells Gershom, Zipporah, Eliezer, and Aaron about this commission, and with this message in their ears, the entire company retires for the night.

l About midnight, Gershom is moaning and tossing on his pallet. Mamma goes to see what is wrong. Gershom is running a temperature of 105, and he is bathed in perspiration.

l Zipporah ransacks her first aid kit (and her brains) and can find nothing to stop the fever. Then Zipporah remembers! In forty years of living with Moses, she has heard Genesis chapter 17 so many times she could repeat []{#cGM.xhtml_page_112}it by memory. Now with Moses' official commission to be the leader of Abraham's [physical ]{.class_s4N4-0}descendants, he will have to conform to Genesis chapter 17 to the letter. She gets the knife, performs the painful operation, and then goes back into the bedroom, wakes up the mighty Moses, and slaps the foreskin down at his feet---he is sleeping on the floor---and says, "You're some husband! If I hadn't done the job you should have done, we would have had to bury Gershom in the morning! You nearly caused the death of your own firstborn with your carelessness; you're a BLOODY HUSBAND!"

Now that is the sense, and if the "Hebrew and Greek" ([Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}, written more than 100 years after the Ascension) doesn't bring out [that sense,]{.class_s4N4-0} then get something accurate like a [King James Authorized Version. ]{.class_s4N4-0}["A bloody husband" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is not in the least to be connected with "Zipporah's abhorrence of Hebrew religious rites," no matter how many "Conservatives" privately interpret the expression to have this meaning. Observe how the infallible English text defines the word without the aid of any scholar, of any persuasion, in any century, of any rank, station, or degree, or of any ability.

1\. ["Gather not\...my life with bloody men"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Psa. 26:9).

2\. ["Because thou art a bloody man"]{.class_s4N2-0} (2 Sam. 16:8).

3\. ["Woe to the bloody city" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Ezek. 24:6, 22:2).

4\. ["For Saul, and for his bloody house" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(2 Sam. 21:8).

Nowhere does the expression ever allow the interpretation given to it by Dummelow, Bullinger, Williams, Clarke, or the [Pulpit Commentary. ]{.class_s4N4-0}They are all aping each other and mimicking the depraved traditions []{#cGM.xhtml_page_113}passed down through the centuries, via the Seminaries. All of the comments on the passage which suggest that Zipporah was "repelled by Hebrew religious rites" are to be classified with [Life ]{.class_s4N4-0}magazine's reports on North Ireland.

Never accept any scholar's opinion about anything in the Bible where the [AV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1611) has already given its own comment.

4:27 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said to Aaron, Go into the wilderness to meet Moses. And he went, and met him in the mount of God, and kissed him.

28 And Moses told Aaron all the words of the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}who had sent him, and all the signs which he had commanded him."

We have already commented on this under 4:17--18, 24. Again, we have an example of the way in which the critics of the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611)---Conservatives foremost---have "expounded the word." One will be amazed to find that Williams has equated [Mount Hor]{.class_s4N4-0} with [Mount Horeb ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Commentary, pg. 47). The "nugget" from this association is to the effect that Moses and Aaron kiss hello and good-bye on the same mountain. However, this "nugget" is quite similar to Kenneth Wuest's "falling up" for ["falling away"]{.class_s4N2-0} in 2 Thessalonians 2:3, and his "myriads" for ["clouds" ]{.class_s4N2-0}in Revelation 1:7. Mount Hor is not within thirty miles of Mount Horeb, and if it could not be located on a map exactly, anyone could see from reading Numbers 20:23 and 21:4 that Mount Hor would have to be north of Ezion-geber. [Ezion-geber is 100 miles from Mount Horeb.]{.class_s4N4-0}

4:29 "And Moses and Aaron went and gathered together all the elders of the children of Israel:

30 And Aaron spake all the words which the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had spoken unto Moses, and did the signs in the sight of the people.

31 And the people believed: and when they heard that the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had visited the children of Israel, and that he had looked upon their affliction, then they bowed their heads and worshipped."

Most of the commentators run out of breath at verse 28, but this is certainly no place to stop. The next scene is fabulous.

Moses and Aaron contact the "underground church," arrange a meeting in someone's basement, and then the action starts. Aaron repeats everything God told Moses in Exodus 3:7--10 and 4:22. He announces, "Sign No. 1!" Moses slaps his rod down on the cement, and it turns into an eight-foot rattler; there aren't enough doors and windows for everybody to climb out. Moses and Aaron start yelling, ["Stop!]{.class_s4N4-0} Wait a minute! It's only a SIGN!" But the seventy elders have decided that it is a "sign" for them to get out of the building. It is not until Moses grabs the serpent by the tail that things calm down.

The elders are mopping sweat off their brows; and one of them says, "Okay, okay, I believe it, man! God sent you! That's good enough for me!"

But by now, Moses is having too good a time to quit. "That ain't nothin', boys! Get a load of [this!"]{.class_s4N4-0} In goes that hand, out it comes; it is covered with leprosy! "Abracadabra," says Moses, "now you see it and now you don't!"

"Gangway! Clear the deck! [Move, man, move!" ]{.class_s4N6}The seventy elders of Israel are heading for the doors and windows again. Moses reinserts his hand into his jacket and pulls it out again---whole.

"Holy Moses!" exclaims one ROMAN Jew.

"Now, don't go cussing," says Moses, "I'm not holy. Only God is holy. Now you see that there sign? Well, that's a perfect picture of your condition and how God is going to restore your spiritual health and\...."

"Hold the phone," interrupts Aaron, "where'd you get that ability to ad lib? I thought you were of 'slow speech,' brother! When did you get to be an extemp speaker?"

Moses ignores him and goes ahead: "And brethren, if that don't do the trick, I've got an ace in the hole! Yessir, I got one more card up my sleeve. You see this here rod? I'm gonna smite the waters of Egypt with this rod and turn them to blood!"

"Well, we think you can do it, Moses! Yessir, after seeing those signs, we believe you can do anything. We're behind you, man, all the way. Three cheers for Moses! Sieg Heil! [Sieg Heil!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Well, good," says Moses, "it's good to be appreciated, and I'm glad you gentlemen are going to give me some support. The last time I tried to help you out, you treated me like something the cat dragged in."

(Now we realize, gentle reader, that one will never get into the Scholars' Union if one persists in expounding the Bible in such a fashion, but honestly, is it as irreverent as the expositions which treat Jesus Christ as an [illegitimate child]{.class_s4N4-0} \[Luke 2:33, [NASV]{.class_s4N4-0}\] and Paul as an [epileptic ]{.class_s4N4-0}and Moses as a superstitious idiot? Have you read what Henry F. Cadbury, Walter Russell Bowie, Julius Bewer, William Albright, Frederick Grant, Millar []{#cGM.xhtml_page_116}Burrows, Willard Sperry, William Irwin, Clovis Chappell, and Luther Weigle actually [said]{.class_s4N4-0} about Jesus, Paul, and Moses?)

["And Aaron spake all the words\...and did the signs in the sight of the people" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 31). Like Thomas, "seeing is believing" with Israel (John 2:18, 6:30), but the believer cannot help but notice that the humbling (["they bowed their heads"]{.class_s4N2-0}) occurs after ["when they heard that the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[\...had looked upon their affliction" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 31). It was not only the grace and mercy of God which touched their hearts and caused them to worship, it was the sudden realization that the Holy One was NEAR; and if He had observed their [affliction,]{.class_s4N4-0} He had also observed their [sins ]{.class_s4N4-0}(see the comments under Exod. 33:3, 9 and [Commentary on Acts, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Acts 7:39).

[]{#cP1.xhtml}

# Chapter 5 {.heading_s4G-0}

5:1 "And afterward Moses and Aaron went in, and told Pharaoh, Thus saith the [Lord ]{.class_s4N9-1}God of Israel, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness.

2 And Pharaoh said, Who is the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, that I should obey his voice to let Israel go? I know not the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, neither will I let Israel go.

3 And they said, The God of the Hebrews hath met with us: let us go, we pray thee, three days' journey into the desert, and sacrifice unto the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}our God; lest he fall upon us with pestilence, or with the sword.

4 And the king of Egypt said unto them, Wherefore do ye, Moses and Aaron, let the people from their works? get you unto your burdens.

5 And Pharaoh said, Behold, the people of the land now are many, and ye make them rest from their burdens.

6 And Pharaoh commanded the same day the taskmasters of the people, and their officers, saying,

7 Ye shall no more give the people straw to make brick, as heretofore: let them go and gather straw for themselves.

8 And the tale of the bricks, which they did make heretofore, ye shall lay upon them; ye shall not diminish ought thereof: for they be idle; therefore they cry, saying, Let us go and sacrifice to our God.

9 Let there more work be laid upon the men, that they may labour therein; and let them not regard vain words."

Now the tempo picks up! Aaron and Moses have direct access to Pharaoh, and the speech which follows must have taken place in the palace at Tanis (Zoan).

No sooner does Moses reenter the courts he had forsaken forty years earlier (Heb. 11:24) than his courage begins to ebb. In spite of his "magic wand," Moses feels that the original version of Exodus 4:23 needed to be revised and "liberated from its archaic language," etc. That is, it is too clear. (Cf. [The New Scofield Reference Bible, ]{.class_s4N4-0}which gets rid of "Hell" by refusing to give any translation for ["hades" ]{.class_s4N4-0}\[Gk.\].)

We don't know what would have happened if Moses had spoken the message God told him to speak at the start, but we do know what happened when he [refused ]{.class_s4N4-0}to speak it: he caused additional suffering to his people (5:4--18). All of the major commentators miss the import of the passage, and not one of them, from Chrysostom to Williams, noticed the similarity between Moses' first message and Balaam's first message (Num. 22:12--13). (The reader will also note that the footnote in BOTH of the [Scofield Reference Bibles ]{.class_s4N4-0}fails even to [indicate ]{.class_s4N4-0}what the trouble is. Balaam is not in the "permissive will of God" in "going." He has deliberately subtracted two-thirds of the first message given to him, and he has made no promise that he will deliver three-thirds of the next one.)

Moses' problem is perfectly understandable. One can imagine what went through Samuel's mind when he was given his first three-point message (1 Sam. 3:11--14). It is one thing to preach John 3:16 and Proverbs 11:30 while you are building a monument to yourself as a Christian celebrity; and it is another thing to walk into the White House and address the Chief Executive with: "Thus saith []{#cP1.xhtml_page_119}the Lord, if you don't straighten up and fly right, you're going to slide into Hell and fry like a sausage!"

(This type of "soulwinning" is called the "lunatic fringe" by the apostate Christians of the Twentieth Century. It also explains why Christians no longer fear to tamper with the Bible, and why the new converts never develop any real Christian character. None of this "new breed" seems to have noticed that many of the Biblical messages which are delivered are not intended to "win" anyone. When Jesus Christ preached John 9:39--41, He was not trying to get anyone converted, nor was He when He spoke Matt. 23:4--36.)

["Thus saith the ]{.class_s4N2-1}[Lord]{.class_s4N8-1}[ ]{.class_s4NX}[God of Israel, Let my people go" ]{.class_s4N2-1}[(vs. 1). But where is the rest of the commission? Where is the ]{.class_s4N3-0}["firstborn"]{.class_s4N2-1}[ of 4:22 and the]{.class_s4N3-0}[ "I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn"]{.class_s4N2-1}[? Moses has diluted the message and made it as sweet and positive as anything Theodore Epp ever preached.]{.class_s4N3-0}

Moses says, "['Let my people go,' ]{.class_s4N2-0}or else\...!"

Pharaoh laughs and says, "Hey, man! [What's this?"]{.class_s4N4-0}

Moses says, "You gotta let my people go, or\...."

Pharaoh: "Or WHAT? !"

Moses' face turns white, and he says, "Well, er, and\...if you don't, you're ah, ah, gonna see something!"

Pharaoh laughs and says, "Well cut off my legs and call me shorty! What am I going to see, son?"

Moses turns to Aaron and whispers hoarsely, "Should I tell him?"

Aaron says, "Well, you know what God told you to tell him about the firstborn. Go'wan and tell him!"

Moses replies, "My goodness, man, I can't tell him THAT! Why, great day in the morning, you know I almost lost my own boy the other day!"

Aaron mutters, "You been mumblin' in your beard about that for the last 24 hours. Now just face it, man, you goofed! You ran out on your people; you were ashamed to let us know you were still a Jew. You can't blame that on God, man. Go on, give him the message!"

Moses says, "Well, yeah, but wheeeww-man! I think I'd better break it to him gently. No use dumpin' the whole load at one time; we'd better leave well enough alone!"

He then turns to the Pharaoh and croaks: "If you don't let the children of Israel go, ah, ah, well ah\...heavy-heavy hangs over thy head!"

Pharaoh grins sardonically. "Izzat right?" Then he bellows, "Listen, fella, I got my problems, and the 'Lord God,' has His, and this ain't the time to discuss it. There's a time and place for religion, and this ain't it! ['I know not the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[']{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 2)."

Neither did Karl Marx (1818--1883), Baruch Spinoza (1632--1677), Charles Darwin (1809--1882), Tom Paine (1737--1809), Sigmund Freud (1856--1939), Bertrand Russell (1872--1970), nor 90% of the historians, philosophers, scientists, or political leaders of ANY age. "Agnosticism" (first coined by Thomas Huxley) has always been a popular religion among people who want to appear "broadminded," when the real trouble is that they think they are smarter than the Holy Spirit. "Agnostics" are among those who suffer[ "flaming fire"]{.class_s4N2-0} at the Second Advent (2 Thess. 1:8). The agnostics of Athens, who worshipped ["THE UNKNOWN GOD" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(see [Commentary on Acts, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Acts 17:23) in their ignorance, were no different than 90% of the educators in the National Education Association, today.

Pharaoh knew Osiris the frog-faced god, Apis the sacred bull, Ra the hawk-faced god and Isis the "Queen of Heaven"; but when it came to ["the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 Pet. 1:3), he knew no more about Him than Mrs. Roosevelt, Ted Kennedy, Angela Davis, or Fidel Castro. ["I know not the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}["]{.class_s4N2-0} is the honest religious confession of modern America among its "educated class."

["Neither will I let Israel go" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 2). Before God got through with Pharaoh, he not only let the children of Israel go (Exod. 12:31), but he also came to know something about God (Exod. 10:17). One is reminded of other famous "last words" when one reads the vainglorious boast of the King of Egypt. I believe it was Napoleon who said before the battle of Waterloo: "Never mind the circumstances; I MAKE circumstances!" (Other famous last liners are: "Go ahead and stand up; the boat won't capsize"; "Light a match and see if there is a gas leak"; "Pull the trigger; it ain't loaded"; and "Phooey! God is too good to send anyone to Hell," etc.)

["Let us go, we pray thee, three days' journey into the desert, and sacrifice unto the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N8-2}[\...lest he fall upon us with pestilence" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 3). The request is not farfetched. God had a perfect right to wipe out the whole nation, for it is evident that at this time they were idolaters worshipping many of the gods of Egypt (Josh. 24:14; Exod. 20:3--5; Lev. 17:7). Moreover, it is clear that the ancient sacrifice of the lamb (see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 3:21, 4:4, 8:20, 22:10--14) had been shelved, and a whole generation had grown up with no knowledge of the required sacrifice (Exod. 12:1--3). This is most clearly pointed out by the fact that not even [Moses ]{.class_s4N4-0}volunteered to sacrifice anything when he []{#cP1.xhtml_page_122}was standing directly in the presence of the Almighty (Exod. 3--4). Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob would have had a bleeding lamb ready in one minute flat. Observe how quickly Gideon and Manoah see the need for an offering as soon as the Angel of the Lord puts in an appearance (Judg. 6, 13).

Exodus 8:26 explains Genesis 43:32 and 46:34; in passing, it corrects the errors of Ellicott, Henry, Lange, Clarke, and the [Pulpit Commentary. ]{.class_s4N4-0}All of these sources suppose (on the grounds of historical research as opposed to the Holy Spirit) that the Egyptians were worried about the Jews killing a[ sacred cow. ]{.class_s4N4-0}However, this is [not ]{.class_s4N4-0}the sense; and the Holy Spirit has been careful to preserve the truth in spite of Fundamental, Liberal, Catholic, and Conservative scholarship.

1\. [Shepherds ]{.class_s4N4-0}are an abomination to Egyptians (Gen. 46:34) .

2\. [Hebrews ]{.class_s4N4-0}are an abomination to Egyptians (Gen. 43:32).

3\. Hebrews are shepherds and sacrifice SHEEP (cf. Gen. 4:2, 4).

The reading plainly, then, is that in order to sacrifice sheep, the Hebrews will have to get out of the land, for it is an abomination for an Egyptian to sacrifice sheep to his gods.

["And the king of Egypt said unto them\...ye make them rest from their burdens" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 4--5). The charge was true, and it pictures the great emancipator, the Lord Jesus Christ, who came to ease the burden for all those who were[ "heavy laden"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Matt. 11:28). However, to ["the god of this world" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(2 Cor. 4:4), the Gospel messenger is merely a "capitalistic tool of the establishment" for "enslaving the masses with the []{#cP1.xhtml_page_123}opiate of religion," etc. Marx and Lenin both confused Biblical Christianity with Roman Catholic "sacraments." But the offer of Aaron and Moses is a genuine offer (i.e., ["the land]{.class_s4N2-0}). Observe: ["I will bring you out\...I will redeem you\...I will be to you a God\...I will give it you\..." ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Exod. 6:7--8).

However, to Pharaoh, the whole show looks like the CIO or the AFL striking for higher wages. "You're slowin' down production, you blankety-blank Communists! Whatdya want for a dollar anyway? Yer gettin' paid twenty cents an hour and only have to work a seventy-hour week; whatd-ya buckin' for? Paid vacations and Social Security?"

["Ye shall no more give the people straw to make brick\...and the tale of the bricks, which they did make heretofore, ye shall lay upon them\...for they be idle\...let them not regard vain words" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 7--9). All of this is explained in the verses which follow. When Kyle excavated Pithom (1908), he found the lower layers in the buildings composed of bricks with chopped straw in them, the middle layer had bricks containing straw plucked up by the roots, and the upper layers had been made with clay only. (Chopped straw increases the strength of brick about twenty percent, and it was often used in ancient building.)

Pharaoh is clear and frank, even if he is demoniac. ["The tale of the bricks" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is the number of bricks, exactly as the word [teller]{.class_s4N4-0} is used today for a money counter (see [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 15:5). The children of Israel are to make up the same quota of bricks as before, except now they have to gather their own straw (vs. 12), and they are beaten when the[ "tale" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is not met (vs. 16). Pharaoh, as the Devil, kicks at the []{#cP1.xhtml_page_124}first promise of liberty for the captive (Gal. 4:1--5), and the only religion he knows is WORKS (Exod. 5:9, 11, 18). Satan always puts out an added effort as the sinner nears salvation (Mark 10:48!).

We should notice that the Biblical origin of the expression "straw boss" is found here. The word is used for a minor superintendent. (The parody of Pharaoh's servitude is the one about the straw boss who was so mean that when a boiler burst and blew the crew up into the air, he docked the workers for the time they were off the job.)

Moses and Aaron vanish at verse 5. In the middle of Pharaoh's tirade about capital and labor, Moses backs off and leaves the building. Aaron goes with him, nudging him all the way, "The signs\![ The signs! ]{.class_s4N4-0}Why didn't you show him one of the signs?"

"Well," says Moses, "Let's, ah, wait awhile and, ah, see how things turn out. We don't want to run ahead of God, you know."

Moses has learned his lesson (see Exod. 4:1), but like most of us, he has learned it too well, and now he leans over too far the other way (see remarks under Exod. 3:11). This time, his pussy-footing costs his brethren some bitter days, and while he is "soft-pedaling" the message, his congregation is getting beaten (vs. 16). If the beating is the infamous "bastinado" of Roman Catholicism, they are being forced to lie down and raise their legs; the beating is administered to the soles of the feet with an iron or a wooden rod. This form of torture is quite common in Romania, Bulgaria, Hungary, Russia, and Red China today; and it was used by the Gestapo extensively in the occupied countries of France, Italy, Poland, and Russia during World War II.

["Let them not regard vain words"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 9). This is the Liberal's attitude towards the original Scriptures, the Communist's attitude towards any religion, and the Roman Catholic's attitude towards the works of Dunlap, Hislop, Manhattan, Carrera, Montano, Zachello, and Chiniquy. It is also the attitude of the conservative scholars in America towards the [Authorized Version of 1611. ]{.class_s4N4-0}It is most "instructive to note" (Eighteenth-Century cliché) that the ["vain words"]{.class_s4N2-0} to which Pharaoh refers are [the words of the Lord God Almighty]{.class_s4N4-0} (5:1, 3 cf. 3:12, 18). What the world system calls ["vain words"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Matt. 23; John 3, 9; Rom. 10; Gal. 3; Gen. 3, 9; Isa. 53; Psa. 22; and Rev. 19--22) are the God-breathed words of divine authority which shall remain steadfast after the heavens and earth have vanished from view. Pharaoh, like Jehudi and the Lockman Foundation, thinks that God's infallible decrees are the private interpretations of Moses or Baruch (see Jer. 43:3). It reminds us of Renan and Strauss, who went around looking for the "historical Jesus" on the grounds that the men who ate, slept, and preached with Him didn't know what they were talking about when they wrote the Gospels.

5:10 "And the taskmasters of the people went out, and their officers, and they spake to the people, saying, Thus saith Pharaoh, I will not give you straw.

11 Go ye, get you straw where ye can find it: yet not ought of your work shall be diminished.

12 So the people were scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble instead of straw.

13 And the taskmasters hasted them, saying, Fulfil your works, your daily tasks, as when there was straw.

14 And the officers of the children of Israel, which Pharaoh's taskmasters had set over them, were beaten, and demanded, Wherefore have ye not fulfilled your task in making brick both yesterday and to day, as heretofore?"

We have commented on these verses somewhat already under verses 6--9.

["Thus saith Pharaoh" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 10) should be compared with verse l. Pharaoh (as Neal, Einstein, Schopenhauer, Nietzsche, Kierkegaard, Tillich, Brunner, Niebhur, Custer, Weniger, Freud, Marx, Haeckel, Paley, Lyell, the Lockman Foundation, and SIECUS) has set himself up as an authority over and against God, and he borrows the prophetic formula out of the mouth of Aaron and Moses---["Thus saith the LORD."]{.class_s4N2-0}

["Get you straw where ye can find it" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 11). It is lying all over the ground in the region of the Delta, but after awhile it will be collected and then the people will be forced[ "to gather stubble instead of straw"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 12). That is, they have to pull the dead growth out of the field by the roots. The student should observe the "substitute" (as usual), which is the Devil's way of "building the Kingdom": stubble for straw and bricks for stone (Gen. 11:2--3). \[See [The Sure Word of Prophecy]{.class_s4N4-0}.\]

["And the officers of the children of Israel"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 14). Pharaoh has set up the slave labor camp something like Buchenwald and Treblinka were set up. ["Sondern Kommandos" ]{.class_s4N4-0}were Jews themselves who were responsible to the kapo or block leader, who in turn was responsible to "the powers that be." With this neat arrangement, Jews were forced to operate the furnaces that burned their own people. They were forced to extract []{#cP1.xhtml_page_127}gold from the corpses of their own sons, daughters, brothers, sisters, and wives; forced to bury the corpses of their own cell mates; forced to whip and beat their own comrades; and forced (in some cases) to dissect the bodies of their own relatives. The psychology behind all of this was simple: you take the job as "overseer" over your own people, or you yourself are buried alive or beaten to death with a shovel---take your pick. If you [accept ]{.class_s4N4-0}the job, you will still be beaten and whipped every time anyone under you fails to pass inspection, fall out for roll call, loafs on the job, or refuses to obey any order. Your only hope, then, of staying alive and staying in any kind of health, lies in your ability to harass and punish your own people. Scores of "kapos" loved Israel and loved their own kinsmen, but under the daily threat of death by torture, they carried out the orders of the Death's Head units exactly as ["the officers of the children of Israel"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 15) were carrying out Pharaoh's orders.

You will observe that the complaint they lodge with the "Commandant" (vs. 16) goes unheeded, and the beatings are now accompanied by killings (vs. 21).

5:15 "Then the officers of the children of Israel came and cried unto Pharaoh, saying, Wherefore dealest thou thus with thy servants?

16 There is no straw given unto thy servants, and they say to us, Make brick: and, behold, thy servants are beaten; but the fault is in thine own people.

17 But he said, Ye are idle, ye are idle: therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}.

18 Go therefore now, and work; for there shall no straw be given you, yet shall ye deliver the tale of bricks.

19 And the officers of the children of Israel did see that they were in evil case, after it was said, Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily task."

We have commented on verse 15 under verse 14.

["Thy servants are beaten" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 16). The officers make their complaint politely and refer to themselves as Pharaoh's servants. All they get for their courtesy is ridicule. ["Ye are idle, ye are idle\...Go therefore now, and work" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 17--18). Worshipping God ["in spirit and in truth" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 17 cf. John 4:24) is foolishness to this world and to ["the princes of this world" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(1 Cor. 2:26). Pharaoh---as Sennacherib, Nebuchadnezzar, Caesar, Nimrod, Genghis Khan, and Pope Paul VI---was a prince of [this world]{.class_s4N4-0} (cf. John 14:30), receiving orders from the prince of Ephesians 2:2, who will show up later to reign over the United Nations (Rev. 13:3--8).

We have already commented on verses 18 and 19.

5:20 "And they met Moses and Aaron, who stood in the way, as they came forth from Pharaoh:

21 And they said unto them, The [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}look upon you, and judge; because ye have made our savour to be abhorred in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of his servants to put a sword in their hand to slay us.

22 And Moses returned unto the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, and said, Lord, wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why is it that thou hast sent me?

23 For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in thy name, he hath done evil to this people; neither hast thou delivered thy people at all."

["And they met Moses and Aaron, who stood in the way" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 20). To fully appreciate the scene, one []{#cP1.xhtml_page_129}must abandon all of the critics of the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) text and thrust himself into the setting in a realistic fashion. (Dead-orthodox linguists like the writers of the [Biblical Viewpoint]{.class_s4N4-0} rarely grasp the real meaning of any Bible text, for they divorce it from reality and especially from PRESENT reality. Most of the commentators live in the past or the future.) To really grasp the text, one must forever discard such mouthy expositions as: "Utterly exhausted by the laborious duties placed mandatorially upon their shoulders, the chosen ones of Israel were no doubt deeply discouraged and emotionally depressed when they next encountered their appointed deliverer who, etc."

No, this is not the scene at all. Moses and Aaron trudge back to Pharaoh's place the next evening, after a restless night and day spent arguing about how to approach the King again. On the way to the palace, they pass the labor gangs coming in from work. They have been driving the bulldozers and tractors all day long, hauling logs, toting bricks, lifting cotton bales, and singing "Ole' Man Riber." As the dusty, sweating column tramps by Moses and Aaron, angry voices cry out: "Ah ya blankety blank, whatdaya doin here?! Run back to Sinai, you desert rat!" Other voices call out: "Heah, you drip, you're nothin' but trouble! Blow!"

Moses looks at Aaron, and the two exchange the glances of men who have suddenly stepped into quicksand. They march over to the column and walking alongside it, Moses pleads, "W-what do you boys mean? I thought you said I was the leader! I thought you were all gung-ho on the operation; you know, "Heil Moses" and all that kinda thing!"

"Go on, buddy; shove off! You're trouble with a capital 'T'!"

"It's off, Moses; we changed our minds!"

"But boys\...!"

"Boys, your foot! Hit the road, you faker, before you get tarred and feathered."

"But fellas, God called me and gave me the signs\...."

"Yeah, we know, we know! Sleight of hand magic, we know!"

A grim face pokes out of the column, and through dust-caked lips it says to Moses: "Listen, fly boy, since you two bozoes showed up, the Egyptians doubled our work hours and cut our pay in half. If I wuz you, I'd get outta town immediately, if not sooner. We weren't born yesterday or the day before, and if you two phonies aren't outta here by tomorrow, we're gonna have us a necktie party!"

And accompanied by hoots and howls,and threats and curses, the prophet and the "god" (Exod. 4:16) make a hasty exit and double-time it to the palace. Moses says, "Man, folks sure are fickle, ain't they?"

"Well," says Aaron, "you can't blame them. They're working a ninety-hour week, and that ain't playin', man! That's gettin' with it."

Another column of concentration camp inmates goes by, and angry voices are heard yelling, "Yeah, water into blood, water into blood! Hah! You can't cut the mustard, fella!" More of the same: "God ain't gonna judge Pharaoh, you religious quack! [He's gonna judge YOU!" ]{.class_s4N4-0}And still more of the same: "God ain't done nothin'! You ain't been called to preach, buster; go back to Midian where you came from!"

By the time ["the officers"]{.class_s4N2-0} have finished their speech of verse 21, Moses is at the point of a nervous breakdown. The last officer spits on the ground in front of Moses as he leaves and says, "You better go back to Alexandria, dude, and get the right grammatico-historico interpretation on that passage, 'cause it sure ain't literal."

Now that is the scene, transplanted through the centuries. The sterile commentators are no more able to comprehend the various components of the scene than they are able to whistle and swallow at the same time. The "new translations" and the "new, new, colloquial, American-English translations" not only fail to translate the Masoretic text, they totally fail to update the material. ["Mind your own business" ]{.class_s4N4-0}(cited by the [Wycliffe Commentary,]{.class_s4N4-0} pg. 56, from the [American Translation]{.class_s4N4-0}) is no more the thought of Exodus 5:22 than the [RSV ]{.class_s4N4-0}reading for Luke 1:34 is speaking the thoughts of Mary. People do NOT talk like the modern translations make them talk, and the ["koine"]{.class_s4N4-0} of Twentieth-Century American is as far removed from ["Living Letters"]{.class_s4N4-0} and ["Good News"]{.class_s4N4-0} as the koine of the first century was removed from [Alexandrinus]{.class_s4N4-0}, [Sinaiticus]{.class_s4N4-0}, and [Vaticanus]{.class_s4N4-0}.

The dead-orthodox critics of the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611)---any major commentator since 1890---are completely divorced from the reality of their own times in the age in which they live, and one will notice an appalling ignorance of human nature manifested in every line of their work as they seek to replace the[ King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}text. Moses and Aaron were real people, the people with whom they dealt were real people, and human nature has not changed one time from Genesis 3:8 to the present date. Real people react certain ways in certain situations, and the reaction of the children of Israel at this time is that noted above. You []{#cP1.xhtml_page_132}can bet on it, you can bank on it, and you can depend on it like you would lean on the Empire State Building for support. There are no "American translations" of the Bible, and if there were, the Liberals and Conservatives would both abhor them like they were Gila monsters. They would treat them with more contempt than they treat the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611), and they already treat that like it was Rip Van Winkle running on the Independent ticket.

Well, Moses is heartbroken (vs. 22). He is in hot water, and he feels that God has let him down. At this time, he and Jonah could have had some real fellowship, for both of them delivered a message that misfired. To understand their feelings, you would have to be a [preacher ]{.class_s4N4-0}or an [infantryman]{.class_s4N4-0}. As one has so aptly said: "The most exhilarating feeling in the world is to be shot at and missed." So it works conversely: "The most depressing thing in the world is to threaten to shoot a man and then find your gun isn't loaded."

Moses retires to a prayer closet and says,[ "]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N7-1}[, wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why is it that thou hast sent me?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 22).

You can be certain that the last eight words passed through the minds of Adoniram Judson (1788--1850), John Paton (1824--1907), Mary Slessor (1848--1915), and Martin Luther (1483--1546) on more than one occasion. And all of God's children are familiar with the text, slightly altered by time and circumstance: "Why did you let the boy die?" "Why did you let them take the property?" "Why must I rot in this dungeon?" "Why wasn't anyone saved tonight?" "Why do I have to go through life like this?" "Why couldn't you have spared her?" ["Why is it that thou hast sent me?"]{.class_s4N2-0}

Rebekah laments, ["Why should I be deprived also of you both\...?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Gen. 27:45).

Daniel says, ["Why is the decree so hasty\...?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Dan. 2:15).

Job cries, ["Why is light given to a man\...?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Job 3:23) .

Pilate questions, ["Why, what evil hath he done?" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Matt. 27:23).

"Tempted and tried we're oft made to wonder WHY IT SHOULD BE THUS all the day long!"

Song writers have penned, "WHY SHOULD I be discouraged, WHY SHOULD the shadows come?" "I do not know WHY OFT ROUND ME, my hopes all shattered seem to be!" And the greatest "why" ever asked of God was asked on the bare, bleak side of a lonely mountain in the dark hour of judgment: ["My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Mark 15:34).

["Why is it thou hast sent me? For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in thy name, he hath done evil to this people; neither hast thou delivered thy people at all" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 22--23). One cannot help but notice that Moses is about to forsake his heritage again. Observe: ["this people\...thy people."]{.class_s4N2-0} Why not "my people"?! We are left with the feeling that Moses is still about half committed to his calling and would probably "flake out" at the drop of a hat.

Moses is praying. "Lord? Lord, are you there? Lord, I'm down in the dumps; I'm at the bottom of the barrel. I did like you told me to do, and look what they're doin' to me! I told them what you told me to tell them and\...."

"You did not."

"But I did, Lord. I came back down here and delivered your message to them\...."

"Now come on, Moses; think twice! Now really think that thing through. [Did you REALLY deliver the message to Pharaoh?"]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Well, ah, well, I did; that is, ah, I did all but that part about slaying the firstborn. I, ah, thought that was, er, spreading it on a little too thick right at the start so I\...."

"So you altered the message, [right?"]{.class_s4N4-0}

Silence.

"Now, Moses, looka here. If you and I are going to work together, we're going to have an understanding right from the start, [right?"]{.class_s4N4-0}

More silence and then a meek, "Yes, Lord."

"All right, there is one thing we are going to get straight before we go a step further, and it is a very simple proposition: when I tell you to say something, bud, YOU SAY IT!"

More silence.

"Now, do you understand [that?!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Yes, Lord."

"I mean, you're not to add to it ['lest I reprove thee, and thou be found a liar' ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Prov. 30:6); and you're not to take from it lest I take your name ['out of the book of life']{.class_s4N2-0} (Rev. 22:19). ['Every word of God is pure']{.class_s4N2-0} and I am ['a shield']{.class_s4N2-0} to those that trust me (Prov. 30:5). My words are purified in a furnace seven times and preserved forever (Psa. 12:6--7), and[ 'heaven and earth shall pass away, but MY WORDS shall not']{.class_s4N2-0} (Matt. 24:35). Now, have you got that, Moses?"

"Yes, Lord."

"All right, Moses, from now on you say what I tell you to say, and don't you go around like your grandmother Eve adding words here and there and taking words out here and there; somebody is liable to think you're []{#cP1.xhtml_page_135}working for the Lockman Foundation. When you quote me, I want you to quote me, and I don't mean my thoughts, doctrines, teachings, or 'fundamentals.' I mean my WORDS, do you read me?"

"Yes, Lord."

"All right," says the Lord, "I'm glad we got that settled!"

(From the above, one can see why so little comment was made on the passage by Williams, Brown, Fausset, Clarke, Hengstenberg, Delitzsch, Gesenius, Lange, Ellicott, and Dummelow! \[See [Commentary on Proverbs, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Prov. 30:5--6.\])

[]{#cTS.xhtml}

# Chapter 6 {.heading_s4G-0}

6:1 "Then the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh: for with a strong hand shall he let them go, and with a strong hand shall he drive them out of his land.

2 And God spake unto Moses, and said unto him, I am the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}:

3 And I appeared unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, by the name of God Almighty, but by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them.

4 And I have also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their pilgrimage, wherein they were strangers.

5 And I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel, whom the Egyptians keep in bondage; and I have remembered my covenant.

6 Wherefore say unto the children of Israel, I am the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will rid you out of their bondage, and I will redeem you with a stretched out arm, and with great judgments:

7 And I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God: and ye shall know that I am the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}your God, which bringeth you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians.

8 And I will bring you in unto the land, concerning the which I did swear to give it to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; and I will give it you for an heritage: I am the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}."

Much of this chapter is review material which goes over the ground of Exodus 4 and 5 again. Notice, especially, everything from verse 9 to verse 30.

["Now shalt thou see"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 1). Now the Lord has an even greater cause to judge Pharaoh: the working conditions of chapter 5 are unreasonable, the reasonable request of 5:3 has been denied, and the usurpation by Pharaoh of the place of God in making his proclamations (5:10). The ["strong hand"]{.class_s4N2-0} of the passage can be the Lord's hand, which is apparent from the context (["what I will do to Pharaoh"]{.class_s4N2-0}), or it can be Pharaoh's own desire to get rid of the Hebrews (Exod. 12:31). Exodus 11:1 clarifies the obscure corrections of the [RV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1885) and the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901), and makes it clear that Pharaoh's hand is as much in the final exodus as the hand of the Lord Himself.

["And I appeared unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, by the name of God Almighty, but by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3). This famous passage is known in dead-orthodox circles (and Liberal circles) as ["Astruc's clue," ]{.class_s4N4-0}from the work of the French physician who tried to dissect the Pentateuch into a sort of alphabet soup (see remarks on "Deutero-Dumpty" in the [Commentary on Genesis, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Gen. 16:7--11).

The "logic" behind this---if one can debase the word to that usage---is that since the word [Jehovah]{.class_s4N4-0} does appear in the book of Genesis in many passages, that a "J" writer (a writer who used the word [Jehovah]{.class_s4N4-0} for the Lord) wrote those passages, whereas an "E" writer wrote [Elohim ]{.class_s4N4-0}for [God ]{.class_s4N4-0}in other passages. Since many of the verses---Genesis 3:1--5, 16:7--13, 32:9, 22:11--12, 14, 28:16--17, etc.---exhibit the remarkable phenomenon of both Elohim and Jehovah appearing together, Astruc was driven to the conclusion (or obsessed with the fancy) that a "redactor" must have carefully put the two accounts together. This, []{#cTS.xhtml_page_138}naturally, introduces us to "R." It was this "X marks the spot" type of "scientific research" that led DeWette, Hupfeld, Graf, Wellhausen, and Kuenen to eventually present "E," "J," "P," "D," and "H" as the authors of the Pentateuch instead of Moses. The fiasco was thoroughly refuted in 1895 by William Henry Green of Princeton ([Unity of the Book of Genesis]{.class_s4N4-0}) and Robert Dick Wilson ([A Scientific Investigation of the Old Testament]{.class_s4N4-0}), but like Darwin's [Origin of the Species,]{.class_s4N4-0} the fairy tale still survives in the theological textbooks of 1900--2010.

Those gullible enough to subscribe to this alphabet hash can at least entertain themselves by the hour with such amusing thoughts as: "P" had Noah go in the ark, but "J" shut the door without "P's" permission. "J" then sent out the dove out and the raven, but "P" got the whole mess together so it looked like "M" (Moses) wrote it! (But as we have said elsewhere: "Watching Laurel and Hardy is just as funny and takes a lot less effort.")

The word [Jehovah]{.class_s4N4-0}---so magnified by Russell and Rutherford---is called the "tetragrammaton" by theologians; it consists of four Hebrew radicals: [Yod, He, Vau]{.class_s4N4-0} (or [Waw]{.class_s4N4-0}), and [He]{.class_s4N4-0}. The word is unpronounceable, as it stands, unless the Masoretic vowel points are added. The Jews read the word as [Adonai]{.class_s4N4-0}, inserting (as vowel points): [hateph, pathach,]{.class_s4N4-0} and [qames]{.class_s4N4-0}. Occasionally, one will find the vowel points for Elohim---[hateph, segol,]{.class_s4N4-0} and [hireq]{.class_s4N4-0}---inserted under the tetragrammaton, but the word is still read [Adonai ]{.class_s4N4-0}("Lord" or "Master"). The nearest that anyone can get to a pronunciation is [Jehovah]{.class_s4N4-0} (at least in the English language where the ["Yod"]{.class_s4N4-0} is converted to a "J"). Observe, [Jesus ]{.class_s4N4-0}is pronounced as "Yesus" (or "Yaysus") in nearly every language in the world but []{#cTS.xhtml_page_139}English. (Spaniards soften it further to the aspiration [Haysus ]{.class_s4N4-0}as in "San Jose"---"San Hosay.")

The Masons have suggested "Yoho" or "Yahoo" or "Yohoo" (or something like that), and the Liberal naturally prefers the word [Yaweh]{.class_s4N4-0}, as this degrades the Deity to a "tribal god" or "mountain god" on the back side of the desert---whom Moses invented. Readers should take note that whenever and wherever any writer, commentator, author, speaker, or reviser uses [Yaweh]{.class_s4N4-0}, the man has Liberal leanings and is automatically disqualified as an intelligent expositor (see 1 Sam. 2:30) .

To give you some idea of the magnitude of the verse, one should observe that:

1\. The[ NASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1963), recommended by Bob Jones University, has used the word [Jehovah ]{.class_s4N4-0}clear through the Old Testament (exactly as the [New World Translation,]{.class_s4N4-0} put out by the Watchtower Society) and then has [eliminated ]{.class_s4N4-0}the word entirely from the New Testament. This gives the reader the impression that there is no connection between Revelation 19:16 and our present text, Exodus 6:3. In keeping with this courtesy paid to Charles Taze Russell (1852--1916), the [NASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1963) has made Jesus a "created" god by its reading of John 1:18.

2\. The [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bible retains the Masoretic Hebrew method of handling the word by using ["LORD"]{.class_s4N2-0} where [Jehovah ]{.class_s4N4-0}appears and ["Lord" ]{.class_s4N2-0}where [Adonai,]{.class_s4N4-0} or others, appear.

3\. The Jehovah's Witnesses ("no Hellers") tie the text up with Psalm 83:18 and misread it to mean "whose name is JEHOVAH alone"; therefore, "no other name will do." This, of course, is an obvious boner, for eleven other names are used in the Bible, plus []{#cTS.xhtml_page_140}the ["name which is above every name"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Phil, 2:9)---["the name of JESUS"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Phil. 2:10)---not "Jehovah" (cf. for example, El, Eloah, Jah, Elohim, Elyon, etc.).

4\. A real orthodox Jew (Twentieth Century) believes that Jesus Christ was a demon-possessed, illegitimate child, and that he performed his "miracles" of healing by using the [tetragrammaton]{.class_s4N4-0}. He obtained this magical name by stealing it from the high priest.
