5\. Jesus Christ Himself claims this name in John 8:58 and 13:19, much to the dismay of the Russellites (see comments under Exod. 3:14).

The text, then, is a blockbuster.

The standard interpretation given by orthodox commentators is that the text should have read "\...but by my name, which I use[ in covenant relationship to my people, ]{.class_s4N4-0}was I not known, etc." This is interesting; so is badminton.

From the standpoint of common sense, anyone could see that Moses himself wrote the name [Jehovah ]{.class_s4N4-0}into the passages in Genesis when he wrote the book of Genesis. The name was certainly known to him, and he could have used it any way he saw fit. This is the most obvious solution to the problem, but because it lies apparent and on the surface, the scholars cannot rest content with it. Nothing could be that simple, or it would be unworthy of their "high calling."

However, if the sincere student thirsts for the "deeper things," the [King James]{.class_s4N4-0} text has some diamonds buried much further down than the shale and limestone dug up by "E," "D," "P," "J," "R," and Goldilocks. Did you notice the context of Exodus 6:3? ["And I appeared []{#cTS.xhtml_page_141}unto Abraham]{.class_s4N2-0}[\...." ]{.class_s4N7-1}Did you check the references? If you did, could you have failed to observe that every time God APPEARS to Abraham, Isaac, or Jacob, they address him as ["Lord" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Adon or Adonai) or ["GOD"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Elyon or Elohim)? And did you observe that only when the LORD finally APPEARS to Moses (Exod. 34:5--7) does He expound the nature and character of the word [Jehovah]{.class_s4N4-0}? How does one explain the fact that every major scholar from Origen to Thieme missed this Biblical truth which correctly exposits the passage?

Moral: where all Conservative writers, speakers, commentators, revisers, and critics ignore the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) text, ignore them cheerfully and confidently.

You see, "the riches" of God's word and the "deeper things" of God's word lie carefully hidden in the English Reformation text. Any miner who approaches these mineral deposits is intercepted by the Holy Spirit before he can get to the shaft (let alone the tunnel), and he is asked a simple question which every Bible critic has had to answer before he began mining operations: "Did you come to destroy this mine and [replace ]{.class_s4N4-0}it with [your tools,]{.class_s4N4-0} or did you come to brag on [God's]{.class_s4N4-0} riches (Job 28:1--28)?"

Whether the man answers audibly or not is immaterial (2 Chron. 16:9), for the Lord records the answer as soon as the response is made. The man then proceeds to the mine and labors. Before he has taken one step after answering the question above, God "writes him off" if the man's intent was to destroy the mine. Such a man can only rehash what he already knows or reinforce truths which anyone knows or destroy truths already revealed or alter the truth to match the mistakes he has already made or degrade the Christians who believe the word or destroy the common man's faith in the word []{#cTS.xhtml_page_142}or compromise with Neo-orthodoxy or compromise with[ "science"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 Tim. 6:20) or make a name for himself as a writer. He cannot find the "riches." If he stumbles on them, he usually does not recognize them, and if perchance he should recognize one out of ten, he cannot exposit it. Basically, there is no more difference in the attitude of Jean Astruc and Phillip Schaff (where they deal with the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}Old Testament text) than there is between Karl Barth and Emil Brunner.

"Astruc's clue" is about as important for an understanding of the Old Testament as a bag of golf clubs is for understanding hieroglyphics.

After stating the name, we find it repeated three more times: ["I am the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[\...I am the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[\...I am the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}["]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 6--8). Seven promises are made to the children of Israel in the passage, each beginning with[ "I will"]{.class_s4N2-0} (three in vs. 6, two in vs. 7, and two in vs. 8). Many spiritual applications can be found in the text.

First of all, notice that the Christian might be dead 400 years before his body gets home to Glory! The promises made to the "fathers" were never fulfilled in their [lifetime ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Heb. 11:13). It is not until Joshua 3--4 that an entrance is made designed to conquer and occupy the land of Canaan. The promise of the sinless, glorified body (1 Cor. 15:40--50) has been held in abeyance more than 1900 years, and yet, this promise was made to Christian men and women who heard Paul preach.

The devout reader will observe the peculiar wording of verse 6, which points not only to the outstretched arms of God's wrath in dealing with Pharaoh, but also the blood-bought redemption of the child of God under the arms that were outstretched on Golgotha. Every promise given here to Israel has some sort of spiritual application []{#cTS.xhtml_page_143}to the child of God in this dispensation, for in either setting, we are dealing with GRACE apart from the Law.

1\. ["I will bring you out from under the burdens"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 6; thus Matt. 11:28 in the New Testament followed by Paul in Gal. 5:1--15).

2\. ["I will rid you out of their bondage"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 6). That is, He will get rid of it. This is accomplished permanently in the Millennium (Isa. 14:1--7).

3\. ["I will redeem you" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 6; see comment above).

4\. ["I will take you to me for a people"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 7). This is fulfilled to the letter in Romans 9:26 where Paul takes a Jewish Tribulation passage and puts it right smack down on the Church Age (how did Cornelius Stam miss that one?).

5\. ["I will be to you a God" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 7), and so it came to pass (John 20:28).

6\. ["And I will bring you in unto the land"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 8). It will be done without a doubt (Rom. 8:17; Rev. 5:10; Gal. 3:29).

7\. ["I will give it you for an heritage" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 8, cf. Rom. 8:17, 22; Eph. 2:19--20).

The children of Israel, then, leave Egypt under the [Abrahamic covenant,]{.class_s4N4-0} not the [Sinaitic covenant. ]{.class_s4N4-0}They were redeemed by grace when they were bound in sin and worshipping the gods of this world. They are redeemed without works of any kind, and the only thing specifically "Jewish" about their redemption is that it is accompanied by apostolic ["signs, and wonders"]{.class_s4N2-0} (2 Cor. 12:12). [But the Ephesians were saved during the ministry of the apostles]{.class_s4N4-0} (how did Cor--nelius Stam miss that one, too?).

With all the promises in the book to offer the labor gangs, Moses goes back out by the work projects and tries some more street preaching. The response is beer cans, []{#cTS.xhtml_page_144}cigarette butts, rotten eggs, tomatoes, and handfuls of dirt.

6:9 "And Moses spake so unto the children of Israel: but they hearkened not unto Moses for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage.

10 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}spake unto Moses, saying,

11 Go in, speak unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land.

12 And Moses spake before the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, saying, Behold, the children of Israel have not hearkened unto me; how then shall Pharaoh hear me, who am of uncircumcised lips?

13 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, and gave them a charge unto the children of Israel, and unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt."

Moses' "gospel message" goes over like a message delivered to the Black Muslims by George Wallace. The oppressed sinner---in bondage to the elements of this world---can see nothing in a message that talks about the future; observe: ["I will\...I will\...I will\...I will\...I will"]{.class_s4N2-0} etc., in verses 6, 7, and 8.

It is hard to preach about Heaven to people who only are interested in this earth, even though every human being (except two) who walked across this earth had to get [buried in it. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Man is still obsessed with the idea that the only object worth living and dying for is "to make this world a better place to live in." Communists and modern Christians agree on this "fundamental of the faith." In today's materialistic society, the "afterlife" is considered to be an impossible subject to discuss, and many of the facts concerning it (Job 19; Isa. 53; Psa. 110; Rev. 20; []{#cTS.xhtml_page_145}1 Cor. 15; Eph. 4; John 3, 5, 10, 17, etc.) are treated as relative opinions which do not have enough substance to be worth serious investigation. The few "spiritual" people on earth, today, are taken up mainly with astrology, necromancy, Ouija boards, Satanism, water witching, ESP, marijuana, LSD and heroin "trips," and mental telepathy. That is, the only "spiritual" subjects in which people will become involved are subjects that deal with the dead spirit of man and the spirit of Satan (Eph. 2:1--3).

["They hearkened not unto Moses for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 9). The old saying states: "No man is happy on the rack." It is very difficult to talk about the peace, joy, and love that can be found in Christ when one is hanging upside down, naked (with hands and feet tied), and someone is beating the soles of his feet with a bamboo rod. A few hardy souls like Richard Wurmbrand and Haralan Popov have maintained the victory, and their testimony is that, at times, they were even rejoicing and happy. But these times were few and far between.

Israel reasons as King Darius reasoned: a "God" [who is God]{.class_s4N4-0} can deliver (Dan. 6:27), and if He can't, He is the wrong god. Better to go back to Ptah, Amun, Hut, Geb, Isis, Osiris, Ra, Sekhmet, Set, Thoth, Horus, Sobek, Buto, Ba, Shu, and Nut than daydream of a deliverance that will never come.

Most transients (Demotic spelling for "tramps") are interested in liquor money before going into a mission to hear the claims of Christ. Most junkies are not going to place their faith in anything that doesn't guarantee a "fix." Most Africans, South Americans, and Asiatics still suffer under economies which were the result of rejecting []{#cTS.xhtml_page_146}the Bible (A.D. 1500--1900). Consequently, hundreds of them tear up Gospel tracts, burn Bibles, torture Christians, and crawl on the ground before statues in an effort to "make the world a better place to live in."

"Good news! You can have forgiveness of sins and a home in Heaven!!" But what modern, civilized man raised on John Dewey, Bertrand Russell, Albert Einstein, and Charles Darwin would call [that ]{.class_s4N4-0}"good news"? The only good news the modern materialist knows of is more [peace ]{.class_s4N4-0}and more [money ]{.class_s4N4-0}on [earth]{.class_s4N4-0}. These are the gods of the Democratic and Republican parties (see [The Sure Word of Prophecy]{.class_s4N4-0}) .

Moses returns the second time to his "prayer closet" (vs. 12) and registers a valid complaint: ["The children of Israel have not hearkened unto me; how then shall Pharaoh\...."]{.class_s4N2-0} The scene is mentioned again in verses 28--30 of this same chapter after the genealogies have been listed. Moses---human as always (see remarks under 2:14)---reasons as any of us would under the same circumstances: "If you can't run with the [footmen, ]{.class_s4N4-0}how can you keep up with the [cavalry?"]{.class_s4N4-0} (Jer. 12:5). Or to update the matter: "If you can't whip Vietnam, what in the world are you going to do with Red China?"

["And the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[spake unto Moses and unto Aaron" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 13). Redemption must be accompanied by a prophet (Moses) and a priest (Aaron), so the "charge" is given to both of them as it was given to the Lord Jesus Christ (John 17:4).

6:14 "These be the heads of their fathers' houses: The sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel; Hanoch, []{#cTS.xhtml_page_147}and Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi: these be the families of Reuben.

15 And the sons of Simeon; Jemuel, and Jamin, and Ohad, and Jachin, and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanitish woman: these are the families of Simeon.

16 And these are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations; Gershon, and Kohath, and Merari: and the years of the life of Levi were an hundred thirty and seven years.

17 The sons of Gershon; Libni, and Shimi, according to their families.

18 And the sons of Kohath; Amram, and Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel: and the years of the life of Kohath were an hundred thirty and three years.

19 And the sons of Merari; Mahali and Mushi: these are the families of Levi according to their generations.

20 And Amram took him Jochebed his father's sister to wife; and she bare him Aaron and Moses: and the years of the life of Amram were an hundred and thirty and seven years."

During Moses' relapse and recovery, the Holy Spirit inserts a genealogy. The list, strangely enough, gives only the descendants of the tribes of Reuben, Simeon, and Levi. Williams is quite incorrect in assuming that this was done to show how much "grace" was manifest. (It is a common fault of conservative commentators to dodge the truth or ignore the revelation of a passage by presenting a devotional cliché stated as some broad generality with which no one can find fault, since it doesn't mean anything in particular to start with.)

["These be the heads of their fathers' houses"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 14). The first thing to observe is that the []{#cTS.xhtml_page_148}list of the[ "heads"]{.class_s4N2-0} [stops before JUDAH. ]{.class_s4N4-0}From Judah comes the real Priest (Heb. 3:1) and the real King (Gen. 49:10). Therefore, the list warns us that [redemption ]{.class_s4N4-0}(doctrinally) [is not complete in Exodus,]{.class_s4N4-0} and even though Moses and Aaron are both from the tribe of Levi, the real Deliverer is missing. Secondly, we should observe that only two verses are given for Reuben and Simeon (vss. 14--15), whereas ten verses are allotted to the tribe of Levi. The list, then, was plainly given to give a detailed account of the background of Aaron and Moses, as they are the central figures in all that is about to follow (6:26). Thirdly, it would be improper to list the tribe of Levi by itself, even if the intent of Moses was to give his own background; [Reuben ]{.class_s4N4-0}is given first in the lists in Genesis chapter 49 and Deuteronomy chapter 33, as he is given here.

Lists for comparison may be found in 1 Chronicles chapters 4--5, Genesis chapter 46, and Numbers chapter 26. (The best layout of the generations will be found in the genealogical charts in the front of the original edition of the [King James, ]{.class_s4N4-0}1611, [Authorized Version.]{.class_s4N4-0})

The remaining verses (17--20) are self-explanatory, and the only thing to be noticed is the corrupt reading of the corrupt [Septuagint]{.class_s4N4-0}, where the "assistants to the Holy Spirit" helped God out by adding ["and Miriam their sister"]{.class_s4N4-0} to verse 20. Very typical.

6:21 "And the sons of Izhar; Korah, and Nepheg, and Zichri.

22 And the sons of Uzziel; Mishael, and Elzaphan, and Zithri.

23 And Aaron took him Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab, sister of Naashon, to wife; and she bare him Nadab, and Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.

24 And the sons of Korah; Assir, and Elkanah, and Abiasaph: these are the families of the Korhites.

25 And Eleazar Aaron's son took him one of the daughters of Putiel to wife; and she bare him Phinehas: these are the heads of the fathers of the Levites according to their families.

26 These are that Aaron and Moses, to whom the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said, Bring out the children of Israel from the land of Egypt according to their armies.

27 These are they which spake to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring out the children of Israel from Egypt: these are that Moses and Aaron. "

The genealogy continues. The ["Korah"]{.class_s4N2-0} of verse 21 is the Korah of Numbers chapter 16. ["Izhar"]{.class_s4N2-0} is the brother of Amram, Moses' father (vs. 18). Aaron marries into the tribe of Judah through the daughter of Amminadab (Num. 2:3). The ["Elkanah"]{.class_s4N2-0} of verse 24 is a distant relative of Samuel (cf. Num. 26:11).

["These are that Aaron and Moses" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 26). The verse, as everything before it (beginning at vs. 14), seems to have been inserted by Joshua (see Josh. 24:26); however, Moses may have been the author since he is very often called upon to speak of himself in the third person (Num. 12:3; Exod. 2:15, etc.). But notice what follows.

["According to their armies"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 26). The expression sounds very much like Joshua (see remarks above). For the first time since they went down into Egypt, the sons of Jacob are going to be organized into []{#cTS.xhtml_page_150}a [political unit. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Although they have never had a military engagement (Exod. 13:17), it is apparent that they leave Egypt with weapons (see Exod. 17:8--13).

6:28 "And it came to pass on the day when the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}spake unto Moses in the land of Egypt,

29 That the LORD spake unto Moses, say--ing, I am the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}: speak thou unto Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say unto thee.

30 And Moses said before the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, Be--hold, I am of uncircumcised lips, and how shall Pharaoh hearken unto me?"

Verse 28 picks up the narrative again where it began in verse 1, and the Holy Spirit repeats (in an abbreviated form) the charge of verses 1--11 (in vs. 29), plus Moses' complaint (vs. 30) found in verse 12.

[]{#cWD.xhtml}

# Chapter 7 {.heading_s4G-0}

7:1 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, See, I have made thee a god to Pharaoh: and Aaron thy brother shall be thy prophet.

2 Thou shalt speak all that I command thee: and Aaron thy brother shall speak unto Pharaoh, that he send the children of Israel out of his land.

3 And I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt.

4 But Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you, that I may lay my hand upon Egypt, and bring forth mine armies, and my people the children of Israel, out of the land of Egypt by great judgments.

5 And the Egyptians shall know that I am the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, when I stretch forth mine hand upon Egypt, and bring out the children of Israel from among them.

6 And Moses and Aaron did as the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}commanded them, so did they.

7 And Moses was fourscore years old, and Aaron fourscore and three years old, when they spake unto Pharaoh."

Again, the material of chapters 5--6 is repeated. At all times, in reading these chapters, we are impressed with the gravity of the occasion and its momentous import to the Jews as a nation. The coming of Aaron and Moses for this work is mentioned in Micah and in the Psalms; Moses (alone) is mentioned in connection with this work [eight times]{.class_s4N4-0} in the book of Psalms,[ four times]{.class_s4N4-0} in the book of Hebrews, and [twice ]{.class_s4N4-0}in the Gospel of John. The Exodus itself is referred to (directly or indirectly) more than fifty times in the Bible.

["See, I have made thee a god to Pharaoh"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 1). The corrupt [ASV, RV, RSV, NRSV, NASV, ]{.class_s4N4-0}etc., have wrongly translated the passage as "made thee as God," which, of course, is [not ]{.class_s4N4-0}the sense. There is no indication that Pharaoh was a monotheist or that ANYONE in Egypt was. ["A god"]{.class_s4N2-0} would be the correct designation for Moses in his role as God's representative, and the wording[ "as God"]{.class_s4N4-0} is a private interpretation divorced from the historical setting of the passage. The[ Septuagint ]{.class_s4N4-0}has added[ "to him" ]{.class_s4N4-0}to verse 2, but one would expect this of the conflated forgery which still survives in the "newer" translations.

["I will harden Pharaoh's heart"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3) has been altered to ["make stubborn" ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Moffatt, 1924) and ["make obstinate"]{.class_s4N4-0} ([American Translation]{.class_s4N4-0}, 1931), but the[ Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} of 1611 preserves the Hebrew Masoretic text---["harden."]{.class_s4N2-0}

["Multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3). The "signs and wonders" in this case are plagues and judgments.

1\. Water turned to blood (chap. 7).

2\. Frogs brought up on the land (chap. 8).

3\. Dust turned to lice (chap. 8).

4\. Flies brought on the land (chap. 8).

5\. Cattle slain (chap. 9).

6\. Boils and blains (chap. 9).

7\. Hailstones (chap. 9).

8\. Locusts devouring the crops (chap. 10).

9\. Darkness over the land (chap. 10).

10\. The death of the firstborn (chap. 12).

The first two plagues in each series---1 & 2, 4 & 5, and 7 & 8, receive warnings, while the third in each series comes without a warning---3, 6, & 9. Inverted, the plagues []{#cWD.xhtml_page_153}have some relation to each other: 1 with 10, 2 with 9, 3 with 8, 4 with 7, and 5 with 6.

["And the Egyptians shall know that I am the ]{.class_s4N2-1}[Lord]{.class_s4N8-1}["]{.class_s4N2-1}[ (vs. 5---cf. 5:2 and comments). The passage is amply fulfilled in Exodus 9:20, 10:7, and 11:3.]{.class_s4N3-0}

7:8 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying,

9 When Pharaoh shall speak unto you, saying, Shew a miracle for you: then thou shalt say unto Aaron, Take thy rod, and cast it before Pharaoh, and it shall become a serpent.

10 And Moses said Aaron went in unto Pharaoh, and they did so as the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had commanded: and Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh, and before his servants, and it became a serpent.

11 Then Pharaoh also called the wise men and the sorcerers; now the magicians of Egypt, they also did in like manner with their enchantments.

12 For they cast down every man his rod, and they became serpents: but Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods.

13 And he hardened Pharaoh's heart, that he hearkened not unto them; as the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had said."

Back go Moses and Aaron to the palace. They gain an audience with Pharaoh who is reclining on a leopard-skin divan, sipping grape juice (Gen. 40:10--11).

"Good morning, your majesty!"

"Well, well, well, look who's here! Walter Reuther and John L. Lewis! Well, what can I do for you this morning, gentlemen?"

"Ah, the Lord God of the Hebrews\...."

"Agh! The Hebrews, the 'Jebrews'! The only reason I haven't thrown both of you in the clink is because you amuse me. Now, you can't get your people a pay raise, and you can't even keep me from taking 95% out of their check for the United Drive, so who do you sklonks think you are anyway?"

Aaron: "We're 'gods' and we represent Jehovah."

"Oh, so you're 'gods,' are you? Well, the gods of Genesis chapter 6 were kin to the [Tanim ]{.class_s4N4-0}of Job 7:12 (see comments on Psa. 104:29 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Psalms,]{.class_s4N4-0} Vol. II, Peter S. Ruckman, hereafter referred to as the [Commentary on Psalms]{.class_s4N4-0}---with volume number); and even if they weren't the [Nachash ]{.class_s4N4-0}of Genesis 3:1, they could work miracles. You got any miracles up your sleeve?" [Pharaoh stumbles into Exodus 7:9 without knowing it]{.class_s4N4-0}!

["And Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 10). (See the comments under 4:2.)

Down goes the rod and off across the floor slithers the prettiest king snake you ever laid eyes on. (Aaron's rod blossoms later and identifies Aaron as a type of the great High Priest of Heb. 3:1.) The king snake displays the ["many crowns"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Revelation 19:12 (!) and coils up at Moses' feet.

Pharaoh laughs. He claps his hands and out of the "wings" step "[Jannes and Jambres"]{.class_s4N2-0} (2 Tim. 3:8). "Can you duplicate that?" asks Pharaoh. "These snollygosters just slapped down a shepherd's rod, and it turned into a snake; let's see you match it!"

["Jannes and Jambres"]{.class_s4N2-0} bow, exit, and return with three "sorcerers' apprentices." [Slap!]{.class_s4N4-0} Down go five rods, and there are five of the prettiest pygmy rattlers you ever saw in your life! Pharaoh rocks back on his elbows []{#cWD.xhtml_page_155}and roars, "You're gods, are you?! Haw haw, hoo hoo, hahahaha, ho ho!! Why, children, we got magicians down here that began where Blackstone and Houdini quit. Who do you think you're trying to kid?!"

Moses sends up a Nehemiah prayer (Neh. 2:4): "Oh, God, Oh Lord, what do I do [now?!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

The Lord says evenly: "Keep your shirt on. The show isn't over yet."

The king snake makes for one of the rattlers---bap! The rattler strikes him. [Bip!]{.class_s4N4-0} Two of the rattlers attack him. The fangs click and flash, and the king snake is bitten four times as all the rattlers turn on him. One of them misses and bites another rattler. The king snake arches and dives for the nearest rattler. [Ulp!]{.class_s4N4-0} Down he goes! In three seconds, there are rattlers headed for the doors, and the king snake is wrapping around them, crushing them, and then swallowing them whole. In five minutes, there is nothing on the floor but a bloated and stuffed king snake. Aaron picks him up by the tail, and there he stands with a shepherd's rod in his hand---about twice as thick as it was before!

(There is considerable difference of opinion about the trick which the magicians performed. The most logical explanation is that they had charmed snakes to start with and had brought them in so rigid that they [looked like rods. ]{.class_s4N4-0}However, this is certain: Aaron had what the Indians call "strong medicine," and it was stronger than Pharaoh's.)

["And he hardened Pharaoh's heart, that he hearkened not unto them; as the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N8-2}[had said"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 13). Pharaoh reacts to the miracle exactly as the ecumenical council of Acts 4:17 (see comments in the [Commentary on Acts]{.class_s4N4-0}) reacted to the miracle of Acts 3:1--[]{#cWD.xhtml_page_156}10. He cannot answer it, he cannot explain it, he cannot deny it, but he certainly is not interested in [promoting ]{.class_s4N4-0}it.

Pharaoh sits up on the divan and watches the slaughter on the floor in front of him. The royal visage clouds. After it is over, he leans forward on one hand, resting his elbow on his knee. He glares darkly at Moses and Aaron. ["Jannes and Jambres"]{.class_s4N2-0} are as quiet as a sick sow in a snowstorm.

Moses feels his blood coming back into his face, and his knees stop knocking long enough for him to say:

"You see there! The Lord God of the Hebrews\...."

"SHUT UP!" says Pharaoh.

Everyone stands stockstill while the King of Egypt gets up. He looks down at the floor where the snakes had performed, he looks at ["Jannes and Jambres"]{.class_s4N2-0} (like a mullet fisherman looks at a catfish), he looks back at Moses and Aaron, he looks out the window at the slave laborers, and he then turns and says to Moses and Aaron: ["Git! ]{.class_s4N4-0}Get outta here 'fore I lose my temper."

Exit the "god" and his "prophet."

7:14 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Pharaoh's heart is hardened, he refuseth to let the people go.

15 Get thee unto Pharaoh in the morning; lo, he goeth out unto the water; and thou shalt stand by the river's brink against he come; and the rod which was turned to a serpent shalt thou take in thine hand.

16 And thou shalt say unto him, The [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}God of the Hebrews hath sent me unto thee, saying, Let my people go, that they may serve me in the wilderness: and, behold, hitherto thou wouldest not hear.

17 Thus saith the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, In this thou shalt know that I am the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}: behold, I will smite with the rod that []{#cWD.xhtml_page_157}is in mine hand upon the waters which are in the river, and they shall be turned to blood.

18 And the fish that is in the river shall die, and the river shall stink; and the Egyptians shall lothe to drink of the water of the river."

If the reader of the Scriptures desires to get the true picture, he must abandon all false modesty and with it all the terminology used by the major commentators who attempt to describe the scene. One will discover that no matter which commentator is used (in Germany or America or England), in the Eighteenth, Nineteenth, or Twentieth Century, that all of them just ape each other. Clothed in theological terminology, devotional terminology, scholastic terminology, Seminary expressions, and writer's clichés, the commentators absolutely refuse to translate the truth of the account into [any contemporary age. ]{.class_s4N4-0}It was as though all of the major commentators had conspired beforehand to limit the words of the Holy Spirit to the remote past, and had agreed to resort to every trick known to man to prevent their readers from grasping the meaning of the passages.

Aaron, Moses, Pharaoh, Jannes, and Jambres were [real people. ]{.class_s4N4-0}To make them void of sarcasm, mistakes, humor, tears, anger, clumsiness, hypocrisy, indignation, fear, laughter, and duplicity is to present Bible truth as a museum of Natural History (which one child called ["a dead circus"]{.class_s4N4-0}). If Pharaoh didn't laugh and ["Jannes and Jambres"]{.class_s4N2-0} didn't blush, Moses didn't write Exodus. And if Moses didn't leave Pharaoh this time feeling like "top man on the totem pole," then the text doesn't mean what it says. (See the comments that deal with this type of exposition in the Preface to [The Acts of The Apostles.]{.class_s4N4-0})

["Get thee unto Pharaoh in the morning; lo, he goeth out unto the water" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 15). Pharaoh, as his daughter (2:5), seems to be in the habit of taking a daily dip in the sacred river---the Nile. ["Against he come" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is the old English way of saying "against his coming," meaning "set yourself across his path so you will be opposite him when he shows up."

["And thou shalt say to him\...hitherto thou wouldest not hear"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 16). Up until now, Pharaoh has been unwilling to listen to the demands of Moses and Aaron.

["I will smite with the rod that is in mine hand upon the waters which are in the river, and they shall be turned to blood\...and the Egyptians shall lothe to drink of the water of the river" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 17--18). This is one of the signs promised to Moses on Mt. Sinai (4:9), and we have already commented on it. The phenomenon will reoccur during the Great Tribulation (Rev. 16:3--6), but all of the commentators miss the references, including Bullinger. Rosenmuller does not believe the account, so he conjectures that verse 17 should have said: "shall be the COLOR of blood." Such a private interpretation destroys the sense of the rest of the chapter, for fish do not die from "colored water," and colored water does not ["stink"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 18 cf. vs. 21) unless it has more foreign matter in it than "color."

Clarke remarks on the great [purity ]{.class_s4N4-0}of the Nile and how the Turks would "eat salt in order to aggravate their thirst for it." Traveling pilgrims going to Mecca and back bragged about how [wonderful ]{.class_s4N4-0}the drinking water of the Nile was if we are to believe Rev. Clarke (Commentary, Vol. I, pg. 325). Unfortunately, our dear brother---like thousands of his friends---forgets the "pure" and beautiful []{#cWD.xhtml_page_159}waters of the Nile, and two paragraphs later (on the same page) drops a cat out of the bag that lands on its back: "As the water of the Nile is known to be[ very thick and muddy\..."!]{.class_s4N4-0} (All of the material was borrowed from a Mr. Harmer, and one cannot help but feel that if Clarke had stuck to the text instead of "looking to Aaron as a mouthpiece," he might have made out better.)

7:19 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}spake unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Take thy rod, and stretch out thine hand upon the waters of Egypt, upon their streams, upon their rivers, and upon their ponds, and upon all their pools of water, that they may become blood; and that there may be blood throughout all the land of Egypt, both in vessels of wood, and in vessels of stone.

20 And Moses and Aaron did so, as the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}commanded; and he lifted up the rod, and smote the waters that were in the river, in the sight of Pharaoh, and in the sight of his servants; and all the waters that were in the river were turned to blood.

21 And the fish that was in river died; and the river stank, and the Egyptians could not drink of the water of the river; and there was blood throughout all the land of Egypt.

22 And the magicians of Egypt did so with their enchantments: and Pharaoh's heart was hardened, neither did he hearken unto them; as the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had said.

23 And Pharaoh turned and went into his house, neither did he set his heart to this also.

24 And all the Egyptians digged round about the river for water to drink; for they could not drink of the water of the river.

25 And seven days were fulfilled, after that the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had smitten the river."

At 5 A.M., Moses and Aaron go down to the riverside. An hour later, the King's chariot reins in, Pharaoh and his retinue dismount, and Pharaoh sashays down to the brink. In the early morning dawn, he does not spot the two figures ahead of him till he almost bumps into them.

"Oh! Oh, it's [you ]{.class_s4N4-0}again, is it? Haven't you characters hit the road yet? Waiting for an invitation or what?"

Moses passes his rod to Aaron, raises his own hand, and says (vs. 16), "Thus saith the Lord God of the Hebrews, 'Let my people go!'"

"And suppose I[ don't?"]{.class_s4N4-0} says Pharaoh.

"Then," says Moses, "Heavy-heavy hangs over thy head!"

"Well, son," says Pharaoh, "You just go ahead and dump the whole load on me; it's all right, I can take it. I own everything from here to Tunisia and from here to Addis Ababa; lower the boom if you want to!"

"All right," says Moses, "Presto-chango! [DOOLB OT NRUT!"]{.class_s4N4-0} (Hebrew, backwards, for "turn to blood".)

Up goes Aaron's rod and up goes Moses' hand (vs. 19). There is a long tearing whistle like a 105mm shell going overhead, and suddenly, the whole river turns [blood red]{.class_s4N4-0} right at Pharaoh's feet. For a moment, his majesty's party thinks that it is natural phenomenon and perhaps the sun has risen suddenly to reflect on the surface (see 2 Kings 3:20--23). However, they quickly notice that two tributaries behind them (still in the dark gulleys of the early morning shadows) are a deep, dark[ red.]{.class_s4N4-0}

Pharaoh grins wryly and calls ["Jannes and Jambres"]{.class_s4N2-0} over. ([Satan always counterfeits the blood as []{#cWD.xhtml_page_161}soon as it shows up.]{.class_s4N4-0}) "See what those monkeys did?" says Pharaoh, "You do it!"

["Jannes and Jambres" ]{.class_s4N2-0}go about forty yards off to a stone well; they haul a bucket up out of it and bring it back to Pharaoh. Before the astonished gaze of Aaron and Moses, they gesture over the bucket and cry, ["Doolb ot nrut!" ]{.class_s4N4-0}The water turns red, and the "transubstantiation" is more convincing than any "mass" performed by any priest in the last eighteen centuries.

"Now, you Geek, what do you think of that?" sneers Pharaoh.

"Lord God of mercy, I don't know what to think of that!" Moses says (but he says this in his heart to God). "Lord? Lord? What in the ever-lovin-blue-eyed world do I do NOW?"

The Spirit of God breathes into Moses' ear,[ "Tell him to drink it."]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Now, take a swallow, your majesty!" says Moses.

Pharaoh obtains a canteen cup and scoops some water out of the bucket. He and ["Jannes and Jambres"]{.class_s4N2-0} all drink, smack their lips, wipe their mouths, and Pharaoh says, "Best spring water I tasted in many a day; yessiree, almost as good as that water out in Arkansas!"

"I didn't mean out of the bucket," says Moses, "I mean over there! [Out of the river."]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Why, sure thing," says Pharaoh, "its all the same stuff. Who are you tryin' to kid, buster?"

Pharaoh takes his canteen cup, goes over the bulrushes and flags, stoops down, and gets him a cupful of river water. He stands up, raises it to his lips, and then his face suddenly pales as he smells the content. He motions for the magicians to join him. They huddle for []{#cWD.xhtml_page_162}a moment, and Pharaoh says, "Something's gone wrong, boys! THIS AIN'T WATER!"

The magicians insist, "Sure it's water! It's bound to be water. He's just trying to pull the wool over your eyes. \'Course it's water."

"All right then," says Pharaoh grimly, ["YOU drink it."]{.class_s4N4-0}

Jannes seizes the cup, tips back his head, and takes a good long swig. His face turns purple, and he spews the "water" out all over the ground in front of him. "Mother of Osiris!" he gasps, "That's [b-b-blood!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Better leave it alone," cautions Pharaoh. "I ain't superstitious, but you remember that legend about that man and that woman in the garden, and all that? You know, Romans are the only people I know of anywhere who drink that stuff. Better leave it alone."

Back behind them, in the mist of the early morning, they hear Aaron's voice: ["You gonna let 'em go?"]{.class_s4N4-0}

Pharaoh returns to Moses and Aaron and manages, "Yeah, yea, sure we're gonna let 'em go---in a few weeks. But how about this water? It'll kill the fish if it stays like that. How 'bout turning it back to water---okay?"

Aaron says, "We'll do it in seven days, your majesty, and by that time all of your people will know that we mean business. [Shalom Elechim!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

["And seven days were fulfilled, after that the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N8-2}[had smitten the river"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 25). The waters that were polluted were in canals, streams, lakes, reservoirs, and containers; nothing was said about wells, fountains, or springs (vs. 19); and consequently, the Egyptians were able to survive by digging for water. The comment of Keil and Delitzsch on verse 18 is a classic and should be preserved forever in the[ Conservative Commentator's Hall of Fame]{.class_s4N4-0} as the perfect representative of []{#cWD.xhtml_page_163}modern conservative scholarship. Of ["the river shall stink"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 18), Keil and Delitzsch have told us: ["This SEEMS TO INDICATE putrefaction"! ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Yes, it might at that.)

Pharaoh has an "ecology problem" that would make the problems of Los Angeles, Cincinnati, and Birmingham look like no problem at all. (The same pollution problem will occur universally in the future. See details in [Commentary on Revelation, ]{.class_s4N4-0}Rev. 9, 14, 16, and 19.)

There is no indication that Aaron and Moses turned the bloody Nile back into water. It dissolves, evidently, and runs off into the Mediterranean.

Pharaoh's response is similar to the response of Europeans and Americans in the Tribulation (Rev. 16:9--11). He hardens his heart (vs. 22), he refuses to take the matter to heart (vs. 23) and he refuses to hearken to Aaron and Moses (vs. 22).

The word spreads quickly. All of the commentators seem to assume that because the Holy Spirit makes no direct reference to the effect of the miracle that things kind of "develop slowly" until the people regain their confidence in Aaron and Moses, but you can be certain that as sure as "God made little green apples," every Jew from Memphis to Pithom knew about the bloody river by the time the sun went down. Nearly every man, woman, and child in Egypt depended on the Nile for either bathing water, washing water (clothes), drinking water, irrigation, or fish and commerce. From now on, the eyes of Israel and Egypt are on [Moses ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [Aaron]{.class_s4N4-0}. Pharaoh is quite conscious of this, for with power in his hands to execute either of them, he does not threaten them with death until chapter 11.

[]{#cZR.xhtml}

# Chapter 8 {.heading_s4G-0}

8:1 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}spake unto Moses, Go unto Pharaoh, and say unto him, Thus saith the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, Let my people go, that they may serve me.

2 And if thou refuse to let them go, behold, I will smite all thy borders with frogs:

3 And the river shall bring forth frogs abundantly, which shall go up and come into thine house, and into thy bedchamber, and upon thy bed, and into the house of thy serv--ants, and upon thy people, and into thine ovens, and into thy kneadingtroughs:

4 And the frogs shall come up both on thee, and upon thy people, and upon all thy servants.

5 And the LORD spake unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch forth thine hand with thy rod over the streams, over the rivers, and over the ponds, and cause frogs to come up upon the land of Egypt.

6 And Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt; and the frogs came up, and covered the land of Egypt.

7 And the magicians did so with their enchantments, and brought up frogs upon the land of Egypt."

Seven days later, Pharaoh runs smack into Moses and Aaron again, and they haven't changed their tune a bit. Here it comes again:

["Thus saith the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[, Let my people go" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 1). This time, a [plague of frogs]{.class_s4N4-0} is promised (cf. Rev. 16:13), and we take for granted from verses 4--6 that Pharaoh's answer to verse 1 was: "Go on! You're bluffin' this time!"

Up goes the rod! Something like a freight train sails through the air over the palace. All the guards who had []{#cZR.xhtml_page_165}"time overseas" mistake it for a 205mm, and they "hit the dirt" (which, in this case, is [marble tile]{.class_s4N4-0}). There is a croaking and a buzzing, and then the sound of "little feet"---slippery, wet little feet---all over the palace. Pharaoh evacuates the throne room. Moses and Aaron squash ten frogs trying to get down the palace steps. The work details have to stop as frogs are all over the equipment. They are in the brick-kilns; they are in the bedrooms, bathrooms, and living rooms; they jump out of the glove compartments of the cars; they croak in the dresser drawers; they hop out of the ovens; and they drop off the TV aerials. People are slipping on mashed frogs all over the street, all over the sidewalks, up and down the hallways, through the corridors, and on the tennis courts. There is pandemonium.

To placate Pharaoh, ["Jannes and Jambres" ]{.class_s4N2-0}duplicate the trick, but this only makes matters worse (vs. 7). When they try to drive the frogs back into the river, they find that their magic is limited to IMITATION; they cannot produce an [original version]{.class_s4N4-0} any more than their modern counterparts who published the [ASV, NASV, RV, RSV, NRSV, Good News, New English Bible,]{.class_s4N4-0} and the brand new, New, NEW, NEW, NEW, NEW---get them while they're hot, ladies and gentlemen! Right this way!---NEW AMERICAN REVISED STANDARD [DUNGHILL!]{.class_s4N4-0}

8:8 "Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron, and said, Intreat the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, that he may take away the frogs from me, and from my people; and I will let the people go, that they may do sacrifice unto the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}.

9 And Moses said unto Pharaoh, Glory over me: when shall I intreat for thee, and for thy servants, and []{#cZR.xhtml_page_166}for thy people, to destroy the frogs from thee and thy houses, that they may remain in the river only?

10 And he said, To morrow. And he said, Be it according to thy word: that thou mayest know that there is none like unto the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}our God.

11 And the frogs shall depart from thee, and from thy houses, and from thy servants, and from thy people; they shall remain in the river only.

12 And Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh: and Moses cried unto the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}because of the frogs which he had brought against Pharaoh.

13 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}did according to the word of Moses; and the frogs died out of the houses, out of the villages, and out of the fields.

14 And they gathered them together upon heaps: and the land stank.

15 But when Pharaoh saw that there was respite, he hardened his heart, and hearkened not unto them; as the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had said."

After a week with the frogs, Pharaoh is ready to meet Moses' demands; at least he pretends to be ready:[ "I will let the people go, that they may do sacrifice unto the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 8). If anyone heard Pharaoh say these words to Aaron and Moses, he would have come to the conclusion, [later]{.class_s4N4-0}, that his majesty was a bald-faced liar; Pharaoh does NOT let the children of Israel go.

["And Moses said unto Pharaoh, Glory over me: when shall I intreat for thee\...?" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 9). Moses' expression has been altered to "explain thyself to me" (Bullinger), "I submit to thy will" ([Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0}), ["Have thou this glory over me"]{.class_s4N4-0} ([RV]{.class_s4N4-0}, 1885), ["You may have the honor of saying"]{.class_s4N4-0} (Moffatt, []{#cZR.xhtml_page_167}1924), and "Appoint thou the time unto me" (Clarke). Anything but believe the text.

None of the commentators are willing to apply the phrase ["Glory over me"]{.class_s4N2-0} to[ the glory that WAS over Moses, ]{.class_s4N4-0}and none would tolerate such an interpretation even with ["Intreat the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 8) standing right in the context of the request. The source of Bullinger's feelings---as those of Clarke, Ellicott, Lange, Moffatt, et al.---is the same old font of corruption which we run into time after time in Bible exposition---the post-Christian[ Septuagint. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Here, the word[ "paar"]{.class_s4N4-0} (Heb.) has been altered to read[ "baer."]{.class_s4N4-0} This conjectural emendation by LeClerc is found also in the Roman Catholic [Vulgate ]{.class_s4N4-0}as it stands in the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901) and the [NASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1963)[ ]{.class_s4N4-0}and other Roman Catholic-Protestant "Bibles." Moses' reference is to [the Deity who will get the glory from the miracles, ]{.class_s4N4-0}and the only sensible reading of the passage is that Moses is invoking the Deity to witness to Pharaoh's promise that he will let the children of Israel go. The [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) text is far superior to its nearest rival in this matter.

["And he said, To morrow" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 10). The man who led me to Christ (Hugh Pyle) has a famous sermon which he calls ["One More Night with the Frogs." ]{.class_s4N4-0}The idea behind it is simple and effective: the sinner wants one more night with sin and filth no matter how defiling and slimy it is, and even though he is sick to death and fed up with "frogs," he still cannot bear the thought of giving them up.

["And he said, To morrow\..."]{.class_s4N2-0}! The evangelistic song says: "Why not come to Him NOW?"[ "Now is the accepted time; behold, NOW is the day of salvation"]{.class_s4N2-0} (2 Cor. 6:2)!

"Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow, creeps in this petty pace from day to day\...etc." (Shakespeare, Macbeth). [Tomorrow ]{.class_s4N4-0}never comes. ["Mañana"]{.class_s4N4-0} is "today" when it gets here, and it is "yesterday" after it is gone. The word[ tomorrow]{.class_s4N4-0} moans through the history of man like a fog horn off the coasts of Maine: "Tomorrow, tomorrow, tomorrow, tomorrow!"

As Talmage has said: ["Tomorrow]{.class_s4N4-0} is the word that robs Heaven of its fairest jewels and makes Hell the capital of immortal souls." Solomon cautioned: ["Boast not thyself of to morrow; for thou knowest not what a day may bring forth"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Prov. 27:1). If all the male Southerners got saved who intended to get saved "some day," Hell wouldn't have 500 white, male Southerners in it; but it is always "tomorrow." ["One more night with the frogs."]{.class_s4N4-0}

["And he said, Be it according to thy word\...And the frogs shall depart\...they shall remain in the river only"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 10--11). The reader will observe that, up to here, the Israelites are getting plagued just like the Egyptians. It is not until the flies come (8:22) that God segregates His people from Egypt as far as the judgments are concerned. Israelites have to dig for water, kick frogs around, and scratch lice just like Pharaoh's people.

["And Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh: and Moses cried unto the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[ ]{.class_s4NB}[because of the frogs\...And the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[ ]{.class_s4NB}[did according to the word of Moses"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 12--13). The contrast is striking. Over forty times in Exodus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy, it says that Moses did as God commanded him (Exod. 39:1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31, etc.). Here, the Lord does[ "according to the word of Moses."]{.class_s4N2-0} My, how the Pope wishes that he had this power! The private interpretation of Matthew []{#cZR.xhtml_page_169}16:19 is designed to give [this power]{.class_s4N4-0}[ ]{.class_s4N2-0}to the "Popes," but of course, anyone with an ounce of sense knows that the only power a "Pope" has is [political ]{.class_s4N4-0}or [demoniacal]{.class_s4N4-0}. No Pope who ever lived had the [spiritual power ]{.class_s4N4-0}given to "Pappy" Reveal of the Evansville Rescue Mission (Evansville, Indiana), and when it came to "power with God and with man," John Paton (Scottish missionary to the New Hebrides: 1824--1907) had more power than the College of Cardinals and "Catholic Action" combined.

["And the land stank"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 14). It must have! Seven days of dead fish followed by seven days (or longer) of smashed frogs is not going to make a river town smell like a petunia. ["But\...Pharaoh\...hardened his heart\...as the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[ ]{.class_s4NB}[had said" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 15). (See comments on the foreknowledge of God under 3:19.)

By now, it should be apparent that Pharaoh is a liar. He inherits this from his father (John 8:44). His promises are worthless, and he (as his father) is not able to "deliver the goods" to those whom he proselytes.

8:16 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch out thy rod, and smite the dust of the land, that it may become lice throughout all the land of Egypt.

17 And they did so; for Aaron stretched out his hand with his rod, and smote the dust of the earth, and it became lice in man, and in beast; all the dust of the land became lice throughout all the land of Egypt.

18 And the magicians did so with their enchantments to bring forth lice, but they could not: so there were lice upon man, and upon beast.

19 Then the magicians said unto Pharaoh, This is the finger of God: and Pharaoh's heart was hardened, []{#cZR.xhtml_page_170}and he hearkened not unto them; as the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had said."

The scholars run from the Bible as quickly as possible and suggest "sand fleas," "gnats," and "mosquitoes" instead of the text. The mosquitoes fly out of Alexandria (as usual) and are found in the "[LXX]{.class_s4N4-0}," which was composed by Origen, Clement, Symmachus, Pamphilus, Aquila, and Theodotian many years after the New Testament was completed. Rosenmuller, Gesenius, Boothroyd, Keil, Kalisch, and Geddes all subscribe to the Alexandrian viewpoint. The corrupt [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901), recommended by Wilbur Smith and other "Conservatives," retains[ "lice"]{.class_s4N2-0} in the text but puts "fleas" in the margin. (The careful reader will observe that the old [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}is a bridge between the Holy Bible \[1611\] and the [RSV ]{.class_s4N4-0}\[1952\]; [the marginal readings of the ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}are incorporated into[ the text of the RSV. ]{.class_s4N4-0}See, for example, the attacks on the Deity of Christ under Zech. 12:10, 13:6; Mic. 5:2; Isa. 7:14; and many others. )

["Aaron stretched out his hand with his rod, and smote the dust of the earth, and it became lice in man, and in beast; all the dust of the land became lice throughout all the land of Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 17). This is "endsville" for ["Jannes and Jambres."]{.class_s4N2-0} They could counterfeit a creation by secondary means (rods into snakes), but now they are called upon to repeat [the original creation]{.class_s4N4-0} of Genesis 2:19, where we are told that the animals---as man---came from ["the dust of the ground"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Gen. 2:7). Neither Clarke, Dummelow, Keil, Ellicott, Delitzsch, Lange, Williams, nor the [Wycliffe Commentary ]{.class_s4N4-0}can find the real significance to the miracle. The [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0} goes right on commenting on []{#cZR.xhtml_page_171}verse 19 without even hinting at the meaning in either verse.

["This is the finger of God" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 19). Williams postulates the ancient heresy that only God can produce LIFE; however, this is soundly refuted in Revelation 13:15. Satan can not only produce "life," but he can give BIRTH (Gen. 3:15; John 8:44; 2 Thess. 2:3--4). But all of this is beyond the mental and spiritual grasp of the modern "Fundamentalist" who stopped where Scofield dropped him in 1909. The miracle of Exodus 8:18--19 is not that God [produced life,]{.class_s4N4-0} it is that Aaron and Moses repeated the original act of creation---living animals out of dust. This the magicians cannot do, and this explains their apology to Pharaoh: ["This is the finger of God" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 19). ["The finger of God"]{.class_s4N2-0} is defined by the Holy Spirit as the Holy Spirit Himself in Luke 11:20 and Psalm 8:3. (All of the major commentators missed the cross-references, and none of them were able to connect the English verses, because all of them followed Origen's allegorical method and thought the ["finger" ]{.class_s4N2-0}was an ["anthropopatheia."]{.class_s4N4-0}) Clarke writes thirteen feeble lines on this great truth without being able to find the Holy Spirit's interpretation, and then he proceeds to turn out 45 lines on "what kind of fly" it was that flew through Exodus 8:21. (Was he following instructions from Beelzebub?)

["This is the finger of God" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 19). The believer sees that the last miracle has made [monotheists ]{.class_s4N4-0}out of ["Jannes and Jambres."]{.class_s4N2-0} Although they will not humble themselves to call God by the name revealed to Moses (the LORD---Jehovah), they, at least, recognized the fact that an original creation cannot be produced by Isis, Horus, Seb, Nut, Osiris, Ra, Thoth, et al.

8:20 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh; lo, he cometh forth to the water; and say unto him, Thus saith the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, Let my people go, that they may serve me.

21 Else, if thou wilt not let my people go, behold, I will send swarms of flies upon thee, and upon thy servants, and upon thy people, and into thy houses: and the houses of the Egyptians shall be full of swarms of flies, and also the ground whereon they are.

22 And I will sever in that day the land of Goshen, in which my people dwell, that no swarms of flies shall be there; to the end thou mayest know that I am the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}in the midst of the earth.

23 And I will put a division between my people and thy people: to morrow shall this sign be.

24 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}did so; and there came a grievous swarm of flies into the house of Pharaoh, and into his servants' houses, and into all the land of Egypt: the land was corrupted by reason of the swarm of flies."

Clarke runs 45 lines on the flies, the [LXX ]{.class_s4N4-0}says ["dog flies,"]{.class_s4N4-0} others say it should have been "beetles," and so forth and so on. All of the commentators (without exception) forgot the Holy Spirit's teaching on the passage, but the serious student of Scripture will look up His opinion before wasting five minutes with Hebrew roots and "Egyptologists." In Psalm 78:45, the Holy Spirit has told the fourth-grade student that the Lord sent ["divers"]{.class_s4N2-0} kinds of flies on Egypt; not[ "dog flies"]{.class_s4N4-0} or beetles or anything like them. The [Wycliffe]{.class_s4N4-0} commentator tries desperately to yoke Alexandria up with the Holy Spirit by stating first that the word for ["flies"]{.class_s4N2-0} denotes a [particular kind]{.class_s4N4-0} of fly; this comes from believing that []{#cZR.xhtml_page_173}the Greek (["kunomuia"]{.class_s4N4-0})---found in manuscripts from A.D. 150--500---refers to a [particular kind]{.class_s4N4-0} of fly. Then the commentator states that the word means "a mixture of [various ]{.class_s4N4-0}sources!"

Fortunately for the Bible believer, we have access to the highly accurate and scientific version of Psalm 78:45,[ preserved without error in the AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611), in spite of "recent discoveries" and "scientific investigations" of modern "Conservative jugheads." There were different kinds of flies, and any opinion to the contrary is only an opinion of an infidel. Running to Hebrew or Greek roots to prove the contrary (or the affirmative) is a waste of time, talent, and paper. What roars over Egypt is the tsetse fly, the bot fly, the dog fly, the horsefly, the drone fly, the blowfly, the Hessian fly, and the [Idia Fasciata]{.class_s4N4-0} (North African fly). Representing Beelzebub (Matt. 12:27), the god of filth, the flies corrupt the land (Exod. 8:24) and prove that all the trouble to which the Egyptians went in order to placate Beelzebub was useless.

["And I will sever in that day the land of Goshen, in which my people dwell, that no swarms of flies shall be there"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 22). With this advent of filth, God segregates His people and puts "[a difference" ]{.class_s4N2-0}between them and the Egyptians (see 11:7). This segregation of evil from good, pure from impure, Heaven from Hell, saved from lost, saints from sinners, and darkness from light (2 Cor. 6:14--17) begins in Genesis 1:3--5 and continues without a letup to Revelation 21:27. ["The god of this world"]{.class_s4N2-0} (2 Cor. 4:4) can stand anything from the Christian but separation (Rom. 12:1--2), so the whole movement of the entire world system in the next thirty years will be to erase every line of demarcation between the [born-again []{#cZR.xhtml_page_174}believer ]{.class_s4N4-0}and the[ science-worshipping Socialist. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Where believers cannot be forced to conform to the system, they will be tortured and killed (Rev. 13:6--18). (See [Foxe's Book of Martyrs ]{.class_s4N4-0}or any work by John Noble, Richard Wurmbrand, or Haralan Popov. )

["And I will put a division between my people and thy people" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 23). Among other things, Jesus Christ came to this earth to do exactly the same thing (Luke 12:51), and by the standards of modern ecumenical Socialists, Jesus Christ should have been crucified, for He was a "church-splitter" every time He showed up (John 7:43, 9:16, 10:19, etc.). The only "integration" the Bible knows anything about is the unity and solidarity of the Body of Jesus Christ (1 Cor. 3:3; Rom. 16:17; and 1 Cor. 11:19). (See [Segregation or Integration?,]{.class_s4N4-0} Peter Ruckman, 1970 \[Hereafter referred to as [Segregation or Integration?]{.class_s4N4-0}\] .)

Egypt has an "ecology problem," to put it mildly. Dead fish and frogs' ova (deposited in putrid masses) are the best breeding places for flies known to nature. The atmosphere, now, consists of a stinking breeze blowing over dead fish, decayed vegetation, and frog's dung, accompanied by the slaps and curses of human beings tormented by flies, and the howls and groans of beasts trying to find rest from the winged plague. The plagues of Egypt were real plagues. They took place [exactly ]{.class_s4N4-0}as they are described in the account. For the credulous---who believe anything they read except the text of the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) Bible---may we note that:

1\. Piles of [eels ]{.class_s4N4-0}fell on the streets of Coalburg, Alabama in 1892; the particular species was only found in the PACIFIC OCEAN.

2\. Fish-shaped objects fell all over Alla--habad, India in 1836. They were dead and dry as crackers and turned to [blood ]{.class_s4N4-0}when they were heated in a pan.

3\. In the Seventeenth Century, a buttery substance the size of a man's finger was found smeared all around the fields near  Tipperary, Ireland.

4\. In 1954, a California housewife heard a thump. She went out in her yard and found a fifteen-inch lump of buttery, wax-like substance lying there.

5\. This same type of substance was found in small "balls" all over Europe and England in the spring of 1966. One hundred of these "balls" were found in one garden in France. [They were found again in 1972 in Kansas.]{.class_s4N4-0}

6\. In 1832, an amber-colored resin, like rubber cotton, was found in a town in Russia, lying two inches deep over an area of about 600 square feet.

8:25 "And Pharaoh called for Moses and for Aaron, and said, Go ye, sacrifice to your God in the land.

26 And Moses said, It is not meet so to do; for we shall sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians to the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}our God: lo, shall we sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians before their eyes, and will they not stone us?

27 We will go three days' journey into the wilderness, and sacrifice to the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}our God, as he shall command us.

28 And Pharaoh said, I will let you go, that ye may sacrifice to the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}your God in the wilderness; only ye shall not go very far away: intreat for me.

29 And Moses said, Behold, I go out from thee, and I will intreat the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}that the swarms of flies may []{#cZR.xhtml_page_176}depart from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people, to morrow: but let not Pharaoh deal deceitfully any more in not letting the people go to sacrifice to the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}.

30 And Moses went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}.

31 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}did according to the word of Moses; and he removed the swarms of flies from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people; there remained not one.

32 And Pharaoh hardened his heart at this time also, neither would he let the people go."

Again, Pharaoh has a "change of heart" to all appearances. ["Go ye, sacrifice to your God"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 25). But the reader will observe the P.S. tacked onto the balloon note: ["IN THE LAND" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 25). At this point, Pharaoh suggests a series of compromises, and all of these compromises give light on Satan's method of dealing with the child of God in any age. The compromises are these:

1\. Go ahead and serve God, [but ]{.class_s4N4-0}stay in the world while you do it. Be worldly, get along with the world, and be friends with the world (James 4:1--4; John 17: 9, 14).

2\. Leave the world, BUT don't leave it [too far behind]{.class_s4N4-0} (Exod. 8:28). Keep in touch through TV, radio, [Life, Look, Time, Newsweek,]{.class_s4N4-0} and the theater.

3\. If you have to go altogether out of the world, at least have the thoughtfulness to leave [the children]{.class_s4N4-0} behind; after all, the ["little ones" ]{.class_s4N2-0}don't realize all the things you do (Exod. 10:10--11). Why spoil Christmas for them?

4\. If you finally take the whole family, at least keep your roots in Egypt through your real estate, life insurance policies, savings accounts, stocks and bonds, and other "holdings" (Exod. 10:24).

The correct answer to these "offers" is found in Hebrews 13:13--14 and John 7:15. The Christian is told ["be not conformed to this world" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Rom. 12:2), because Paul calls it an ["evil world"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Gal. 1:4) and John says that it ["passeth away" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(1 John 2:17). The "positive view" is out of the question, and this means that a real Bible believer will either be ridiculed, opposed, persecuted, hated, reviled, despised, or killed---no matter [what country]{.class_s4N4-0} he lives in and no matter [which century ]{.class_s4N4-0}he lives in. The attitude of the world towards the separated believer is: "He is an odd ball. He is negative. He is an enemy of mankind. He is [antisocial. ]{.class_s4N4-0}He opposes peace and progress. He is an isolationist---a dangerous [reactionary." ]{.class_s4N4-0}These are the charges brought against Jeremiah (Jer. 37:13), the Lord Jesus (Luke 23:1--2), Simon Peter and John (Acts 5:28), the Apostle Paul (Acts 24:5), and every great evangelist and missionary who ever lived (see comments on Gen. 8:21 in the Commentary on Genesis).

["And Moses said, It is not meet\...shall we sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians before their eyes" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 26). We have commented on the matter before under 5:3. The Egyptians believed that the "gods" of Genesis 6:1--3 hid under the guise of animals at the time of the Flood (Eusebius writes of this peculiar belief in Metam.l.v., fab.v.,1326, cited by Clarke on pg. 331 of his commentary). According to this belief, Jupiter hid in a ram, Apollo hid in a crow, Diana in a cat, Juno in a []{#cZR.xhtml_page_178}white heifer, Venus in a fish, Mercury in a bird, etc., etc. As another has so beautifully worded it: "Oh be kind to your web-footed friends, for a duck may be somebody's uncle; be kind to your friends in the swamp!" (For the proper atmosphere this is to be sung to the tune of[ "Stars and Stripes Forever."]{.class_s4N4-0})

The Christian should observe that Moses does not even know yet how to sacrifice or give an offering: ["\...and sacrifice to the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[our God as he shall command us"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 27). Pharaoh accepts this suggestion, but adds:["only ye shall not go very far away"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 28), which is the second compromise. Then he hastily adds: ["intreat for me" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 28). This injection of the "devotional element" unseats Moses for a minute and not fully grasping what Pharaoh meant by ["not go very far away,"]{.class_s4N2-0} Moses says, ["I will intreat the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[that the swarms of flies may depart from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people, to morrow"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 29).

The last word is a touch of sarcasm, for Pharaoh wanted the flies to leave [that minute. ]{.class_s4N4-0}But since he had said ["to morrow"]{.class_s4N2-0} concerning the frogs (vs. 10), Moses just goes ahead and "rubs it in."

No exposition is needed for verses 30--32. As soon as there is respite, Pharaoh cracks down on his policies again, and the slave labor gangs go right on working. What follows---chapters 9 and 10---is a contest unique in the annals of man's history. Every three or four weeks, Moses and Aaron show up at the palace. Pretty soon, the guards saluted them when they came in, and several of the attendants bowed down and "crossed" themselves. Moses and Aaron would march into the throne room and say, ["You gonna let 'em go?!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Nope. Changed my mind."

"You know why you remind me of a harp struck by lightning, King?"

"Well, I guess it's 'cause I hit quick and make good music."

"Nossir! You remind me of a harp struck by lightning because you are a blasted lyre!"

"Well, its politics you know. Gotta get along with folks. How you gettin' along these days?"

"That ain't all. You're a hypocrite!"

"Now look out! [Look out there! ]{.class_s4N4-0}Don't you call me [that ]{.class_s4N4-0}word! I'll have your head cut off!"

"You ain't gonna cut MY head off, King, because you know what'll happen to you if you do. You've seen enough by now to know what the score is."

And Pharaoh would grit his teeth, pop his knuckles, crack some ice cubes on the table, and curse and mumble and say, "Well, I'm gonna let 'em go some day, but I'm just not quite ready yet."

About a month later, in would come Moses and Aaron, and without a how-do-you-do, they would say:[ "Gonna let 'em go yet?!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Well," Pharaoh would say, "I need to pray about it awhile. It's a very important decision, you understand. You all sit down and have a cup of coffee while we talk it over."

Moses would reject the invitation. Aaron would say, "We aren't about to warm our feet at the Devil's fire like Simon Peter, and if you don't straighten up and fly right, I'll raise this rod again!"

Pharaoh would glare at him and say: "All right, I'll call your hand! You're just bluffin'!"

And up would go the rod! Then all hell would break loose, just like it will in the next several years (Rev. 6:8, 9:1--3).

[]{#c12M.xhtml}

# Chapter 9 {.heading_s4G-0}

9:1 "Then the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Go in unto Pharaoh, and tell him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me.

2 For if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still,

3 Behold, the hand of the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}is upon thy cattle which is in the field, upon the horses, upon the asses, upon the camels, upon the oxen, and upon the sheep: there shall be a very grievous murrain.

4 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}shall sever between the cattle of Israel and the cattle of Egypt: and there shall nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel.

5 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}appointed a set time, saying, To morrow the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}shall do this thing in the land.

6 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}did that thing on the morrow, and all the cattle of Egypt died: but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one.

7 And Pharaoh sent, and, behold, there was not one of the cattle of the Israelites dead. And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go."

The contest continues. Now the Lord prepares to attack the gods represented by bulls, cows, calves, goats, and rams (12:12). Camels and horses are mentioned, and the use of both animals in pagan worship is alluded to in the Bible (Deut. 17:16; Judg. 8:21, 26). This time, the children of Israel "sit one out" (vs. 4), and the only cattle that suffer are those belonging to Pharaoh's people. ["And the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[ ]{.class_s4NB}[shall sever between the cattle" ]{.class_s4N2-0}clearly points out the Divine policy of discrimination and segregation, in spite of the contemporary (and perennial) []{#c12M.xhtml_page_182}delusion that "equality" and "integration" are "Christian" principles.

The ["grievous murrain" ]{.class_s4N2-0}strikes down the animals, with its accompanying difficulty of breathing, rattling in the throat, swelling of the head, and the "blind staggers." Here, the animals are suffering vicariously for the sins of their masters, and it was not the first time (nor the last) that this ever happened. In World War II, dogs were sent out to "trip" land mines and anti-personnel mines; many times they were shot down by snipers before they could locate the mines. In World War I, many a farm boy was cursing the war by every god he knew; not because men were being shot and killed, but because horses were being shot and crippled on the battlefield, and their neighings and cries so shattered the night that it was more terrible than the screams of wounded men.

["And the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[did that thing on the morrow, and all the cattle of Egypt died" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 6). These are limited to those[ "in the field" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 3); consequently, when the hail comes down (vss. 18--19), many of the Egyptians get their cattle in out of the field (vs. 20). The reader will also notice that the[ time element]{.class_s4N4-0} is not given between verse 6 and verse 25. (If the typology is exact, the plagues on Pharaoh run seven years, although most commentators limit the contest to a year or less.)

The royal palace is besieged by telegrams, phone calls, newspaper reporters, committees, delegations, and air-pollution experts from Harvard and Yale. Pharaoh is rushing around like a chicken with his head cut off; ["Jannes and Jambres"]{.class_s4N2-0} are taking turns psychoanalyzing one another; Pharaoh's daughter is trying to hire someone to assassinate her "son"; and []{#c12M.xhtml_page_183}things are in a condition known as ["SNAFU," ]{.class_s4N4-0}to cite an old Army expression.

Pharaoh storms across the hallway and almost bumps into Aaron and Moses. "What the blankety-blank are you doin\' here?!" roars Pharaoh.

"Watchin' the sideshow, your majesty," says Aaron.

["You gonna let 'em go now?" ]{.class_s4N4-0}asks Moses.

"Nosireee Bob! Not by a long shot! Why, these things happen all the time. Bad feed---that's the trouble. Anybody's cattle can get hoof-and-mouth disease; happens all the time. God causes His rain to fall on the JUST and the UNJUST; ain't that right? Happens to all of us!"

"You been over to Goshen lately?"

"No. Why?"

"Well, your honor, I hate to tell you this, but there isn't a sick calf in the land of Goshen, and there ain't a 'hot spot' on a cow or sheep anywhere in the parish!"

"I don't believe it!"

"Well, send one of your flunkies on over there and check it out," says Aaron.

"Yeah," confirms Moses, "Your news releases aren't givin' you the straight dope, Pharaoh, ole' son. You better start believing God instead of the Associated Press."

["And Pharaoh sent, and, behold, there was not one of the cattle of the Israelites dead"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 7). While Pharaoh is checking the rumor, Moses and Aaron return to their headquarters. They are met by the elders of Israel. And now, once again, it is: "Heil, Moses!" "Yeah, keep up the good work, man!" "Pour it to him, Moses!" "We're with you all the way, boy! Pharaoh hasn't let up, but buddy, I'll tell you things are movin' up out where I work!" "We don't understand it, Moses, but boy []{#c12M.xhtml_page_184}you're gettin' results!" "Push him again, Moses! Lean on him! You got him runnin' now!" etc., etc.

The next day, Pharaoh is just finishing coffee and toast when in comes you-know-who. ["You gonna let 'em go ?"]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Naw. Changed my mind" (vs. 7).

"All right, if that's how you feel about it---here goes!"

"Here goes [what?"]{.class_s4N4-0}

"Here goes another plague."

"So what! Anything you can do my magicians can do better!"

"I don't care if they can do it or not; here she goes again!"

And up would go that rod! And all of Pharaoh's attendants would hit the ground like a German 88 was going overhead.

9:8 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses and unto Aaron, Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace, and let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh.

9 And it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt, and shall be a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast, throughout all the land of Egypt.

10 And they took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh; and Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven; and it became a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast.

11 And the magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils; for the boil was upon the magicians, and upon all the Egyptians.

12 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he hearkened not unto them; as the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had spoken unto Moses."

The student of the word should observe that God did not rest content with segregating His people (8:23); He has now segregated their [possessions]{.class_s4N4-0} (vs. 4)[. ]{.class_s4N4-0}This is a double warning to Pharaoh that a real disaster is heading his way.

["Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace\...and it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt\...a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 8--9). This is ["the botch of Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} mentioned in Deuteronomy 28:27, and it appears to be a swelling inflammation which turns into a boil on the skin. Undoubtedly, there is a connection between this ["botch"]{.class_s4N2-0} and the ["grievous sore"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Revelation 16:1--2. The reader who refers to the Holy Spirit for his information, instead of the Christian educators, cannot fail to notice that the leprosy of Leviticus chapters 13--14 is described in this fashion (see Lev. 13:2, 8, 18, 23, 24, 28, and 43).

Moses and Aaron have entered the palace with the ashes in their hands, and they simply throw the ashes up in the air right in the throne room (vs. 10). Then, right before their eyes, the magicians break out with the bloody boils and are in such pain they cannot finish the contest. ["The furnace"]{.class_s4N2-0} can be [any ]{.class_s4N4-0}furnace, and the [Pulpit Commentary's ]{.class_s4N4-0}fantastic notion that "bricks were not often baked in Egypt" is too much for any reader to stomach who has read Exodus chapters 1--6. Egypt is called ["the iron furnace" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(Deut. 4:20; 1 Kings 8:51), and the most natural explanation of the text is that Moses []{#c12M.xhtml_page_186}and Aaron picked up a handful of ashes from the very place where the Jews had been sweating out their life's blood. Up go the ashes, down come the boils and blains, and now, in addition to dead fish, frog offal, fly bites, murrain, and itching, the Egyptians are tormented with sores that leak pus and inflamed skin that festers and burns.

["And the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he hearkened not unto them"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 12). As we read the words, we cannot imagine the obstinacy of the man unless we assume that everyone got smitten with boils but HIM. There is no mention of Pharaoh contracting the disease of the text (vss. 10--11). The [LXX ]{.class_s4N4-0}has altered verse 11 to read "all the land of Egypt" after[ "Egyptians,"]{.class_s4N2-0} and although the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}does not follow this Catholic lead this time, it will be found in the Latin [Vulgate, ]{.class_s4N4-0}which has the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}readings for Luke 2:33; Matthew 1:25; etc. (The [LXX ]{.class_s4N4-0}has also added "the sons of Israel" to vs. 7.)

9:13 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh, and say unto him, Thus saith the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me.

14 For I will at this time send all my plagues upon thine heart, and upon thy servants, and upon thy people; that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth.

15 For now I will stretch out my hand, that I may smite thee and thy people with pestilence; and thou shalt be cut off from the earth.

16 And in very deed for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power; and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth.

17 As yet exaltest thou thyself against my people, that thou wilt not let them go?

18 Behold, to morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail, such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now.

19 Send therefore now, and gather thy cattle, and all that thou hast in the field; for upon every man and beast which shall be found in the field, and shall not be brought home, the hail shall come down upon them, and they shall die.

20 He that feared the word of the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}among the servants of Pharaoh made his servants and his cattle flee into the houses:

21 And he that regarded not the word of the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}left his servants and his cattle in the field."
