["Let my people go, that they may serve me" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 13) has been the one, unremitting, monotonous demand that has beaten on Pharaoh's ears for months. This time when Moses and Aaron go in, ["Jannes and Jambres" ]{.class_s4N2-0}have disappeared, and Pharaoh has doubled his bodyguard.

["For I will at this time send all my plagues"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 14). Compare this "stepping up" of the timetable with the exact duplicate in Revelation chapter 16. ["Upon thine heart" ]{.class_s4N2-0}is explained in 2 Chronicles 6:28--30, and it means that God will "pour it on" so hard that Pharaoh will have to "take to heart" the things he has heard. If Pharaoh sneered at the declaration[ here, ]{.class_s4N4-0}you can bet your last sawbuck that he did not when he bent over the crib of []{#c12M.xhtml_page_188}his firstborn in the royal nursery (12:29). Not many men can separate the "affections of their hearts" from their firstborn[ sons.]{.class_s4N4-0}

["For now I will stretch out my hand" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 15). The majority of the commentators---Clarke and Lange in the lead---assure us that the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611) translators have grossly erred in translating the passage thus. No fair peeking!) Look at the thing objectively: where do [any ]{.class_s4N4-0}corruptions of the Bible come from? Where do ALL corruptions of the Bible come from? Then why would you expect this one to be any different?

The reading of Clarke, Ainsworth, the [Pulpit Commentary, ]{.class_s4N4-0}and others is the reading of the fifth column of Origen's "[Hexapla]{.class_s4N4-0}." It is the same "LOGIC" that led the [NASV]{.class_s4N4-0} (1963) to knock salvation out of the mouth of the dying thief in Luke 23:42, and the Deity of Jesus Christ out of Luke 24:51--52.

The "judicious critic" (Clarke's words---an excellent description of Pharaoh) reasons that since God did NOT dump everything on Pharaoh right at [that minute,]{.class_s4N4-0} that the text must be altered to  prevent a contradiction. We have observed this monkey business many times before: you help God out so folks will think well of Him and think better of [you ]{.class_s4N4-0}for giving Him the assistance that He needed so badly. The scholars believe the word should be translated in the past subjunctive (Heb. ["shalachti vaach otheca"]{.class_s4N4-0}). Should it?

Here we have another one of those superlative examples of the two truths which the [Bible Believer's Commentary Series ]{.class_s4N4-0}emphasizes.

Verse 14 said perfectly plainly ([in ANY manuscript]{.class_s4N4-0}): ["For I will at this time send all my plagues\...." ]{.class_s4N2-0}How does one justify Clarke and Philip Schaff for changing the tense of the verb in verse 15 to make it contradict the tense of the one in verse 14, while professing to be helping God out so He will not contradict Himself? Did God send ALL of His plagues in chapter 9? Then why stop with halfway measures? If you are going to butcher verse 15, why don't you have the nerve to butcher verse 14 and make it read: "For HAD I SENT at this time all my plagues\...? Cold feet there, Doctor? No guts? Chicken, Doctor? Chicken guts?

No, there is something more to be found in the English text than meets the eye, and the fact that 95% of the Fundamental scholars cannot see it is no testimony to the "present state of the text." B. God [does ]{.class_s4N4-0}stretch out His hand, and He [does ]{.class_s4N4-0}["smite"]{.class_s4N2-0} Pharaoh, and He [does ]{.class_s4N4-0}["cut ]{.class_s4N2-0}\[him\] [off from the earth"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 15). Pharaoh is ["cut off,"]{.class_s4N2-0} and before he is ["cut off"]{.class_s4N2-0} (see 15:1--10), he and his people ARE "smitten with the pestilence" (cf. 2 Sam. 24:13--16). ["And in very deed for this cause have I raised thee up"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 16). This is the famous Calvinistic passage quoted in Romans 9:17 and elaborated on (endlessly) by Kuyper, Berkhof, Pink, Dabney, Hodge, Strong, Ross, Gill, Gilpin, Shelton, the ["Sovereign Grace Book Club,"]{.class_s4N4-0} and[ "The Baptist Examiner."]{.class_s4N4-0} As usual, the dead-orthodox Conservatives rush to God's aid to make sure that no one turns "supralapsarianist" after reading verse 16. 1\. The Hebrew word is ["amad." ]{.class_s4N4-0}In Psalm 107:25, the inconsistent [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901) has translated it as "RAISE," exactly as it stands in the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}text of Exodus 9:16.

2\. The Holy Spirit Himself interpreted the word correctly in Romans 9:17 by using the Greek word ["exegeira."]{.class_s4N4-0} This is a case which is identical to the Holy Spirit's interpretation of ["alma" ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Heb.: Isa. 7:14) as[ "parthenos" ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Gk.: Matt. 1:23). Clarke's criticism, therefore, of Paul (see Comment., pg. 325) does not differ in substance from the criticism of any agnostic or atheist.

[Moral]{.class_s4N4-0}: where Keil, Delitzsch, Clarke, Lange, Schaff, or the Lockman Foundation attack the Holy Spirit---while professing to believe in the "verbal inspiration of the [originals"]{.class_s4N4-0}---discard their private interpretations exactly as you would discard the private interpretations of Rome. The trouble with the text is its overtones of "Unconditional Election." But the believer must never make the mistake that Origen, Machen, Robertson, []{#c12M.xhtml_page_192}Keil, Delitzsch, Westcott, Gesenius, Hort, Philo, Irenaeus, Augustine, Jerome, Griesbach, Lachmann, Mill, Walton, Wuest, Thieme, or Schaff made---altering the Reformation text to help God out of a tight place.

D. No amount of mishandling John 12:37--43 can change the picture of unbelieving Israel, for right in the context (where a nation has supposedly been forced into unbelief---John 12:39), it says: ["Nevertheless among the CHIEF RULERS also many believed on HIM"]{.class_s4N2-0} (John 12:42)! (See Rom. 9:6 for a similar case.)

F. The ["election"]{.class_s4N2-0} of verse 11 is a[ temporal act ]{.class_s4N4-0}in time, and nowhere in the Bible does the word [election]{.class_s4N4-0} ever occur in an [eternal ]{.class_s4N4-0}setting. God []{#c12M.xhtml_page_193}elected nothing nor anyone until AFTER Genesis 1:1, and this is clear in every context where the word is used (Isa. 42:1, 45:4, 65:9, 22; Matt. 24:22, 24, 31; Rom. 8:33; Col. 3:12; 1 Tim. 5:21; 2 Tim. 2:10; Tit. 1:1; 1 Pet. 1:2; etc., etc.).

G. The illustration of Pharaoh, in Romans chapter 9, is that he was a piece of clay in the potter's hands (vss. 19--23). Clay is a visible substance [which did not exist before Genesis 1:1. ]{.class_s4N4-0}The context is the clay [after ]{.class_s4N4-0}it is on the table (!), and there is no mention of the clay [before ]{.class_s4N4-0}it is dug out of the hill or brought to the shop. If the "clay pot" repents (Jer. 18:8), its destination is changed.

H. We have clearly seen that "foreknowledge" preceded Pharaoh's "hardened heart" (Exod. 3:19), exactly as it preceded every act that God ever did (Acts 15:18). There is not a statement that God decided to show Pharaoh "no mercy" [before ]{.class_s4N4-0}Pharaoh was born (Rom. 9:15). The text in Romans is a quotation from Exodus 33:19---not Exodus 9:16.

I. Lastly, observe how carefully the Holy Spirit has guarded the correct reading of the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bible from the corruptions of the [NASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1963), recommended by Hyles-Anderson, Liberty University, Tennessee Temple, Bob Jones University, Dallas, Fuller, BIOLA, and other "Conservative" schools. God raised up Pharaoh exactly as He raised up Nebuchadnezzar and Caesar. None of these men are said to be "prepared aforetime" (Rom. 9:23) for destruction, and no judgments are pronounced on them until AFTER Genesis chapter 1; in every case (see John 6:64), []{#c12M.xhtml_page_195}foreknowledge precedes any "divine decree." He was "RAISED UP" to demonstrate the longsuffering of God (Rom. 9:17) and God's power that God's ["name may be declared throughout all the earth."]{.class_s4N2-0} (See Exod. 9:16.)

["Behold, to morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail\...upon every man and beast\...the hail shall come down upon them, and they shall die"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 18--19). For the first time since the contest started, HUMAN LIFE is threatened. Pharaoh, by now, should see "how the land lies," or "how the snow drifts." If he had any sense (or fear of God), he should have followed his confession (vs. 27) with ACTION, for now, there is a hint that before the Lord gets through with Pharaoh, there are going to be some piles of dead [people ]{.class_s4N4-0}alongside the dead [fish]{.class_s4N4-0} and dead[ frogs. ]{.class_s4N4-0}We should notice, further, that the last plagues (boils and blains) have come [down from heaven,]{.class_s4N4-0} not [up from the earth. ]{.class_s4N4-0}The water into blood, the frogs, lice, and the flies were all products of the earth, but now the hail rains down from ABOVE.

The word ["rain" ]{.class_s4N2-0}causes the commentators considerable anguish, and after going into great lengths to prove that it does occasionally rain in Egypt, Clarke points out that [hail ]{.class_s4N4-0}is actually formed by [rain. ]{.class_s4N4-0}However, this Alexandrian attempt to help God out---after tearing up His words in the preceding eight chapters---is useless. The word ["rain"]{.class_s4N2-0} here, as in Psalm 78:24, 27, Exodus []{#c12M.xhtml_page_196}16:4 and Luke 17:29 (and numerous other places), is clearly the verb---not the noun. We are accustomed to saying, "It[ rained ]{.class_s4N4-0}today," without saying WHAT it "rained." However, it has rained snow, hail, water, blood, bread, manna, snares, and, as the old saying goes, "cats and dogs and---babies!" (Censored by the NAACP and upheld by the Supreme Court.)

["He that feared the word of the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[ ]{.class_s4NB}[among the servants of Pharaoh made his servants and his cattle flee into the houses: And he that regarded not the word of the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[ ]{.class_s4NB}[left his servants and his cattle in the field" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 20--21). Ham's children were said to be ["a servant of servants,"]{.class_s4N2-0} and so one will find on the Egyptian monuments [Nubians ]{.class_s4N4-0}serving Egyptians, and [Egyptians ]{.class_s4N4-0}serving Shemites (Hyksos dynasty) and Egyptians. Rice, Gleason Archer, and Merrill Unger towards the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}New Testament than Eve's attitude toward Genesis 2:16--17. And the difference in eternity between those in New Jerusalem and those in the Lake of Fire is the same difference; race, color, sex, money, culture, age, civilization, time, education, intelligence, religion, motives, objectives, and background are not even points in the case.

Some people regard the word;[ others don't.]{.class_s4N4-0}

George Bernard Shaw (1856--1950), Ralph Sockman (1889--1970), Karl Barth (1886--1968), and Harry Emerson Fosdick (1878--1969) treated the word as John Dewey (1859--1952), and Bertrand Russell (1872--1970) treated it.

D. L. Moody (1837--1899), Billy Sunday (1862--1935), and Bob Jones Sr. (1883--1968) treated it as John Chrysostom (346--407), Martin Luther (1483--1546), and George Whitefield (1714--1770) treated it.

9:22 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Stretch forth thine hand toward heaven, that there may be hail in all the land of Egypt, upon man, and upon beast, and upon every herb of the field, throughout the land of Egypt.

23 And Moses stretched forth his rod to-ward heaven: and the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}sent thunder and hail, and the fire ran along upon the ground; and the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}rained hail upon the land of Egypt.

24 So there was hail, and fire mingled with the hail, very grievous, such as there was none like it in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation.

25 And the hail smote throughout all the land of Egypt all that was in the field, both man and beast; and the hail smote every herb of the field, and brake every tree of the field.

26 Only in the land of Goshen, where the children of Israel were, was there no hail."

What follows is a genuine foretaste of the Tribulation (Rev. 8:7--8). This time, not only men and animals are stricken (vs. 22), but[ "every herb of the field" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 22) and ["every tree of the field'']{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 25). According to [Unger,]{.class_s4N4-0} the founding of Egypt's kingdom is 5613 B.C. [Boch ]{.class_s4N4-0}dates it at 5702 B.C., [Mariette ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [Lenormant ]{.class_s4N4-0}at 5004 B.C., [Brugsch ]{.class_s4N4-0}at 4455 B.C., [Lepsius ]{.class_s4N4-0}at 3853 B.C., and [Bunsen ]{.class_s4N4-0}at 3623 B.C. Bunsen has the best of the argument, and he is as good an "Egyptologist" as any other two combined; however, his date is probably a little early. As it stands (3623 B.C.), there would be a nation in Egypt around 300 years after Adam and Eve were driven out of the garden. There is certainly a thriving civilization in Mesopotamia at this time. Since history always moves EAST to WEST with the sun (see comments under Gen. 4: 16 in the [Commentary on Genesis),]{.class_s4N4-0} the chances are ten to one that the Mesopotamian kingdom is first (Gen. 4---7), and Egypt's kingdom is [second ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Gen. 10:6).

["And the fire ran along upon the ground; and the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[ ]{.class_s4NB}[rained hail upon the land of Egypt" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 23). That isn't all. It got so cold (Psa. 78:47) that FROST ruined the vines and trees. Somewhere the major commentators missed the reference, and not one of them (in 1,880 years of commenting) could find anything to say about this remarkable phenomenon. That isn't all; ["the fire ran along upon the ground."]{.class_s4N2-0}

The [RV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1885) decided to take this reading out since the action was impossible---there must have been a mistake in the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611)---so the [RV ]{.class_s4N4-0}says the fire ["ran down unto the earth."]{.class_s4N4-0} Here, the [RV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1885) has followed the error of the Alexandrian school exactly as it did []{#c12M.xhtml_page_202}when it mistranslated Judges 5:14. The literal Hebrew rendering is: "and the fire WALKED upon the earth." The[ Authorized Version]{.class_s4N4-0} brings this out quite clearly with[ "fire ran along upon the ground" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 23).

What we have here is lightning [running horizontally ]{.class_s4N4-0}across the ground, hitting men and animals at random. In addition to this horror, down come hailstones which probably weighed a talent a piece (Rev. 16:21).

In spite of the very naive and stupid book[ Is That In the Bible? ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Charles Potter, 1933), history bears testimony to the large hailstones. (The work referred to above is about 272 pages \[Crest reprint\] of questions and answers designed to make a novice think the Bible is about as authoritative as a [Farmer's Almanac.]{.class_s4N4-0})

Stones weighing [10--17 pounds ]{.class_s4N4-0}fell at L'Aigle, France (Normandy) on April 26, 1803. Hailstones [five inches]{.class_s4N4-0} in diameter fell near Liverpool, England in 1795. On April 29, 1697, a hailstorm two miles wide and sixty miles long fell on England, knocking down men and animals and splintering trees. Bluish hailstones weighing [100 pounds apiece]{.class_s4N4-0} were reported to have fallen in Italy in 1510; they killed men, animals, and birds  (Mezeray's [History of France]{.class_s4N4-0}). Joshua 10:11 is the Biblical account of this kind of thing, and Joshua (Jesus) clearly pictures the Second Advent which destroys the cursed city (Babylon, Rev. 17---18) of Jericho after "seven days" (cf. Dan. 9:27) encirclement.

Egypt has had about enough of it. The populace has been subjected to digging for water, going two to three weeks with no fish to eat, squashed frogs heaped in piles, itching lice, biting flies, and boils all over their bodies. Now fire is screeching across the fields of barley, flax, []{#c12M.xhtml_page_203}and wheat; killing cattle, setting fire to dry brush, and knocking men down like ten pins.

There is a committee outside of the palace waving petitions signed by thousands of Egyptians. Pharaoh in his "state of the union" message has been saying "hard luck," "ironic misfortune," "stroke of fate," and "accident" for so long, no one believes him anymore. He has adopted that typical view of the typical international politician (which is typical in any age, be he Pope, King, ambassador, philosopher, educator, President, Premier, or dictator; see[ The Sure Word of Prophecy).]{.class_s4N4-0}

["]{.class_s4N4-0}We must bravely face the future"; "Oh well, that's water over the dam"; "No use crying over spilt milk"; "Let us press forward"; "Our luck is bound to change one of these days"; "Keep looking up"; "The tide is turning"; "We'll hit it rich next time"; "The brightest dawn is just after darkness"; "The Kingdom is coming"; "Have faith"; "Things have to get worse before they can get better"; "Peace is just around the corner"; "Believe in your fellowman"; etc., etc., etc.

9:27 "And Pharaoh sent, and called for Moses and Aaron, and said unto them, I have sinned this time: the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}is righteous, and I and my people are wicked.

28 Intreat the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}(for it is enough) that there be no more mighty thunderings and hail; and I will let you go, and ye shall stay no longer.

29 And Moses said unto him, As soon as I am gone out of the city, I will spread abroad my hands unto the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}; and the thunder shall cease, neither shall there be any more hail; that thou mayest know how that the earth is the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}'S.

30 But as for thee and thy servants, I know that ye will not yet fear the LORD God.

31 And the flax and the barley was smitten: for the barley was in the ear, and the flax was bolled.

32 But the wheat and the rie were not smitten: for they were not grown up.

33 And Moses went out of the city from Pharaoh, and spread abroad his hands unto the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}: and the thunders and hail ceased, and the rain was not poured upon the earth.

34 And when Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunders were ceased, he sinned yet more, and hardened his heart, he and his servants.

35 And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, neither would he let the children of Israel go; as the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}had spoken by Moses."

This time it looks like Pharaoh means business. In come Moses and Aaron like sleet and slush in March, and the same old demand is sounded forth: ["You gonna let 'em go?"]{.class_s4N4-0}

This time, Pharaoh has sent for them (vs. 27), and so he has his speech all prepared. ["I have sinned this time" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 27). (Isn't that a beaut? [What about the other six times?!]{.class_s4N4-0}) ["The ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[ ]{.class_s4NB}[is righteous, and I and my people are wicked."]{.class_s4N2-0}

Here, at last, is a genuine confession worthy of any confessional booth in the Catholic communion. Pharaoh confesses his sins and asks for prayer! Surely this is "conversion"! But, alas! (mid-Victorian for "Oh, hell"!) many a man who said ["I have sinned" ]{.class_s4N2-0}was only stating a well-known fact (Balaam, Num. 22:34; Judas, Matt. 27:4; Saul, 1 Sam. 15:24), and many a man who asked for []{#c12M.xhtml_page_205}prayer (Simon, Acts 8:24; Jeroboam, 1 Kings 13:6; Saul, 1 Sam. 15:25) was only trying to get out of praying himself.

Observe that Pharaoh confesses (vs. 27), acknowledges (vs. 27), and offers to make restitution (vs. 28); but like any fruit-stand sacramentalist, the inner man remains obstinate and  rebellious. No sooner does the hail stop than Pharaoh changes his tune (vs. 34). Looking out the palace window after the racket has subsided, Pharaoh mutters: "I'll put 'em to work [outside of Goshen ]{.class_s4N4-0}tomorrow, and then if their 'Lord God' does anything, He'll have to kill them with my folks. If that stumble-bum thinks he's going to win this hand, he's mistaken. I'M PHARAOH!"

But Moses is ahead of Pharaoh, because Moses has believed what God said about Pharaoh (3:19). He knows the nature of his subject (vs. 30), and he tells Pharaoh this to his face when Pharaoh asks for prayer. ["I will spread abroad my hands unto the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[ ]{.class_s4NB}[" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 29) plainly indicates "I will pray about it" (see 1 Tim. 2:8; 2 Chron. 6:13; Ezra 9:5; etc.).

["And the flax and the barley was smitten\...But the wheat and the rie were not smitten" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 31--32). Readers of commentaries have noticed, through the years, the never-ending harangue against the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bible for using ["corn"]{.class_s4N2-0} where the writer thought it should have used [wheat, barley,]{.class_s4N4-0} or [rye]{.class_s4N4-0}. Here is a very interesting and amusing case before us. If the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}translators didn't know the difference between "corn" and other forms of grain, how does one account for the sudden appearance of ["BARLEY," "WHEAT," ]{.class_s4N2-0}and ["RIE"]{.class_s4N2-0} in the passage? As a matter of fact, there is a grain grown in Egypt called "the CORN of Damascus" (Popcock, cited by Clarke, pg. 338).

The text is self-explanatory. The barley and the cotton are "in the ear" and "bolled," and the [Septuagint's]{.class_s4N4-0} confounding of the text by saying "in seed and was seeding" means nothing at all. Barley and cotton were harvested in March, and wheat and rye came a month later.

The [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901) substitutes ["spelt"]{.class_s4N4-0} for ["rie,"]{.class_s4N2-0} to make an American translation in "the language of the people that clarifies the [archaic words ]{.class_s4N4-0}of the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bible[,]{.class_s4N4-0} etc., etc." All of the commentators ape each other and say that ["rie" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(or rye) was unknown in ancient Egypt. But we have seen this movie before on the "late, late show"---[no corn ]{.class_s4N4-0}in Egypt, [no grapes]{.class_s4N4-0} in Egypt, [no badgers]{.class_s4N4-0} in Sinai, [no camels]{.class_s4N4-0} in Egypt, [no horses]{.class_s4N4-0} in Palestine, etc. After a man has been proved to be a blank fool ten times out of ten, his batting average is a thousand. If the majority of scholars say that "rye was unknown" to ancient Egypt, we can be certain of one thing: [it was a common crop in ancient Egypt.]{.class_s4N4-0}

["And the rain was not poured upon the earth\...the rain and the hail"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vss. 33--34). We are now told that not only was there a rain of hail, but there was a rain of [water. ]{.class_s4N4-0}If this weren't enough, it was accompanied by frost (Psa. 78:47) and lightning (Exod. 9:23). The seventh plague, then, is a most remarkable thing (see comments under vs. 14). Frost accompanies rain---in a tropical climate!---and lightning runs [horizontally ]{.class_s4N4-0}instead of vertically. ["And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened\...as the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[had spoken by Moses" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 35).

[]{#c177.xhtml}

# Chapter 10 {.heading_s4G-0}

10:1 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Go in unto Pharaoh: for I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants, that I might shew these my signs before him:

2 And that thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son, and of thy son's son, what things I have wrought in Egypt, and my signs which I have done among them; that ye may know how that I am the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}.

3 And Moses and Aaron came in unto Pharaoh, and said unto him, Thus saith the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}God of the Hebrews, How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me? let my people go, that they may serve me.

4 Else, if thou refuse to let my people go, behold, to morrow will I bring the locusts into thy coast:

5 And they shall cover the face of the earth, that one cannot be able to see the earth: and they shall eat the residue of that which is escaped, which remaineth unto you from the hail, and shall eat every tree which groweth for you out of the field:

6 And they shall fill thy houses, and the houses of all thy servants, and the houses of all the Egyptians; which neither thy fathers, nor thy fathers' fathers have seen, since the day that they were upon the earth unto this day. And he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh.

7 And Pharaoh's servants said unto him, How long shall this man be a snare unto us? let the men go, that they may serve the LORD their God: knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?

8 And Moses and Aaron were brought again unto Pharaoh: and he said unto them, Go, serve the LORD your God: but who are they that shall go?

9 And Moses said, We will go with our young and with our old, with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds will we go; for we must hold a feast unto the LORD.

10 And he said unto them, Let the LORD be so with you, as I will let you go, and your little ones: look to it; for evil is before you.

11 Not so: go now ye that are men, and serve the LORD; for that ye did desire. And they were driven out from Pharaoh's presence."

A week after the thunders, lightnings, rain, and hail have stopped, it is apparent that Pharaoh's promises are about as good as an American President's promises to cut taxes. The inmates of the concentration camps go right on about their duties, although, now, there is a spirit present which was wholly absent for nearly eighty years. Now, the guards work in pairs; each of them handles a German shepherd on a leash and carries a "grease-gun" with two extra bandoleers of ammunition. Orders are screamed in high-pitched nervous voices, and time and time again, there are sarcastic retorts coming from the ranks, and none of these voices can be located. "Making bricks" is out of the question, so the treasure cities stop where they began. The land is "shot" (in the koine vernacular), and Pharaoh is running par for the course: ["neither would he let the children of Israel go"]{.class_s4N2-0} (9:35).

["How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3). That is, []{#c177.xhtml_page_209}confession, acknowledgment, and promised restitution are not necessarily signs of [humility]{.class_s4N4-0}. "Humbling yourself" (James 4:6, 10) includes not only self-judgment, but [obeying God. ]{.class_s4N4-0}So far, Pharaoh has stopped short, every time, of doing what God wanted done. ["Let my people go, that they may serve me" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 3) is the commandment.

["Else, if thou refuse to let my people go, behold, to morrow will I bring the locusts into thy coast: And they shall cover the face of the earth, that one cannot be able to see the earth: and they shall eat of the residue of that which is escaped" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 4--5). No one who has seen photographs of locust or grasshopper invasions needs to make any reference to anyone's technical books on the matter. The Egyptian locust is larger than a grasshopper, and when it lights on a field, the swarms pile up to six or seven inches. Swarms of locusts can blot out sunlight, and it only takes them ten to thirty minutes to strip one hundred acres of land. Swarms have been so thick that they put out fires which had been kindled to drive them away.

All of the scholars, including Gaebelein, Pettingill, Moorehead, Pierson, Eerdman, Bullinger, and Scofield (who should have known better), make the mistake of running the cross-reference to Joel 2:1--10. All of these men ["wrongly]{.class_s4N4-0} divide" one sentence in the middle (see Joel 2:10--11). Since none of them could believe the Bible literally, they (with one accord) have called the "Lord's army" (Joel 2:11) the DEVlL'S army! Note the ludicrous notation at the top of Joel chapter 2 on page 931 of the [Scofield Reference Bible. ]{.class_s4N4-0}To attribute the work of the Holy Spirit to the Devil (Joel 2:1--10), the Scofield editors []{#c177.xhtml_page_210}have had to[ make one paragraph out of half a sentence. ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Note Joel 2:11, which forms a paragraph by itself; this was done by taking[ half of the sentence]{.class_s4N4-0} which began in verse 10 and dissecting it!)

The ["locusts"]{.class_s4N2-0} of Joel 1:4 are mentioned nowhere in Joel chapter 2, and [\"the northern army"]{.class_s4N2-0} of the invading [Antichrist]{.class_s4N4-0} (Joel 2:20) has no more connection with the ["Lord's]{.class_s4N4-0} army" (Joel 2:1--11) than the United Nations Assembly has to do with the New Testament Church. This traditional error has been passed down intact through eighteen centuries of Bible commenting; the reader will be shocked to find that not one premillennial expositor from Irenaeus (A.D. 130--202) to Dwight Pentecost believed the Lord's army [was ]{.class_s4N4-0}the Lord's army (Joel 2:10--11).

The drama is building to a climax. Not only were human beings included in the last destruction, but another "ingredient" has been added: the winged animals have been getting [larger ]{.class_s4N4-0}by the minute---lice, flies, locusts. In the Tribulation, these wings suddenly expand to more than a man's arm length (Rev. 9:1--10). The [RV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1885) preserves a gross error in verse 2 which the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1901) should have picked up (since they picked up the other 5,000 errors of that corrupt text), but here, the [ASV ]{.class_s4N4-0}refused to follow the apostate British leadership. The [RV]{.class_s4N4-0} has written ["How I have made sport"]{.class_s4N4-0} for ["what things I have wrought."]{.class_s4N2-0} (The plague of locusts is aimed at making a fool out of the god Serapis, who was supposed to protect the country from such a calamity.)

["How long shall this man be a snare unto us\...knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 7). Of course Pharaoh knows it, but Pharaoh (as [Adolf Hitler]{.class_s4N4-0}), near the end of his reign, is []{#c177.xhtml_page_211}not primarily concerned with whether or not his land survives. In the midst of soul-stirring messages (1944--1945), with the accent on the "final victory," "secret weapons," "throwing the invader out," and "pushing the enemy into the sea," Hitler was perfectly aware of the fact that he had lost 2,000,000 square miles of territory in less than two years. Anyone who has read [The Fatal Decisions ]{.class_s4N4-0}and[ Hitler Moves East ]{.class_s4N4-0}knows that Hitler was fully aware of the appalling debacle at Stalingrad; so much so, that he himself officially "wrote off" General Paulus' Sixth Army and sent the General a Field Marshal's baton with a pistol and [one round]{.class_s4N4-0}---ostensibly for suicide.

Pharaoh knows the condition of the troops and the terrain, but he has been snared into one of those situations (vs. 7) where to "quit" is to "lose face." One might say that he was in the position of Presidents Kennedy, Johnson, and Nixon (1963--1970). You have to hang on for the sake of the argument (Vietnam) long after the point in the matter has disappeared. If America HAD won the war in 1970, what would it have proved? Did you ever pause to think about things like that? America did not stop one Communist in [East Berlin]{.class_s4N4-0} or [Czechoslovakia;]{.class_s4N4-0} not one Communist at [Yale, Harvard, ]{.class_s4N4-0}or [Columbia University; ]{.class_s4N4-0}not one Communist controlling the [Health, Education, and Welfare departments;]{.class_s4N4-0} no Communist leader of the [NAACP;]{.class_s4N4-0} not one Communist on the [Supreme Court; ]{.class_s4N4-0}and not one Communist in [Cuba. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Pray tell me, what was the point in stopping Communism in [Vietnam?]{.class_s4N4-0} Was it cheaper?

Pharaoh wants to keep control simply to keep control. There is really no utility in staying on the throne. At this time, a "Pharaoh" would simply be a clown sitting []{#c177.xhtml_page_212}on a pile of dead bodies, burned bushes, withered grass, inflamed flesh, and stinking carcasses, while listening to complaining tenants. This is the perfect picture of [\"the god of this world"]{.class_s4N2-0} (2 Cor. 4:4), whose flunkies are "bringing in the Kingdom." (Every believer should obtain a more detailed picture of this "reign" by reading [The Sure Word of Prophecy]{.class_s4N4-0}.)

"You gonna let 'em go?"

["And he said unto them, Go, serve the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[L]{.class_s4NE-0}[ORD, your God: but who are they that shall go?" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 8). Or to put it in the New Testament setting: ["And WHO is my neighbour?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Luke 10:29), or "But WHICH commandments am I supposed to keep?" (Matt. 22:36).

["And Moses said, We will go with our young and with our old, with our sons and with our daughters" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 9). (See comments under 8:25.) Ephesians 6:4 and Genesis 7:1 are good comments on the text. If the sons and daughters had been left, Pharaoh would have held "security" as good as any kidnapper held while waiting for ransom money. What normal mother and father would not return to Egypt to get their children? Pharaoh wants a guarantee that Israel will return. When one considers the whole scene, it is too much. What would make Pharaoh think anyone would trust him with "sons and daughters" when his predecessors were in the habit of [drowning "sons" and integrating "daughters]{.class_s4N4-0}"?

["And he said unto them, Let the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[be so with you, as I will let you go, and your little ones: look to it; for evil is before you. Not so: go now ye that are men, and serve the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[; for that ye did desire" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 10--11). Bullinger, Williams, Ellicott, Lange, Clarke, the [Pulpit Commentary, ]{.class_s4N4-0}and the [Wycliffe []{#c177.xhtml_page_213}Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0} all copy each other in expounding the text. They all preserve for us the original boner which some relative of Origen thought up when he ran out of ideas. The general feeling of the commentators is that Pharaoh is [sarcastically ]{.class_s4N4-0}saying, "I will not let you go." However, any sixth-grade reader of the text can see that Pharaoh says three times, "go on and serve the Lord," (vss. 8, 10--11). For a man who has just confessed his sins and asked for prayer (9:27--28), Pharaoh has certainly gotten brutally self-righteous, if we accept this interpretation.

No, the meaning plainly is the one given in the Reformation text and preserved in the Masoretic text. "The Lord be with you, and I am going to let you go and your little ones. [But ]{.class_s4N4-0}you'd better look out before you think of taking the little ones, because you're going three days into the wilderness, and a lot can happen to the children. Beside that, your original petition was only for the [men, ]{.class_s4N4-0}so take them and leave the kiddies here." This is the meaning of the text as it stands in English (or Hebrew), and if 90% of the scholars missed it, 99% of them missed the reference to Joel chapter 2, so what is the point?

But perhaps we have been guilty of wording things too nicely (see remarks under 5:20) in an effort to straighten the critics out. What Pharaoh [meant ]{.class_s4N4-0}to say, in Twentieth-Century English, was:

"Now, I know you are all hung up on a religious kick, and that's all right, you know. We got some of our own people that get way out in left field once in awhile. But now listen, don't let a bunch of kooks ruin your children. Gotta think of the [little ones, ]{.class_s4N4-0}you know! Why, the little darlin's might get bit out there in the desert, and it's hot out there. [Man, it's hot!]{.class_s4N4-0} And think of what you're leaving! []{#c177.xhtml_page_214}Why, this is a UNIVERSITY town, and you wouldn't deprive your children of a [College education,]{.class_s4N4-0} would you? Of course not! I mean, it might be all right for you older people to [throw your lives away, ]{.class_s4N4-0}but the children don't understand, and you have to think of their future. Think of the kids! Why, think of their playmates. They've been raised with our people for four generations. Why, you're going to take them away from their buddies at school, and they won't have anywhere to play, and nothin' but [church services]{.class_s4N4-0} to go to. You know, that can get to be a drag, man!"

10:12 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts, that they may come up upon the land of Egypt, and eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail hath left.

13 And Moses stretched forth his rod over the land of Egypt, and the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}brought an east wind upon the land all that day, and all that night; and when it was morning, the east wind brought the locusts.

14 And the locusts went up over all the land of Egypt, and rested in all the coasts of Egypt: very grievous were they; before them there were no such locusts as they, neither after them shall be such.

15 For they covered the face of the whole earth, so that the land was darkened; and they did eat every herb of the land, and all the fruit of the trees which the hail had left: and there remained not any green thing in the trees, or in the herbs of the field, through all the land of Egypt."

Moses and Aaron go out of the palace. All of the guards salute them. Some Egyptian women going by cry out, "No, no! Don't do it, Lords! Not again!" Moses []{#c177.xhtml_page_215}and Aaron tramp down the steps like Brown Shirts on the stairs at Nuremberg. Their faces are like stone. The crowd at the foot of the palace steps takes one look at their visages, and they all read the handwriting on the wall. Panic seizes the multitude, and they scatter like birdshot, crying, screaming, praying, cursing, and calling for their families. The "servants of Pharaoh" (vs. 7) have relayed the message before Pharaoh can give his official version (vs. 10). There is a mad rush to the fields to build brush fires. But the protective measures are hopeless,

["For they covered the face of the whole earth, so that the land was darkened" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 15). The fisherman's jinx (the east wind) brings with it locusts who[ "rested in all the coasts of Egypt\...and they did eat every herb of the land, and all the fruit of the trees which the hail had left" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vss. 14--15). This means that, now, there is no more cotton for clothing; no more wheat, rye, or barley for bread; and no more figs, olives, pears, peaches, plums, or grapes. Inflation follows the plague like night follows day, for in addition to this loss of starch and vitamins, the [protein ]{.class_s4N4-0}vanishes. One must remember that the cattle have been smitten TWICE in the last several months (9:6, 21). Pork goes up by \$3.00 a pound, steak by \$4.00 a pound, a loaf of bread by \$1.50, and you can't get a pound of grapes under \$6.00. You see, the God who wrote the Bible and called the messengers to preach His message (2 Cor. 5:18--21) is the God of[ animals and nature]{.class_s4N4-0} (Matt. 24:21).

The earthquakes of Chile and Peru (1969--1970), and the famines of India and Africa (1950--1972), cannot be disconnected from the [Reformation Bible. ]{.class_s4N4-0}The One who wrote the originals and [preserved the truth]{.class_s4N4-0} (Psa. 12:7) stated that these things would come (Matt 24:7), as men []{#c177.xhtml_page_216}depart from the [words ]{.class_s4N4-0}of God (Matt. 24:11--12, 35). There is always a connection between high taxes and the Bible, inflation and the Gospel, famines and the word of God, catastrophe and the words of God, political oppression and the infallible truth of the eternal Godhead (see comments under 9:20--21).

Those working in the fields look up from their frantic preparations, and they see a cloud rising in the east. It spreads as it rises. But this is no thunderhead (although it "thunders"); it is a[ dark brown]{.class_s4N4-0} instead of white, pink, blue, purple, or gray. The cloud spreads till it stretches from horizon to horizon. The whole cloud is alive; it buzzes, it hums, it roars. Suddenly the top of the cloud seems to break apart, and the whole formation drops to the ground. Those still in the fields are smothered with a pile of winged creatures. Flailing with brooms, rakes, shovels, poles, fans, and pitch forks, the maddened populace tries to beat the swarms off the ground; they stick. There is a cracking and snapping and clicking and chirping as 5,000,000 locusts eat up Egypt---1,000 acres at a lick!

["And there remained not any green thing in the trees, or in the herbs of the field, through all the land of Egypt" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 15). When the swarm leaves, the [caterpillars ]{.class_s4N4-0}finish what is left, according to Psalm 78:46. Now Egypt looks like the Argonne Forest (1918) after an artillery counter-bombardment. Pharaoh's palace looks like Kent University (1970) in Ohio. A committee composed of twenty Mayors, ten sheriffs, 55 councilmen, three cabinet members, and forty highway patrolmen have gained access to the throne room; and they are all shouting and gesticulating at the same time.

"You gotta [do ]{.class_s4N4-0}something! You gotta listen to the demands of the union!"

"My God, Pharaoh, have you seen the bottom land out there?! Go out and [look at it!]{.class_s4N4-0} LOOK AT IT! See it for yourself!"

"I'm telling you the truth, your Majesty; the whole cotton-pickin\' land is a disaster area!"

"Let 'em go, King; let \'em go. We can't work \'em anymore anyway! We haven't got enough food to feed our own families; LET \'EM GO!"

"YOUR MAJESTY\...!!"

Pharaoh smiles pleasantly through all the howling and hollering, and finally, he waves his hand for silence. Then, as blandly as he knows how, he says: "All right, now we will have to do something about this. Definitely, we will. I'll call for Moses and Aaron, and we'll have a confrontation and a little dialogue about\...."

One grey-haired gentleman at the front of the ranks has enough nerve to interrupt his Highness. He steps forward with a paper---his face as dark as a thunderhead---and says firmly, "Your majesty, if you will pardon me, [sir,]{.class_s4N4-0} your [Highness]{.class_s4N4-0}, we are full up to HERE, and we can't weather out anymore dialogues and confrontations. [You gotta let them people go!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

The crowd behind him roars its approval, and again, the heads begin to nod while the arms and hands begin to wave. Pharaoh quiets them down again, but before he can take advantage of the quietness, the grey-haired representative from Louisiana (or [Mississippi]{.class_s4N4-0}---I forget which!) goes on with: "Your majesty, we have a petition here signed by 90,000 citizens. It is a redress of grievances, and to be perfectly plain about it, your Honor[]{#c177.xhtml_page_218}---your Highness---your Clemency---we, ah, we are going to [impeach you]{.class_s4N4-0} if you don't let them Jews go!"

There is a breathless moment of suspense, and as Pharaoh tries to conceal his rage and form the proper response, another representative (from Georgia or [South Carolina]{.class_s4N4-0}---I forgot which!) puts in with: "Now don't misunderstand us, Pharaoh. You've been a good King up till now, and we sure got a lot done since the Hyksos left; but man, when you put Abernathy and Angela and Earl on your cabinet, you done shot the works! Now, we gotta see some action!"

10:16 "Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron in haste; and he said, I have sinned against the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}your God, and against you.

17 Now therefore forgive, I pray thee, my sin only this once, and intreat the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}your God, that he may take away from me this death only.

18 And he went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}.

19 And the LORD turned a mighty strong west wind, which took away the locusts, and cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust in all the coasts of Egypt.

20 But the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go."

Pharaoh cuts the deck and deals. ["I have sinned against the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[your God"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 16). This is an excellent confession and clearly indicates a deep conviction wrought by the Holy Spirit (cf. Joseph---Gen. 39:9, and the Prodigal---Luke 15:18). Furthermore, this time Pharaoh asks for personal forgiveness---["Now therefore forgive, I pray thee, my sin\..."]{.class_s4N2-0}---as well []{#c177.xhtml_page_219}as prayer to God for him from someone else. On the surface of the text, it would appear that, at last, Pharaoh has repented, confessed, believed, and---if he were a Campbellite---is about ready to be baptized ([which he was]{.class_s4N4-0}---15:4, 19)[! ]{.class_s4N4-0}Pharaoh is one man who followed Alexander Campbell clear to the pool, accompanied by[ all the water-dog requirements;]{.class_s4N4-0} unfortunately, the New Testament consigns Pharaoh to Hell (Rom. 9:17, 22) [with ]{.class_s4N4-0}his belief, confession, repentance, and "buptism" (that's how they say it).

However, the careful student of the Scripture will observe the word ["only" ]{.class_s4N2-0}appearing twice in verse 17. (There is no use to consult any commentary on the word. No major commentator paid any attention to it, for none of them took Luke 4:4 to heart. "Commentaries," as such, are usually criticisms of the Bible.) ["Only" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 17) betrays a condition of heart that does not match the profession of the mouth. We need forgiveness more than "once," and if our desire in getting saved is to be saved from ["this death only,"]{.class_s4N2-0} then we have a "foxhole religion" composed of salvation by works (see Gen. 4:1--6 in the Commentary on Genesis).

Anyone desires to escape physical death, but the death from which we are saved is called ["the second death"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Rev. 20:14). This salvation is a [spiritual salvation]{.class_s4N4-0} (Eph. 2:1--8) which saves a man's SOUL (1 Pet. 1:9) before it saves his BODY (1 Cor. 15:49--55). When Pharaoh says ["this death" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 17), he is only seeking relief from the destruction of his [land ]{.class_s4N4-0}and the [crops ]{.class_s4N4-0}which he intended to eat in order to stay alive (Eccl. 8:11).

["And the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord ]{.class_s4N9-0}[turned a mighty strong west wind\...there remained not one locust in all the []{#c177.xhtml_page_220}coasts of Egypt"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 19). The reader should notice how the word ["coasts"]{.class_s4N2-0} is used for any border (Josh. 15:12). For the finicky commentators who are spoiled with a sense of their own knowledge about the proper use of words, we may note that:

1\. The word[ coast]{.class_s4N4-0} means "to slide down a hill."

2\. The word [coast]{.class_s4N4-0} means "to put a car out of gear."

3\. The word [coast]{.class_s4N4-0} means "a shoreline."

4\. The word [coaster]{.class_s4N4-0} means "a vehicle for rolling up and down tracks."

5\. The word [coaster]{.class_s4N4-0} means "a piece of metal, wood, or cloth you put under a drinking glass."

If a modern American can use the word five different ways, what in the world is wrong with God using it TWO WAYS---"border" and "sea coast"? (You see, much of what we call "scholarship" or "making the Bible [easier to read"]{.class_s4N4-0} is nothing but the destructive censorship of a hypercritic. [If you give God the liberties you give yourself, you won't have any trouble at all with an Authorized Version \[1611\].]{.class_s4N4-0})

Moses and Aaron pray for Pharaoh (vs. 18), and this time, when they drop their hands and open their eyes, they find themselves in the middle of a mob of 40,000 Egyptians who are kneeling. Half of them are crossing themselves (OH, YES! Remember Osiris and the "[crux anasta]{.class_s4N4-0}"?) and twiddling their beads (OH, YES! All the priests of Isis and Horus had beads!). One must never forget that Rome is a Latin church, and its roots were in [Alexandria ]{.class_s4N4-0}and [Carthage]{.class_s4N4-0}, not [Jerusalem ]{.class_s4N4-0}or [Antioch ]{.class_s4N4-0}(see [Mark of the Beast]{.class_s4N4-0}).

Pharaoh decides to take his chances with ["the second death"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Rev. 20:14), and a "second death" is not long in coming (Exod. 12:13). Moses and Aaron have []{#c177.xhtml_page_221}not been gone from Tanis fifteen minutes when Pharaoh dismisses his cabinet and secret police and goes back into his bedchamber. On the way, he stops by the nursery and gazes, for a moment, into the face of his ten-year-old son, who has slept through the maelstrom without sneezing. He stands by the bed for about thirty minutes, chin in hand, eyebrows knit together, and his jawbones trembling from rage and frustration. If a Sony or a Wollensak could have been hooked up to Pharaoh's mind at this time, it would have played back something like this:

"They ain't gonna impeach nobody! I'll put you on the throne or die trying, and if they think they can get rid of our family that easy, they got another think coming! It's just a matter of time. If they got contact, their God can't keep up the pace forever, or they'll all starve to death with us. They're just as tore up as we are! By Thoth, I'll sweat 'em out yet! One more plague like that and [we'll bury them!]{.class_s4N4-0} They haven't been eating as good as we have, anyway, in the last seven years. There's gonna be an end to this, son; yessir, the day is gonna dawn, boy! The light is gonna break out! And when it shines, it's going to rise on your throne!"

No sooner has Pharaoh faced his problem positively and exercised "the power of positive thinking" than [out go the lights!]{.class_s4N4-0}

10:21 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, even darkness which may be felt.

22 And Moses stretched forth his hand toward heaven; and there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt three days:

23 They saw not one another, neither rose any from his place for three days: but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings.

24 And Pharaoh called unto Moses, and said, Go ye, serve the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}; only let your flocks and your herds be stayed: let your little ones also go with you.

25 And Moses said, Thou must give us also sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may sacrifice unto the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}our God.

26 Our cattle also shall go with us; there shall not an hoof be left behind; for thereof must we take to serve the LORD our God; and we know not with what we must serve the [Lord]{.class_s4N8-0}, until we come thither.

27 But the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let them go.

28 And Pharaoh said unto him, Get thee from me, take heed to thyself, see my face no more; for in that day thou seest my face thou shalt die.

29 And Moses said, Thou hast spoken well, I will see thy face again no more."

There is a blackout, and while the electricians are trying to locate the power failure, the Egyptians (on the border of Goshen) notice that the Israelites are still watching TV and listening to their Hi-Fis. But no one can locate the power failure. Moses and Aaron have been monkeying with electronics again, and when ["Moses stretched forth his hand"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 22), everything goes out in Egypt from the kerosene lamps to the searchlights. The flashlights don't work; the Coleman lanterns don't []{#c177.xhtml_page_223}work; the candles won't burn; the lights on the runway at the airport won't work; the headlights on all the cars have gone out; and all the food in the iceboxes and deep freezers rots away like dead fish on the beach. For three days, ["there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 22).

The worst part of the darkness was not the electric failure; the worst part was [the sun-and-moon failure!]{.class_s4N4-0} At 6 a.m., when Pharaoh and his daughter went down to bathe in the Nile, they couldn't see their hands in front of their faces; after waiting two hours for the daylight to "dawn," they [felt ]{.class_s4N4-0}their way back to the palace and sat down in a darkness as black as the inside of a coal cellar on a starless night. (The judgment is again a foretaste of outer ["darkness"]{.class_s4N2-0} \[2 Pet. 2:17\], and it is one of the judgments which is destined to fall on the earth during the Great Tribulation \[Rev. 16:10\].)

Pickpockets and "second-story men" have a field day in Tanis, Hanes, Taphenes, Raamses, and Pithom---the ["treasure cities" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(1:11). When the mess is over, there isn't a jewelry store or a grocery store in Egypt that hasn't lost half of its stock. One culprit explains to the "judge" as follows:

"You see, yo' honor, Ah wuz standin' by this plate glass window when de blackout come, and sudden like, I hears dis glass bustin'. The fust thing ah knows, [someone is stuffin' watches and rings in mah pockets!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

Pharaoh's position, by now, is ridiculous. The supreme god (["Ra"]{.class_s4N4-0}) has been put to shame. Now the golden disk (on the head of Isis and on the altar of the Roman Catholic churches and in the square at St. Peters, and symbolized by the circular collar and the circular tonsure, etc.) means nothing. Whoever Moses' God is, []{#c177.xhtml_page_224}He can control water, land, insects, animals, nature, the elements, and the SOLAR SYSTEM. To say that Pharaoh is fighting a losing battle is like saying that a roe mullet is having a rough time with a school of [sharks]{.class_s4N4-0}. What can you do with an adversary who can ruin your drinking water, kill your fish and cattle, destroy your crops, smite you with diseases, and then plunge you into a blackness so dark that you can [feel ]{.class_s4N4-0}it (vs. 21)?

(Dummelow adopts the Liberal view that the darkness was just a dust cloud that blew up \[Comment., pg. 57\]. This was the ancient view of Celsus \[A.D. 105--180\] and Porphyry \[A.D. 232--303\], but Dummelow nicely explains his infidelity by saying that since all the other plagues were due to "natural causes," this one would be also. Exactly how "natural" it is to [make lice out of dust]{.class_s4N4-0} has not yet been discovered. And one must admit that it is not often that ashes tossed into the air produce[ ]{.class_s4N2-0}boils and blains \[9:8--9\]!)

["But all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 23). The spiritual lesson is obvious. Israel had candlesticks burning in the Tabernacle to illustrate the presence of the Holy Spirit. The Christian has "light" in his dwelling (Eph. 5:8; 1 Thess. 5:5), for his earthen vessel is the temple of God (1 Cor. 6:19). The unsaved man is filled with darkness (1 Thess. 5:4--6), and ["how great is that darkness"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Matt. 6:23)! Haralan Popov and Richard Wurmbrand "rotting" in their dungeons had a light that Darwin, Huxley, Paley, Dewey, Russell, Shaw, Einstein, Wells, and Marx never saw, though these men professed to be "light-bearers" themselves. A saved coal miner, suffocating 500 feet underground in a collapsed mine shaft, has ["the light of the world"]{.class_s4N2-0} (John 8:12) in his "dwelling," and Pope John []{#c177.xhtml_page_225}XXIII and Pope Paul VI could not even [talk about THAT light]{.class_s4N4-0} when they addressed the "church" and the world before 5,000 watts of TV floodlights.

["YOU GONNA LET 'EM GO?" ]{.class_s4N4-0}In comes Moses and Aaron again, and they haven't crossed the threshold before the old record begins to play in the same groove at 78 rpm.

["Go ye, serve the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[; only let your flocks and your herds be stayed: let your little ones also go with you"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 24). The royal pride is hurting badly. It is apparent from the permission granted that Pharaoh is now grasping for strings instead of diamond necklaces. Actually, there is no point, now, in even keeping the cattle, unless it is to feed a starving populace. Furthermore, it is pretty clear that the children of Israel have a God who doesn't need cattle (Psa. 50:9--12), or anything else.

Moses is adamant and as immovable as the Rock of Gibralter. ["Our cattle also shall go with us; there shall not an hoof be left behind" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 26). One can tell at a reading that Moses has had a revival of "gumption" (Nineteenth-Century American). He could have stopped at ["with us,"]{.class_s4N2-0} but seeing the highest man in Egypt in the wretched condition he is in, and feeling the power of ["the rod of God" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(4:20) in his own hand (and having survived nine plagues that would turn your hair white), Moses now has the grit (spunk, guts, pluck, etc.) to beard the lion in his own den; so he puts salt in the wound and rubs---["There shall not an hoof be left behind"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 26). Moses' response to the fourth compromise (see comments under 8:25) matches Paul's warlike approach in Galatians 2:5, which see. (The same approach will be found in Eph. 4:27 and 6:10--14.)

["We know not with what we must serve the ]{.class_s4N2-0}[Lord]{.class_s4N9-0}[, until we come thither"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 26). (No instructions have yet been given ["concerning burnt offerings or sacrifice" ]{.class_s4N2-0}\[Jer. 7:22\]. This problem, which plagued Graf, Wellhausen, Hupfeld, DeWette, Astruc, and Kuenen, is so clearly explained in the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bible that one has a perfect right to ask: "Why didn't Graf, Wellhausen, et al., take remedial reading courses before attempting to comment on the Scriptures"?) As Moses is ignorant of the true worshipping ["in spirit and in truth" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(John 4:24), so the sinner cannot please God until[ the blood has been applied ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Rom. 8:1--6 cf. Exod. 12:13); and even then, he must forsake the world (Heb. 13:13) [to prove]{.class_s4N4-0} that ["good, and acceptable, and perfect, will of God"]{.class_s4N2-0}[ ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Rom. 12:1--2).

This time the transmission falls out (see comments under 11:1, 8). Pharaoh sees Moses' attitude perfectly. Moses is no longer a scared ambassador trying to make a peaceful settlement (see comments under Exod. 4--6); he has become an intolerable nuisance. Moses was only a foreigner when he first appeared, but he has passed through the stages of a rabble rouser to a leader. Now, he is a conceited, arrogant religious dictator.

Pharaoh suddenly abandons all hope of reaching an understanding, and for the first time since Exodus 5:2, he unmasks the "inner man." The real "Pharaoh of the Exodus" steps forth into the center of the Biblical stage where the floodlights of the centuries can reveal him.

["Listen, you two-bit punk! ]{.class_s4N4-0}I've put up with your crap as long as I can stand it, and I've got a belly full! Now I'll tell [you ]{.class_s4N4-0}what you do, grease--ball! You double-clutch it out of here in fourth gear and take that sidekick with you. []{#c177.xhtml_page_227}And so help me Thoth, if you ever set foot in this town again, I'll hang you so high the buzzards won't be able to breakfast off ya\![ HIT THE ROAD!!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

Aaron and Moses are nearly withered by the blast, and for a moment, Moses thinks God has left him again. The two turn "about-face" and start out of the throne room. Pharoah lets off one more blast of buckshot in the back before they get out: "And if I ever see ya again---either of ya---you better have your will made out[ 'cause I'm notifying the next of kin!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

The old hot blood (2:12) rushes up into Moses' ears, and he wheels on one foot before leaving the throne room. He faces a silent courtroom packed to the rafters with attendants, chambermaids, cabinet members, secret police, bodyguards, stewards, and ministers of State. Moses says heatedly: "All right, if that's the way you want it, it's Jake with me. We won't be back unless you send for us, and I'll bet you every iron-man in the jackpot that you'll send for us [sooner than you think!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

["You're faded!"]{.class_s4N4-0} roars Pharaoh, ["Go on and shoot!"]{.class_s4N4-0}

But Pharaoh will never survive the bombardment that is on the way. Without knowing it (and all of the commentators overlooked it), Pharaoh has at last attacked the greatest of God's attributes---His holiness. In verse 28, Pharaoh has claimed that divine attribute of Jehovah for himself (see comments under 33:20).

[]{#c1A8.xhtml}

# Chapter 11 {.heading_s4G-1}

11:1 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}said unto Moses, Yet will I bring one plague more upon Pharaoh, and upon Egypt; afterwards he will let you go hence: when he shall let you go, he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether.

2 Speak now in the ears of the people, and let every man borrow of his neighbour, and every woman of her neighbour, jewels of silver, and jewels of gold.

3 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians. Moreover the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the sight of Pharaoh's servants, and in the sight of the people."

Now the commission of 4:22--23 must be fulfilled. Pharaoh is "spent out"; he's "played all his cards" (early American runic discovered by Dr. Smellfungus in excavations at Two Egg, FL); there's no way for him to recover. His situation was perfectly reproduced in an underground bunker underneath the Reich Chancellery, in the Spring of 1945. There, a lonely, demented man spent his last few days with some trusted friends; and as the cannonade and the bombing drew closer and closer, there was no surrender, no turning back, and [no compromise. ]{.class_s4N4-0}Pride sustained the chancellor of the Third Reich to the bitter end. Buried under a ruined city (and a ruined continent), Adolf played all the cards out on the table till 52 were lying faceup.

["Yet will I bring one plague more upon Pharaoh\...he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 1). (See comments under 6:1.) The modern counterpart is "good riddance" (vss. 2--3). When the Jews finally leave, they are not merely "permitted" to []{#c1A8.xhtml_page_229}go, they are kicked out bag-and-baggage and run out of Goshen in less than eight hours, with the people urging them to go quicker (12:33). ["He shall surely thrust you out hence" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 1), and so it comes to pass in 12:31.

["Speak now in the ears of the people" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 2). We have already commented on the expression ["borrow"]{.class_s4N2-0} found in the passage (see 3:22). The "jewels of silver and gold" explain the abundance of these materials which later go into the construction of the Tabernacle. "Silver and gold" the Egyptians have (Egypt being a type of the world), but they cannot do what Peter and John did without either (Acts 3:1--6). The disciples were told to go and ["freely give"]{.class_s4N2-0} and ["provide neither gold, nor silver"]{.class_s4N2-0} in their ["purses"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Matt. 10:8--9). These are the first two items mentioned in the revenue of Babylon the Great (Rev. 18:12), and the clear statement of Simon Peter is that neither will redeem you from your sins (1 Pet. 1:18).

["Moreover the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 3). You bet he was. The last time he came into the palace, there was a mob kneeling and crossing themselves ([crux anasta]{.class_s4N4-0}, the female sex symbol of life, was carried around the neck of all the big shots in Egypt), twiddling their beads (all Egyptian priests used "rosaries" 2,000 years before Christ was born), and praying to blessed Isis, blessed Osiris, and blessed Ptah the Baptist. The only difference between Moses coming into Tanis and a Pope coming into Newark, New Jersey, would be that Moses could produce and demonstrate his calling off the cuff, at a moment's notice. He had the apostolic "signs, miracles, and wonders" (2 Cor. 12:12), and there was never any doubt about it. On the other hand, the poor Italian winoes could never produce lice, []{#c1A8.xhtml_page_230}frogs, hail, or locusts, and could no more cut off sunlight for three days than they could produce Bible believers or Bible revivals. Moses, by the power of God, had earned his reverence; the Popes got theirs by fable, proxy, and forged documents.

The ["one plague more"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 1) of the commission shows that the death angel [Apollyon ]{.class_s4N4-0}(see Rev. 9:11 and comments under Exod. 12:23) uses a fatal sickness as his weapon (see 1 Sam. 5:11). To tell the truth about it, the Philistines of 1 Samuel actually refer to PHARAOH when they discuss the plague of 1 Samuel 6:4. Observe: ["Wherefore then do ye harden your hearts, as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened their hearts?"]{.class_s4N2-0} (1 Sam. 6:6).

11:4 "And Moses said, Thus saith the [Lord]{.class_s4N9-1}, About midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt:

5 And all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sitteth upon his throne, even unto the firstborn of the maidservant that is behind the mill; and all the firstborn of beasts.

6 And there shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more.

7 But against any of the children of Israel shall not a dog move his tongue, against man or beast: that ye may know how that the [Lord ]{.class_s4N9-1}doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel.

8 And all these thy servants shall come down unto me, and bow down themselves unto me, saying, Get thee out, and all the people that follow []{#c1A8.xhtml_page_231}thee: and after that I will go out. And he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger.

9 And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N9-1}said unto Moses, Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you; that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt.

10 And Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh: and the [Lord ]{.class_s4N9-1}hardened Pharaoh's heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land."

From verse 8, it would appear that the words of verses 4--8 were spoken at the time of 10:27--29. If these words were spoken, one can easily see why Pharaoh "lost his cool" (hippy cliché). Observe:

1\. [Your ]{.class_s4N4-0}heir-apparent is no better than a [​]{.class_s4NT} slave's baby (vs. 5).

2\. The Hebrews are better than the Egyptians (vs. 7).

3\. God thinks this and will confirm it with[ racial discrimination]{.class_s4N4-0} (vs. 7).

4\. Your people will stop serving YOU and will serve ME (vs. 8).

5\. I'll go when I get good and ready (vs. 8).

This is the drift of the passage (as the commentators say), and where none of the commentators can explain or expound the passage, the "King's English" does much to restore "the original." No wonder Pharaoh was "flushed" into the open before Moses stomped out of the palace.

The account shows that Moses is "somebody" in the land of Egypt (vs. 3). When he says "jump!" they say, ["Which way?"]{.class_s4N4-0} and ["How far?" ]{.class_s4N4-0}There is not much doubt in the eyes of the "silent majority" about who the real King of Egypt is: it's MOSES (cf. Matt. 28:18). The bow (reserved for royalty---vs. 8) is performed at the feet of []{#c1A8.xhtml_page_232}Moses on the Passover night, showing that Pharaoh had not only lost his land, animals, gods, magicians, crops, and temper, he had lost his influence and his [subjects.]{.class_s4N4-0}

["About midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 4). The killer is plainly [Abaddon,]{.class_s4N4-0} the death angel (Exod. 12:23; Heb. 2:14; Rev. 9:11), although the Lord Himself takes the responsibility (12:12). Again, all the commentators miss the import of the statements; they are so busy trying to correct the text of the Holy Bible that they fail to comment.

The change of the tense (vs. 1) from ["said"]{.class_s4N2-0} to "had said" ([Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0}) does nothing for the passage but make the usual mess of pottage. If the tense is followed, the translator should have altered ["said"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 4) to "Moses HAD said." These things are quite typical of the "deeper" truths (or the "nuggets") which commentators reproduce to stuff the covers of a book. Going blind as a bat over the great spiritual truths in this passage (see vs. 4 and comments), and absolutely refusing to comment in a practical and intelligible fashion, all that the commentators can find (many times) is the wrong tense in the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bible[. ]{.class_s4N4-0}(Can't an atheist do the same thing?)

Not content with one bungle, the [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0} bungles again and insists that verse 2 is wrong and "the Hebrew will not bear this rendering." That is, confronted with the beginning of the final scene where the whole drama of Exodus comes to a climax, the Conservative commentators unmask themselves (exactly as Pharaoh!) and reveal their true nature: [they are destructive critics who never]{.class_s4N4-0} [could tolerate the words of the AV (1611), and they have only written commentaries for the purpose of venting their wrath on the text of the Holy Bible. ]{.class_s4N4-0}(The []{#c1A8.xhtml_page_233}Masoretic text reads as the [King James ]{.class_s4N4-0}Bible in both vss. 1 and 2.)

We have commented before on the error of Dummelow, Ellicott, Lange, Jamieson, Fausset, Brown, Williams, Bullinger, Keil, Delitzsch, the [Interpreter's Bible]{.class_s4N4-0}, the [New Bible Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0}, and the [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_s4N4-0} in translating ["borrow"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 2) as "ask."

Now it is Clarke's turn to give us some of the "unsearchable riches" of the "grammatico-historico" method of private interpretation, as he tries out for "Rookie of the Year." He writes a whole page of small print to the effect that the Bible has [omitted seven passages]{.class_s4N4-0} and refused to put [three verses ]{.class_s4N4-0}in their proper place. Clarke recommends the Samaritan text here over the Masoretic Hebrew.

He assures us that "competent critics" have the situation well in hand. However, when one surveys nineteen centuries of church history and examines the fruit of these "competent critics"---who pop up in every century---one is compelled to say with Job (in the "original"!): "You're a bunch of QUACKS (cf. Job 13:14)!" The trouble with Clarke and the other 45 competent "quacks" is the same trouble that Origen and Philo had: what they couldn't figure out [logically ]{.class_s4N4-0}must not exist.

Clarke confesses that his problem is that verse 8 has no ["logical]{.class_s4N4-0} connection" with verse 7; on this basis, we are to believe the theories of Dr. Kennicott---that the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}messed up five chapters (chaps. 7, 8, 9, 10, and 11) by omitting seven verses and then misplaced Exodus 10:28--29. (Question: what is the difference between this way of approaching the word of God and that used by Tom Paine and Voltaire?) Clarke and Kennicott are a little late in "helping God out." He could have used a lot more help in []{#c1A8.xhtml_page_234}the chronology of 4:18--6:30, if anyone really thought He needed "help." At least Clarke is honest enough to admit that [Homer ]{.class_s4N4-0}(850 B.C.) is the source of Dr. Kennicott's destructive theories. (The Greeks may have "had a word for it," but the correct word is [FAKER]{.class_s4N4-0}.)

Since mill work is slave work (Judg. 16:21; Isa. 47:1--2), Moses has insulted Pharaoh just about as blatantly as he could do it without getting decapitated on the spot. The gist of verses 5 and 7 is: "I'm going to segregate Jews from Hamites, and then, I'm going to integrate YOU with a Nubian slave girl who stands behind the mill wheels while they grind."

Detailed descriptions of the millstones, grinding processes, etc., can be found in any commentary. None of them are as important as the issue of SEGREGATION vs. INTEGRATION, clearly mentioned throughout the passage. When it comes to these subjects, most of the Conservative commentators suddenly contract lockjaw.

The Lord sums up the entire contest in verse 10.

A. Moses and Aaron did the wonders.

B. The LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart.

C. Pharoah would NOT let Israel go out of the land.

[]{#c1BJ.xhtml}

# Chapter 12 {.heading_s4G-0}

12:1 "And the [Lord ]{.class_s4N8-0}spake unto Moses and Aaron in the land of Egypt, saying,

2 This month shall be unto you the beginning of months: it shall be the first month of the year to you.

3 Speak ye unto all the congregation of Israel, saying, In the tenth day of this month they shall take to them every man a lamb, according to the house of their fathers, a lamb for an house:

4 And if the household be too little for the lamb, let him and his neighbour next unto his house take it according to the number of the souls; every man according to his eating shall make your count for the lamb.

5 Your lamb shall be without blemish, a male of the first year: ye shall take it out from the sheep, or from the goats:

6 And ye shall keep it up until the fourteenth day of the same month: and the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall kill it in the evening.

7 And they shall take of the blood, and strike it on the two side posts and on the upper door post of the houses, wherein they shall eat it.

8 And they shall eat the flesh in that night, roast with fire, and unleavened bread; and with bitter herbs they shall eat it.

9 Eat not of it raw, nor sodden at all with water, but roast with fire; his head with his legs, and with the purtenance thereof.

10 And ye shall let nothing of it remain until the morning; and that which remaineth of it until the morning ye shall burn with fire."

Now there follows one of the greatest pictures of the Crucifixion found anywhere in the Old Testament. Whoever wrote the chapter---and preserved it for the [AV ]{.class_s4N4-0}(1611)---had to know at least twelve details of the Crucifixion of Christ more than 1,400 years before the Lord Jesus was born. We shall note these as they occur in the text, instead of listing them together here.

["This month shall be unto you the beginning of months" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 2). The month is ["Abib"]{.class_s4N2-0} (13:4), which would roughly correspond to March-April on our calendar. This month is called ["Nisan"]{.class_s4N2-0} in Nehemiah 2:1, after the Babylonian captivity. (The word[ Abib ]{.class_s4N4-0}means "green-ear month" \[see comments under Esth. 3:7 in the [Bible Believer\'s Commentary on Ezra, Nehemiah, Esther:]{.class_s4N4-0} hereafter referred to as the [Commentary on Ezra, Nehemiah, Esther]{.class_s4N4-0}\]). In the back of Clarke's commentary will be found five tables which will indicate the day of the week and the calendar week (in English) on which the Passover will fall in any given year. It would appear that, up until here, Israel has used September-October as the first month ([Tishri ]{.class_s4N4-0}or [Ethanim]{.class_s4N4-0}: Exod. 23:16 cf. 1 Kings 8:2).

This season was traditionally held to be the date of the Creation (Sept. 20--23). Babylonians and Assyrians had never used this date, but always began their year with April ([Nisannu]{.class_s4N4-0}), while the Egyptians began their calendar year with July (called [Thoth]{.class_s4N4-0}). Thus, Israel inherits two calendars---a civil calendar beginning at Yom Kippur in the seventh month, and a religious calendar beginning at the Passover on the first month.

["In the tenth day of this month they shall take to them every man a lamb" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 3). These instructions regarding the tenth day and the fourteenth day are []{#c1BJ.xhtml_page_237}remarkable, for [not once does Israel ever again use this exact order]{.class_s4N4-0} in observing the Sabbath or the Passover. The careful student of the Scripture should notice that it is the eleventh day of Nisan ([Saturday ]{.class_s4N4-0}evening to Sunday evening) that Christ enters Jerusalem on ["a colt the foal of an ass"]{.class_s4N2-0} (Matt 21:5); the twelfth day of Nisan ([Sunday ]{.class_s4N4-0}sunset to Monday sunset), He is teaching; the thirteenth day of Nisan ([Monday ]{.class_s4N4-0}sunset to Tuesday sunset), He is teaching; and the fourteenth day of Nisan ([Tuesday ]{.class_s4N4-0}sunset to Wednesday sunset), He is crucified. Thus, the "Lamb" is "taken" on the [tenth day of Nisan ]{.class_s4N4-0}([Friday ]{.class_s4N4-0}sunset to Saturday sunset) and is "kept up" until the fourteenth day, for one must never forget that [the Lord Jesus was anointed for his death and burial the day BEFORE His Triumphal Entry]{.class_s4N4-0} (John 12:1--8)[. ]{.class_s4N4-0}According to the calculations of Ainsworth, Christ is slain within one hour of the regular time for killing the Passover Lamb, and many converted Rabbis identify the cry of John 19:30 with the descent of the priest's knife into the neck of the Paschal lamb.

["Every man a lamb"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 3). This Lamb is foreshadowed in Adam's clothing (Gen. 3:21), in Abel's sacrifice (Gen. 4:4), and in Abraham's remarkable experience on Mount Moriah (see Gen. 22:1--3 in the [Commentary on Genesis]{.class_s4N4-0}). This Lamb is so important that God doesn't count the national life of Israel as having any "life" at all until the blood of the Lamb is shed. ["This month shall be unto you the beginning of months\..."]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 2). This Lamb is [personalized ]{.class_s4N4-0}in Isaiah (Isa. 53:3--7), [identified ]{.class_s4N4-0}by John the Baptist (John 1:29), and [glorified ]{.class_s4N4-0}by the Father (Rev. 5:13). This Lamb is born in a manger where human shepherds can stare at Him []{#c1BJ.xhtml_page_238}(Luke 2:15--20), yet when the elders and cherubim gaze on Him in His glory, they fall on their faces (Rev. 5:8, 14).

["If the household be too little for the lamb" ]{.class_s4N2-0}(vs. 4). If the household is only one or two people, then they invite the neighbors in to share the lamb. Another reading would be that if the house is too small to afford a lamb (Lev. 12:8), they can go next door and share in the one killed there. Anyway you read it, you share the Lamb with your neighbor.

["Your lamb shall be without blemish"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 5). No student of the Scripture could fail to see 1 Peter 1:19 and Ephesians 5:27 sticking out all over the verse. [Pilate ]{.class_s4N4-0}said the Lamb was without blemish (John 19:6); [Judas ]{.class_s4N4-0}said the Lamb was without blemish (Matt. 27:4); Pilate's [wife ]{.class_s4N4-0}said the Lamb was without blemish (Matt. 27:19); and the [centurion ]{.class_s4N4-0}who had charge of the execution said the Lamb was without blemish (Luke 23:47). His worst enemies could not prove a fault in Him (John 8:46), and the verdict of nineteen centuries is that He was head and shoulders above every "lamb of the flock" in any flock that has appeared (on any continent) since the foundation of the world (Isa. 53:3--7).

On the chain gangs in Carolina, the spike drivers would sing, "Oh Ananias! Oh Ananias! What kinda man izzis man called 'Jesus'?" And the gang would answer back (as they slap the spikes into the railroad bed): "Well, He's alright---Ugh! Well, He's alright---UGH! Well, He's alright---UMPH!" And YOU know, He is "alright," and you know that YOU are [not,]{.class_s4N4-0} and that is all any sinner needs to know to be saved (Luke 23:41). When you go beyond those two simple facts of history and theology [without receiving HIM,]{.class_s4N4-0} you have gone too far.

["And the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall kill it in the evening"]{.class_s4N2-0} (vs. 6).
